blocks:
Ayahs

الٓر ۚ تِلْكَ ءَايَـٰتُ ٱلْكِتَـٰبِ ٱلْمُبِينِ (١)
Aif, Lam, Ra. These are the signs of the Manifest Book. (1)
إِنَّآ أَنزَلْنَـٰهُ قُرْءَٰنًا عَرَبِيًّا لَّعَلَّكُمْ تَعْقِلُونَ (٢)
Indeed We have sent it down as an Arabic Quran so that you may exercise your reason. (2)
نَحْنُ نَقُصُّ عَلَيْكَ أَحْسَنَ ٱلْقَصَصِ بِمَآ أَوْحَيْنَآ إِلَيْكَ هَـٰذَا ٱلْقُرْءَانَ وَإِن كُنتَ مِن قَبْلِهِۦ لَمِنَ ٱلْغَـٰفِلِينَ (٣)
We will recount to you the best of narratives in what We have revealed to you of this Quran, and indeed prior to it you were among those who are unaware [of it]. (3)

(Commentary)

The purpose of the Surah is to explain the guardianship (الولاية) — the divine authority and protective care Allah exercises over His servants — of Allah over His servant who has purified his faith (الإيمان) — the sincere conviction and submission to truth — for Him, the Exalted, with absolute sincerity, and whose heart is filled with His love, seeking no substitute for Him and turning toward nothing other than Him. It demonstrates that Allah, the Exalted, takes charge of his affairs, nurturing him with the finest upbringing, leading him to the stations of proximity (القرب), and quenching his thirst from the spring of nearness (الزلفى). Thus, He reserves him for Himself and grants him a divine life, even if outward causes (الأسباب الظاهرة) seem to conspire for his destruction. He elevates him even if events strive for his debasement, and He grants him might (العزة) even if calamities and the misfortunes of time beckon toward his humiliation and the lowering of his status.

The Exalted has clarified this by recounting the story of Yusuf the Truthful (يوسف الصديق) عليه‌السلام. No other story in the chapters of the Noble Quran has been detailed with such comprehensiveness from beginning to end as his story عليه‌السلام, and this Surah has been uniquely dedicated to it without any other story sharing its space.

He عليه‌السلام was a servant sincere in his servitude (العبودية) — the state of total submission and devotion to Allah — so Allah reserved him for Himself and empowered him with His might. Although various causes gathered to humiliate and debase him, every time they cast him into a place of peril, Allah, the Exalted, brought him to life through the very same path that was meant to lead him to destruction. His brothers envied him and cast him into the depths of the pit, then sold him for a miserable price—a few numbered dirhams—yet that very path led him to Egypt and entered him into the house of sovereignty and honor. The woman in whose house he resided sought to seduce him and then accused him before the Governor (العزيز), yet it was not long before she confessed his innocence before the women. She then accused him again and had him imprisoned, yet that became the very cause of his proximity to the King. The shirt stained with blood, which they brought to his father Ya’qub on the first day, was the sole cause for the loss of his sight; yet that very shirt, sent by his hand from Egypt to his father on the final day, became the cause for the return of his sight. Such is the pattern throughout.

In short, whenever any opposing cause contended with him or obstructed his path toward perfection (الكمال), Allah, the Exalted, made that very obstacle the cause for the rectitude of his affairs and the success of his quest. The Glorified continued to transform him from one state to another until He granted him judgment and sovereignty, chose him, taught him the interpretation of dreams (تأويل الأحاديث), and completed His favor upon him, just as his father had promised him.

Allah, the Glorified, began his story by mentioning a vision (الرؤيا) he saw at the outset while he was a young boy in his father’s care. A vision is among the glad tidings (المبشرات); He then fulfilled His glad tidings and completed His word regarding him through the divine upbringing He specifically granted him. This is His way, the Exalted, with His friends (الأولياء), as He, the Exalted, says: أَلا إِنَّ أَوْلِياءَ اللهِ لا خَوْفٌ عَلَيْهِمْ وَلا هُمْ يَحْزَنُونَ الَّذِينَ آمَنُوا وَكانُوا يَتَّقُونَ لَهُمُ الْبُشْرى فِي الْحَياةِ الدُّنْيا وَفِي الْآخِرَةِ لا تَبْدِيلَ لِكَلِماتِ اللهِ ذلِكَ هُوَ الْفَوْزُ الْعَظِيمُ "Look! The friends of Allah will indeed have no fear nor will they grieve—those who have faith and are God-conscious. For them is glad tidings in the life of this world and in the Hereafter. There is no changing the words of Allah. That is the great triumph." (Yunus: 62-64).

In the saying of the Exalted, after mentioning the vision of Yusuf and his father’s interpretation of it: لَقَدْ كانَ فِي يُوسُفَ وَإِخْوَتِهِ آياتٌ لِلسَّائِلِينَ "In Yusuf and his brothers there are certainly signs for the seekers" (Yusuf: 7), there is an indication (إشعار) that there were people who questioned the Prophet صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله regarding this story. This supports the reports stating that a group of Jews sent the polytheists of Makkah to ask the Prophet صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله about the reason for the migration of the Children of Israel to Egypt, given that Ya’qub عليه‌السلام was residing in the land of Sham; thus, the Surah was revealed.

Accordingly, the purpose is to explain his story عليه‌السلام and the story of the family of Ya’qub. Through His exposition, the Exalted has extracted the supreme purpose therein, which is the manner of Allah’s guardianship (ولاية الله) over His sincere servants, as is evident from the opening and conclusion of the Surah. The Surah is Meccan (مكية), as indicated by the context (سياق) of its verses. The report narrated from Ibn Abbas that four of its verses are Medinan—namely the first three verses and His saying: لَقَدْ كانَ فِي يُوسُفَ وَإِخْوَتِهِ آياتٌ لِلسَّائِلِينَ—is refuted by the fact that they are part of a single, unified context.

His saying, the Exalted: الر تِلْكَ آياتُ الْكِتابِ الْمُبِينِ "Alif, Lam, Ra. These are the signs of the Manifest Book." (Yusuf: 1). The use of the distant demonstrative (تِلْكَ) is for veneration and glorification. It appears that what is meant by the Manifest Book (الكتاب المبين) is this recited Quran, which is manifest and clear in itself, and manifest in clarifying for others the divine gnosis (المعارف الإلهية) and the realities of the Origin and the Return (المبدأ والمعاد) that Allah has placed within it.

The Book is described in this verse as "Manifest" (المبين), unlike His saying at the beginning of Surah Yunus: تِلْكَ آياتُ الْكِتابِ الْحَكِيمِ "These are the signs of the Wise Book," because this Surah was revealed concerning the story of the family of Ya’qub and its exposition. It is also possible that what is meant by the Manifest Book is the Preserved Tablet (اللوح المحفوظ).

His saying, the Exalted: إِنَّا أَنْزَلْناهُ قُرْآناً عَرَبِيًّا لَعَلَّكُمْ تَعْقِلُونَ "Indeed We have sent it down as an Arabic Quran so that you may exercise your reason." (Yusuf: 2). The pronoun refers to the Book insofar as it contains divine signs and true gnosis. Its being sent down as an "Arabic Quran" means it was clothed, in the stage of descent (الإنزال), in the garment of recitation and the Arabic language, making it a recited word corresponding to the language used by the Arabs, as He, the Exalted, says in another place: إِنَّا جَعَلْناهُ قُرْآناً عَرَبِيًّا لَعَلَّكُمْ تَعْقِلُونَ ، وَإِنَّهُ فِي أُمِّ الْكِتابِ لَدَيْنا لَعَلِيٌّ حَكِيمٌ "We have made it an Arabic Quran so that you may exercise your reason. And it is indeed in the Mother of the Book with Us, surely exalted and wise." (Az-Zukhruf: 3-4).

His saying: لَعَلَّكُمْ تَعْقِلُونَ "so that you may exercise your reason" is a form of broadening and generalizing the address, for the Surah opens with an address to the Prophet صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله: تِلْكَ آياتُ الْكِتابِ "These are the signs of the Book," and it continues in this manner, as in His saying: نَحْنُ نَقُصُّ عَلَيْكَ أَحْسَنَ الْقَصَصِ بِما أَوْحَيْنا إِلَيْكَ "We relate to you the best of stories by what We have revealed to you..." (Yusuf: 3).

The meaning of the verse—and Allah knows best—is: We have made this Book, which contains the signs, clothed in the garment of Arabic wording and adorned with its beauty in the stage of descent, so that it may be subject to the exercise of reason (التعقل) by you and your people or your nation. Had it not been cast in its revelation into the mold of recited wording, or had it not been made a manifest Arabic [Book], your people would not have grasped the secrets of the signs within it; rather, its understanding would have been exclusive to you due to your exclusivity in receiving its revelation and teaching.

In this, there is an indication that the words of the Mighty Book—by virtue of their determination through reliance on revelation and their being Arabic—have a role in preserving the secrets of the signs and the realities of gnosis. Had the meaning alone been revealed to the Prophet صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله, with the wording expressing it being his own صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله—as in the Divine Narrations (الأحاديث القدسية), for example—or had it been translated into another language, some of the secrets of its clear signs would have remained hidden from human intellects, and the hands of their reason and understanding would not have reached them.

His care, the Exalted, for the wording in what He revealed of His Book is something that no one who contemplates His speech can doubt. How could it be otherwise, when He has divided it into the clear (المحكمات) and the metaphorical (المتشابهات), and made the clear verses the "Mother of the Book" (أم الكتاب) to which the metaphorical ones are referred? He, the Exalted, says: هُوَ الَّذِي أَنْزَلَ عَلَيْكَ الْكِتابِ مِنْهُ آياتٌ مُحْكَماتٌ هُنَّ أُمُّ الْكِتابِ وَأُخَرُ مُتَشابِهاتٌ "It is He who has sent down to you the Book. Parts of it are definitive verses, which are the mother of the Book, while others are metaphorical." (Al-Imran: 7). He, the Exalted, also says: وَلَقَدْ نَعْلَمُ أَنَّهمْ يَقُولُونَ إِنَّما يُعَلِّمُهُ بَشَرٌ لِسانُ الَّذِي يُلْحِدُونَ إِلَيْهِ أَعْجمِيٌّ وَهذا لِسانٌ عَرَبِيٌّ مُبِينٌ "We certainly know that they say, ‘It is only a human who teaches him.’ The language of him they allude to is foreign, while this is a manifest Arabic language." (An-Nahl: 103).

His saying, the Exalted: نَحْنُ نَقُصُّ عَلَيْكَ أَحْسَنَ الْقَصَصِ بِما أَوْحَيْنا إِلَيْكَ هذَا الْقُرْآنَ وَإِنْ كُنْتَ مِنْ قَبْلِهِ لَمِنَ الْغافِلِينَ "We relate to you the best of stories by what We have revealed to you of this Quran, though before this you were indeed among the uninformed." (Yusuf: 3). Al-Raghib says in al-Mufradat: "The word al-qass (القص) means to follow a track; it is said: 'I followed his track' (قصصت أثره). Al-qasas (القصص) refers to the track itself; He said: فارتدا على آثارهما قصصا 'So they retraced their tracks' (Al-Kahf: 64), and وقالت لأخته قصيه 'She said to his sister, "Follow him"' (Al-Qasas: 11)." He continues: "Al-qasas also refers to narrated reports that are followed; He, the Exalted, says: لَهُوَ الْقَصَصُ الْحَقُ 'This is indeed the true narrative' (Al-Imran: 62), فِي قَصَصِهِمْ عِبْرَةٌ 'In their stories there is certainly a lesson' (Yusuf: 111), وَقَصَّ عَلَيْهِ الْقَصَصَ 'and he related the story to him' (Al-Qasas: 25), and نَقُصُّ عَلَيْكَ أَحْسَنَ الْقَصَصِ 'We relate to you the best of stories' (Yusuf: 3)." [End of quote]. Thus, al-qasas is the story (القصة), and "the best of stories" (أحسن القصص) is the finest story and narrative. It is also said that it is a verbal noun (مصدر) meaning the act of narrating (الاقتصاص).

If it is a noun for the story, then the story of Yusuf عليه‌السلام is the best story because it describes the sincerity of monotheism (التوحيد) — the absolute oneness of Allah in His essence, attributes, and acts — in servitude. It exemplifies the guardianship of Allah, the Glorified, over His servant, showing how He nurtures him by leading him along the path of love, raising him from the depths of humiliation to the pinnacle of might, and taking him from the pit of captivity, the tether of slavery, and the prison of punishment and affliction to the throne of honor and the seat of sovereignty.

If it is a verbal noun, then the narration (الاقتصاص) of his story in the manner that the Glorified has narrated it is the best narration, for it is the narration of a story of love and passion in the most chaste and veiled manner possible.

The meaning—and Allah knows best—is: We relate to you the best of stories by means of Our revealing this Quran to you, whereas before Our relating this story to you, you were among those unaware of it.

Ayahs

إِذْ قَالَ يُوسُفُ لِأَبِيهِ يَـٰٓأَبَتِ إِنِّى رَأَيْتُ أَحَدَ عَشَرَ كَوْكَبًا وَٱلشَّمْسَ وَٱلْقَمَرَ رَأَيْتُهُمْ لِى سَـٰجِدِينَ (٤)
When Joseph said to his father, ‘Father! I saw eleven planets, and the sun and the moon: I saw them prostrating themselves before me,’ (4)
قَالَ يَـٰبُنَىَّ لَا تَقْصُصْ رُءْيَاكَ عَلَىٰٓ إِخْوَتِكَ فَيَكِيدُوا۟ لَكَ كَيْدًا ۖ إِنَّ ٱلشَّيْطَـٰنَ لِلْإِنسَـٰنِ عَدُوٌّ مُّبِينٌ (٥)
he said, ‘My son, do not recount your dream to your brothers, lest they should devise schemes against you. Satan is indeed man’s manifest enemy. (5)
وَكَذَٰلِكَ يَجْتَبِيكَ رَبُّكَ وَيُعَلِّمُكَ مِن تَأْوِيلِ ٱلْأَحَادِيثِ وَيُتِمُّ نِعْمَتَهُۥ عَلَيْكَ وَعَلَىٰٓ ءَالِ يَعْقُوبَ كَمَآ أَتَمَّهَا عَلَىٰٓ أَبَوَيْكَ مِن قَبْلُ إِبْرَٰهِيمَ وَإِسْحَـٰقَ ۚ إِنَّ رَبَّكَ عَلِيمٌ حَكِيمٌ (٦)
That is how your Lord will choose you, and teach you the interpretation of dreams, and complete His blessing upon you and upon the house of Jacob, just as He completed it earlier for your fathers, Abraham and Isaac. Your Lord is indeed all-knowing and all-wise.’ (6)

(Commentary)

The verses recount a vision (رؤيا) that Yusuf beheld and related to his father, Ya‘qub (peace be upon him). His father interpreted it for him and forbade him from relating it to his brothers. This vision was a glad tiding (بشرى) with which Allah, the Glorified, favored Yusuf, to serve as spiritual sustenance for His upbringing (تربيته) of His servant upon the path of guardianship (صراط الولاية) and proximity to his Lord. It serves as the introduction to his story (peace be upon him).

His saying, the Exalted: إِذْ قالَ يُوسُفُ لِأَبِيهِ يا أَبَتِ إِنِّي رَأَيْتُ أَحَدَ عَشَرَ كَوْكَباً وَالشَّمْسَ وَالْقَمَرَ رَأَيْتُهُمْ لِي ساجِدِينَ — "When Joseph said to his father, ‘Father! I saw eleven planets, and the sun and the moon: I saw them prostrating themselves before me.’" Ya‘qub (peace be upon him) is not mentioned by name; rather, he is referred to by the title of "father" to indicate the attribute of mercy, kindness, and compassion between them, as evidenced by the subsequent verse: "He said, ‘O my son, do not relate...’" etc.

His saying "I saw" (رَأَيْتُ) and "I saw them" (رَأَيْتُهُمْ) refers to the vision (الرؤيا) — that which a sleeper witnesses during sleep, or one whose external senses have faded due to fainting or the like. This is attested by his saying in the following verse: "Do not relate your vision (رُؤْياكَ) to your brothers," and his saying at the end of the story: "Father! This is the interpretation of my vision (رُءْيايَ) of yore."

The repetition of the verb "to see" is due to the long interval between his saying "I saw" and his saying "prostrating themselves before me." Among the benefits of this repetition is the indication that he saw them gathered together in prostration all at once, not individually. Furthermore, what he witnessed consisted of two different types: witnessing the forms of the planets, the sun, and the moon is the witnessing of a formal matter (أمر صوري), while witnessing their prostration, submission, and veneration of him is the witnessing of a conceptual matter (أمر معنوي).

He referred to the planets and the two luminaries in his saying "I saw them prostrating themselves before me" (رَأَيْتُهُمْ لِي ساجِدِينَ) using pronouns and plural forms reserved for rational beings (أولى العقل) — the masculine plural pronoun and the sound masculine plural — to indicate that their prostration was born of knowledge and will, just as one rational being prostrates to another.

The Glorified began his story (peace be upon him) by mentioning this vision He showed him. It was a glad tiding (بشرى) for him, representing the divine guardianship (الولاية الإلهية) he would attain, the divine election (اجتباء) Allah would grant him, the teaching of the interpretation of events (تأويل الأحاديث), and the completion of His favor upon him. From that point, his divine upbringing (التربية الإلهية) began; for that which he was promised in his vision remained before his eyes throughout his life. He would not shift from one state to another, nor move from one affair to another, nor face a calamity, nor encounter an affliction, except that he would remember it, seeking support through the care of Allah, the Glorified, and conditioning his soul for patience.

This is the wisdom behind Allah, the Glorified, singling out His friends (أولياء) with glad tidings of the honors He will bestow upon them of the station of proximity (مقام القرب) and the rank of nearness (منزلة الزلفى), as in His saying: "Look! The friends of Allah will indeed have no fear nor will they grieve... For them is glad tidings in the life of this world and in the Hereafter" (Yunus: 62-64).

His saying, the Exalted: قالَ يا بُنَيَّ لا تَقْصُصْ رُؤْياكَ عَلى إِخْوَتِكَ فَيَكِيدُوا لَكَ كَيْداً إِنَّ الشَّيْطانَ لِلْإِنْسانِ عدوٌّ مُبِينٌ — "He said, ‘O my son, do not relate your vision to your brothers, lest they should devise a scheme against you. Indeed Satan is man’s manifest enemy.’" It is mentioned in al-Mufradat that a scheme (الكيد) — a type of artifice or plotting — can be blameworthy or praiseworthy, though it is more frequently used in a blameworthy sense, as are the terms "gradual enticement" (الاستدراج) and "deception" (المكر). It has also been noted that the verb "to scheme" (كاد) can be transitive by itself or through the particle lam.

The verse indicates that when Ya‘qub heard the vision Yusuf related, he became certain of what it signified: that Allah would take charge of Yusuf’s affairs, elevate his status, seat him upon the throne of sovereignty and the chair of might, and distinguish him from among the family of Ya‘qub with immense honor. Thus, he felt compassion for Yusuf (peace be upon him) and feared for him from his brothers, who were a strong group (عصبة). He feared that if they heard the vision — which clearly applied to Ya‘qub (peace be upon him), his wife, and eleven of his sons besides Yusuf, and clearly indicated that they would all submit and prostrate to Yusuf — pride and disdain would lead them to envy him and devise a scheme (يكيدوا له كيداً) to prevent the fulfillment of what his vision promised.

Therefore, he addressed Yusuf with the language of compassion, as indicated by his use of the diminutive form "O my little son" (يا بُنَيَّ), and forbade him from relating his vision to his brothers before he could interpret it for him and inform him of the divine honor (الكرامة الإلهية) decreed for him. He only prioritized the prohibition over the glad tiding due to his excessive love for him and his intense concern and care for his welfare, and because of what he perceived in his brothers of their envy and their hearts being filled with hatred and resentment toward him.

The evidence for the extent of their envy and the manifestation of their resentment and hatred is his saying: "do not relate your vision to your brothers, lest they should devise a scheme against you" (فَيَكِيدُوا لَكَ كَيْداً). He did not say, "I fear they might scheme," or "I do not feel he is safe from them," by making the fear of their scheme a mere possibility; rather, he made the scheme a direct consequence of relating the vision. He further emphasized the certainty of their scheming by using the verbal noun (المصدر) — the absolute object (المفعول المطلق) — when he said: "lest they should devise a scheme (كَيْداً) against you."

Then he reinforced this by saying, by way of explanation: "Indeed Satan is man’s manifest enemy (عدوٌّ مُبِينٌ)." That is, there is another external cause for their scheming that supports and incites the internal cause of envy, and that is Satan. He is a manifest enemy to man; there is never any friendship (خلة) between them. Through his whispering (وسوسة) and enticement (تسويل), he drives man to depart from the straight path (صراط الاستقامة) and felicity toward a crooked way of wretchedness in this world and the Hereafter. Thus, he corrupts the relationship between father and son, sows discord between brothers, and separates friends to lead them astray from the path.

The meaning is as if Ya‘qub said to Yusuf (peace be upon him): "O my son, do not relate your vision to your brothers, for they will envy you and be enraged by your affair; they will then scheme against you through the provocation and incitement of Satan, who has taken hold of their hearts and will not let them refrain from plotting against you, for Satan is a manifest enemy to man."

His saying, the Exalted: وَكَذلِكَ يَجْتَبِيكَ رَبُّكَ وَيُعَلِّمُكَ مِنْ تَأْوِيلِ الْأحادِيثِ وَيُتِمُّ نِعْمَتَهُ عَلَيْكَ وَعَلى آلِ يَعْقُوبَ — "‘That is how your Lord will choose you, and teach you the interpretation of dreams, and complete His blessing upon you and upon the house of Jacob...’" until the end of the verse. Election (الاجتباء) — the act of choosing and gathering — comes from al-jibayah, which means collecting. It is said, "I collected (جبيت) the water in the basin," and from it comes the collection of taxes (جباية الخراج). The Exalted says: "to which are brought (يُجْبى) the fruits of all things" (al-Qasas: 57). Thus, the meaning of election (الاجتباء) involves gathering the parts of a thing and preserving them from being scattered or dispersed. It also implies a movement from the gatherer toward the gathered. Allah’s election of a servant from among His servants means He targets him with His mercy, singles him out for His immense honor, gathers his affairs, protects him from wandering into the various satanic paths that disperse man, and sets him upon His straight path (صراط المستقيم). This means He takes charge of his affairs and reserves him for Himself, such that no one else has a share in him, as Allah informed regarding Yusuf (peace be upon him) when He said: "Indeed he was one of Our dedicated (المُخْلَصِينَ) servants" (Verse 24 of this Surah).

His saying: "and teach you the interpretation of events (تأويل الأحاديث)." Interpretation (التأويل) — the ultimate reality or outcome to which a matter or vision leads — is the reality that manifests to the dreamer in his vision in a form suitable to his perceptions and senses. For instance, the prostration of Yusuf’s parents and his eleven brothers was represented in the form of eleven planets, the sun, and the moon falling down prostrate before him. A comprehensive discussion on the meaning of interpretation (التأويل) was provided in the commentary on His saying: "so they follow the part of it that is metaphorical, seeking to cause discord and seeking to tap its interpretation (تأويله)" (Al ‘Imran: 7) in the third volume of this book.

Events (الأحاديث) is the plural of hadith (حديث). It may refer to visions because they are from the "speech of the soul" (حديث النفس); for the human soul portrays matters to itself during sleep just as a speaker portrays matters to a listener during wakefulness. Thus, a vision is a "speech" like it. From this, the weakness of the view that visions are called ahadith because they are narrated and spoken of becomes apparent.

Similarly, the view that they are called ahadith because they are from the "speech of the angel" if they are true, or the "speech of Satan" if they are false, is also weak. This is because a vision may not stem from an angel or Satan, such as a vision resulting from a physical temperament affecting a sleeper, like a fever or accidental heat, which the soul portrays as a hot bath or the heat of midsummer; or a coldness that the soul portrays as winter and falling snow.

Some have rejected the term "speech" (حديث) here, arguing it contradicts reality because Yusuf’s vision contained no speech, nor did the visions of his two companions in prison or the vision of the King of Egypt. However, such a person has misunderstood the meaning of "speech" (الحديث), thinking that the "speech of the angel or Satan" refers to verbal communication. This is not the case; rather, it means the dream portrays the story or an event in a suitable form, just as a verbal speaker portrays it in a linguistic form from which the listener infers the intended meaning. This is like saying of someone who intends a matter or resolves upon an action that "his soul spoke to him" (حدثته نفسه) to do such-and-one, meaning it portrayed it to him until he desired to do it, as if his soul said, "You must do this." In short, the meaning of visions being "events" (أحاديث) is that they are the portrayal of matters to the sleeper just as news and stories are portrayed through verbal speech. Thus, it is a "speech" — whether angelic, satanic, or from the soul. However, the truth is that they are from the "speech of the soul" directly, and a full discussion on this will follow, Allah willing.

Nevertheless, what is apparent from his story (peace be upon him) as narrated in this Surah is that the "events" (الأحاديث) whose interpretation Allah taught him are broader than just visions. They are the "events" — meaning the occurrences and facts — that are portrayed to man, whether in wakefulness or sleep. For there is an undeniable connection between events, the principles from which they arise, and the goals toward which they lead. Through this, they are linked to one another. It is possible for a servant, by Allah’s permission, to be guided to these links, such that the interpretation of events (تأويل الأحاديث) and the realities they lead to are unveiled to him.

This is supported, regarding sleep, by what Allah recounted of Ya‘qub’s interpretation of Yusuf’s vision, and Yusuf’s interpretation of his own vision, the visions of his two companions in prison, and the vision of the King of Egypt. Regarding wakefulness, it is supported by what He recounted of Yusuf in prison: "He said, ‘No food which you are served shall come to you, but I will inform you of its interpretation before it comes to you. That is among the things my Lord has taught me’" (Verse 37). Likewise, His saying: "So when they took him away and conspired to put him into the bottom of the cistern, We revealed to him, ‘You will surely inform them of this affair of theirs while they are not aware’" (Verse 15). The clarification of this will come to you, Allah willing.

His saying: "and complete His blessing (نِعْمَتَهُ) upon you and upon the house of Jacob." Al-Raghib said in al-Mufradat: Blessing (النعمة) — a good state or condition — is a noun of state, like "sitting" (جلسة) or "riding" (ركبة). Al-Na‘mah (with a fatha) refers to "enjoyment" (التنعم) and is a noun of instance, like "a strike" (ضربة). Al-Ni‘mah as a genus applies to both small and large amounts.

He said: Al-In‘am is the bestowing of favor upon another. It is only used when the recipient is of the rational species; it is not said, "So-and-so bestowed a blessing upon his horse." The Exalted says: "You have blessed them" (1:7) and "When you said to him whom Allah had blessed and whom you [too] had blessed" (33:37). Al-Na‘ma’ (نعماء) is the opposite of al-darra’ (ضراء) — distress.

He said: Al-Na‘im (النعيم) is "abundant blessing." The Exalted says: "in the Gardens of Bliss (النعيم)." Tanattu‘ (تنعم) is to partake in what contains blessing and a pleasant life. It is said, "He blessed him (نعمه) with a blessing (تنعيماً)," meaning he made his life soft and fertile. The Exalted says: "He honors him and blesses him (ونعمه)." And "soft (ناعم) food" and a "tender (ناعمة) girl." [End of quote].

Thus, the word contains — as you see — an element of softness, pleasantness, and suitability. It seems to be derived from "softness" (النعومة), which is its root meaning. Its usage has been specialized for humans because they possess an intellect by which they distinguish the beneficial from the harmful; thus, they find the beneficial pleasant and suitable and enjoy it (يتنعم به), unlike others who do not distinguish what benefits them from what harms them. Similarly, wealth, children, and other things considered blessings may be a blessing for one and a curse (نقمة) for another, or a blessing for a person in one state and a curse in another.

For this reason, the Noble Quran does not consider these divine gifts — such as wealth, status, spouses, and children — as a blessing (نعمة) for a person unless they occur on the path of felicity and are colored with the hue of divine guardianship (الولاية الإلهية), drawing the person near to Allah. If they occur on the path of wretchedness and under the guardianship of Satan, they are a curse (نقمة) and not a blessing. The verses on this are numerous.

Yes, if attributed to Allah, the Glorified, they are a blessing from Him, a favor, and a mercy, because He is Goodness that emanates goodness and does not intend evil or harm in His gift. He is the All-Compassionate, the Most Merciful, the All-Forgiving, the All-Loving. The Exalted says: "If you enumerate Allah’s blessings, you will not be able to count them" (Ibrahim: 34), and the address in the verse is to all people. The Exalted says: "Leave Me to the deniers, the possessors of ease (أُولِي النَّعْمَةِ), and give them a little respite" (al-Muzzammil: 11). And He says: "Then, when We grant him a blessing (نِعْمَةً) from Us, he says, ‘I was given it only because of [my] knowledge’" (al-Zumar: 49). These and their like are blessings when attributed to Him, the Exalted, but they are a curse when attributed to the one who is ungrateful for them. The Exalted says: "If you are grateful, I will surely enhance you [in blessing], but if you are ungrateful, My punishment is indeed severe" (Ibrahim: 7).

In short, if a person is under the guardianship (ولاية) of Allah, all the means they employ to sustain life and attain felicity are divine blessings for them. If they are under the guardianship of Satan, all of them turn into curses. Yet, they are all blessings from Allah, the Glorified, even if they are met with ingratitude.

Furthermore, if the means of life are deficient and do not suffice for all aspects of a felicitous life, they are a blessing [in a sense], like one who is given wealth but deprived of security and peace, thus unable to enjoy it as he wishes, when he wishes, and where he wishes. But when he has that which enables him to reach the felicity of life without any deficiency, that is the completion of the blessing (تمام النعمة).

Thus, His saying: "and complete His blessing upon you and upon the house of Jacob" means that Allah has blessed you with that which will make you felicitous in your life, but He will complete that for you and for the house of Ya‘qub — who are Ya‘qub, his wife, and the rest of his sons — just as he saw in his vision.

He made Yusuf the primary subject and the house of Ya‘qub joined to him, saying: "upon you and upon the house of Jacob," as indicated by the vision; for Yusuf saw himself as the one prostrated to, and he saw the house of Ya‘qub in the form of the sun, the moon, and eleven planets prostrating to him.

Allah mentioned among the things with which He completed the blessing upon Yusuf (peace be upon him) that He gave him judgment (الحكم), prophethood, sovereignty, and might in Egypt, in addition to making him one of the dedicated (المخلصين) and teaching him the interpretation of events. Among the things with which He completed the blessing upon the house of Ya‘qub was that He delighted Ya‘qub’s eye with his son Yusuf (peace be upon him), and brought him and all his family from the desert, providing them with a settled life by settling in Egypt.

His saying: كَما أَتَمَّها عَلى أَبَوَيْكَ مِنْ قَبْلُ إِبْراهِيمَ وَإِسْحاقَ — "‘...as He completed it before for your fathers, Abraham and Isaac.’" That is, similar to how He completed the blessing before upon Ibrahim and Ishaq, who are your fathers, for He gave them the good of this world and the Hereafter. His saying "before" (مِنْ قَبْلُ) is connected to "He completed it." It is also possible that it is an adverbial phrase acting as an attribute for "your fathers," meaning "your fathers who existed before."

"Abraham and Isaac" (إِبْراهِيمَ وَإِسْحاقَ) is an appositive (بدل) or an explanatory adjunct (عطف بيان) for "your fathers." The benefit of this phrasing is to intimate that the blessing is continuous and inherited in the house of Ibrahim through the line of Ishaq, as Allah completed it upon Ibrahim, Ishaq, Ya‘qub, Yusuf (peace be upon him), and the rest of the house of Ya‘qub.

The meaning of the verse is: Just as you saw in your vision, your Lord will reserve you for Himself by purifying you from polytheism (الشرك), so that no one else has a share in you. He will teach you the interpretation of events — which is the ultimate outcome of occurrences portrayed in sleep or wakefulness — and He will complete this blessing, which is the divine guardianship (الولاية الإلهية), by your settling in Egypt, the gathering of your family, and the sovereignty and might bestowed upon you, your parents, and your brothers. Your Lord only does this for you because He is All-Knowing of His servants, aware of their states, and All-Wise, decreeing for them what they deserve. He is All-Knowing of your state and what they deserve of His wrath.

Contemplation of this noble verse yields the following:

First: Ya‘qub was also among the dedicated (المخلصين), and Allah had taught him the interpretation of events. For he (peace be upon him) informed, as in this verse, of the interpretation of Yusuf’s vision, and he would not have informed based on conjecture or guesswork without Allah having taught him that.

Furthermore, after Allah recounted his words to his sons: "O my sons, do not enter by one gate, but enter by separate gates..." etc., He said regarding him: "Indeed he possessed knowledge because of what We had taught him, but most people do not know" (12:68).

Moreover, after He recounted Yusuf’s words in prison while conversing with his two companions: "No food which you are served shall come to you, but I will inform you of its interpretation before it comes to you. That is among the things my Lord has taught me," he informed that it is from the interpretation of events and that his Lord had taught him that. Then he explained the reason for this teaching by saying: "Indeed I have renounced the creed of a people who do not believe in Allah and who are disbelievers in the Hereafter. And I follow the creed of my fathers, Abraham, Isaac and Jacob. It is not for us to ascribe any partner to Allah..." etc. Thus, he informed that he is dedicated (مُخْلَص) — purified and reserved — for Allah like his fathers Ibrahim, Ishaq, and Ya‘qub, pure in existence and sound of heart from polytheism (الشرك) absolutely. Therefore, his Lord taught him, among what He taught him, the interpretation of events. The shared cause — as you see — indicates that his noble fathers Ibrahim, Ishaq, and Ya‘qub were, like him, dedicated to Allah and taught the interpretation of events.

This is supported by Allah’s saying in another place: "And remember Our servants, Abraham, Isaac and Jacob, men of strength and insight. Indeed We purified them with an exclusive quality: the remembrance of the [ultimate] abode" (Sad: 45-46). It also indicates that knowledge of the interpretation of events is a branch of being dedicated (الإخلاص) to Allah, the Glorified.

Second: Everything Ya‘qub (peace be upon him) informed of corresponds to the core of what Yusuf (peace be upon him) saw in the vision, namely the prostration of the sun, the moon, and eleven planets to him. This is because their prostration to him — including Ya‘qub, who was among the dedicated and would prostrate to none but Allah alone — reveals that they only prostrated before Yusuf for Allah. They took Yusuf only as a direction (قبلة), like the Ka‘bah toward which one prostrates, intending thereby only Allah, the Glorified. Thus, there was nothing with Yusuf or for him except Allah. This is what it means for a servant to be dedicated (مُخْلَص) to his Lord, reserved for Him, with nothing sharing with Him in that servant, as Yusuf alluded to by saying: "It is not for us to ascribe any partner to Allah." It was mentioned just now that knowledge of the interpretation of events is a branch of dedication.

Hence, Ya‘qub said in his interpretation of the vision: "That is how (وَكَذلِكَ)" — meaning, just as you saw yourself being prostrated to — "(يَجْتَبِيكَ رَبُّكَ)" — meaning, He reserves you for Himself — "(وَيُعَلِّمُكَ مِنْ تَأْوِيلِ الْأحادِيثِ)."

Similarly, seeing the house of Ya‘qub in the form of the sun, the moon, and eleven planets — which are celestial bodies high in station, radiant in light, and vast in orbits — indicates that their status would be elevated and their rank would rise in their felicitous human life, which is the religious life that builds both this world and the Hereafter, and that they would be distinguished in this from others.

Hence, Ya‘qub continued his speech and said: "and complete His blessing upon you (وَيُتِمُّ نِعْمَتَهُ عَلَيْكَ)" — meaning, upon you alone, distinguished from others, just as you saw yourself as such — "and upon the house of Jacob (وَعَلى آلِ يَعْقُوبَ)" — meaning, upon me, my wife, and all my children, just as you saw us gathered together, similar in form — "as He completed it before for your fathers, Abraham and Isaac. Indeed your Lord is All-knowing, All-wise."

Third: The meaning of "completing the blessing" (إتمام النعمة) is following the guardianship (الولاية) by removing all other deficiencies of a felicitous life and joining this world to the Hereafter. There is no contradiction between attributing the completion of the blessing to everyone and the specialization of election (الاجتباء) and the teaching of the interpretation of events to Ya‘qub and Yusuf (peace be upon them) among them. This is because the blessing — which is guardianship — has different degrees and varying ranks. When attributed to everyone, each takes his share of it.

Furthermore, it is permissible to attribute a matter to a group by virtue of it including parts, some of which embody the meaning of that matter, as in His saying: "Certainly We gave the Children of Israel the Book, judgement and prophethood, and We provided them with all the good things" (al-Jathiyah: 16). The giving of the Book, judgment, and prophethood was specific to some of them, not all, unlike the provision of good things.

Fourth: Yusuf was the means through which Allah, the Glorified, completed His blessing upon the house of Ya‘qub. Therefore, Ya‘qub made him the primary subject in the speech and joined others to him, even distinguishing him from among his family and mentioning him individually when he said: "and complete His blessing upon you and upon the house of Jacob."

For this reason also, he attributed this care and mercy to his Lord, saying repeatedly: "your Lord" (رَبُّكَ), and did not say "Allah chooses you" or "Allah is All-Knowing, All-Wise." All of this testifies that he is the primary cause in the completion of the blessing upon the house of Ya‘qub. As for his fathers Ibrahim and Ishaq, the expression indicating comparison — "as He completed it before for your fathers, Abraham and Isaac" — removes them from being under the primacy of Yusuf. Understand this.

(Narrative Discussion)

In Tafsir al-Qummi, it is said: In the narration of Abu al-Jarud from Abu Ja‘far (peace be upon him), he said: "The interpretation of this vision is that he would rule Egypt and his parents and brothers would enter upon him. As for the sun, it was Yusuf’s mother, Rachel; the moon was Ya‘qub; and the eleven planets were his brothers. When they entered upon him, they prostrated in gratitude to Allah alone when they looked at him, and that prostration was for Allah."

In al-Durr al-Manthur, Ibn al-Mundhir narrated from Ibn ‘Abbas regarding His saying, the Exalted: "eleven planets," he said: "his brothers"; "the sun," he said: "his mother"; and "the moon," he said: "his father." And his mother Rachel possessed a third of all beauty.

I say: The two narrations — as you see — interpret the sun as his mother and the moon as his father, and they are not free from weakness. It has also been narrated that the one who entered upon him in Egypt was his maternal aunt, not his mother, for his mother had died before that; this is also mentioned in the Torah.

In Tafsir al-Qummi, from al-Baqir (peace be upon him): "He had eleven brothers, and he had one brother from his mother named Benjamin." He said: "Yusuf saw this vision when he was nine years old, and he related it to his father, who said: 'O my son, do not relate...' (the verse)."

I say: In some narrations, it is said he was seven years old that day, and in the Torah, it says he was sixteen years old. This is unlikely.

There are other narrations regarding the story of the vision, some of which will follow in the upcoming traditionary discussion, Allah willing.

Ayahs

۞ لَّقَدْ كَانَ فِى يُوسُفَ وَإِخْوَتِهِۦٓ ءَايَـٰتٌ لِّلسَّآئِلِينَ (٧)
In Joseph and his brothers there are certainly signs for the seekers. (7)
إِذْ قَالُوا۟ لَيُوسُفُ وَأَخُوهُ أَحَبُّ إِلَىٰٓ أَبِينَا مِنَّا وَنَحْنُ عُصْبَةٌ إِنَّ أَبَانَا لَفِى ضَلَـٰلٍ مُّبِينٍ (٨)
When they said, ‘Surely Joseph and his brother are dearer to our father than [the rest of] us, though we are a hardy band. Our father is indeed in manifest error.’ (8)
ٱقْتُلُوا۟ يُوسُفَ أَوِ ٱطْرَحُوهُ أَرْضًا يَخْلُ لَكُمْ وَجْهُ أَبِيكُمْ وَتَكُونُوا۟ مِنۢ بَعْدِهِۦ قَوْمًا صَـٰلِحِينَ (٩)
‘Kill Joseph or cast him away into some [distant] land, so that your father’s attention may be exclusively towards you, and thereafter you may become a righteous lot.’ (9)
قَالَ قَآئِلٌ مِّنْهُمْ لَا تَقْتُلُوا۟ يُوسُفَ وَأَلْقُوهُ فِى غَيَـٰبَتِ ٱلْجُبِّ يَلْتَقِطْهُ بَعْضُ ٱلسَّيَّارَةِ إِن كُنتُمْ فَـٰعِلِينَ (١٠)
One of them said, ‘Do not kill Joseph, but cast him into the recess of a well so that some caravan may pick him up, if you are to do [anything].’ (10)
قَالُوا۟ يَـٰٓأَبَانَا مَا لَكَ لَا تَأْمَ۫نَّا عَلَىٰ يُوسُفَ وَإِنَّا لَهُۥ لَنَـٰصِحُونَ (١١)
They said, ‘Father! Why is it that you do not trust us with Joseph? We are indeed his well-wishers. (11)
أَرْسِلْهُ مَعَنَا غَدًا يَرْتَعْ وَيَلْعَبْ وَإِنَّا لَهُۥ لَحَـٰفِظُونَ (١٢)
Let him go with us tomorrow so that he may eat lots of fruits and play, and we will indeed take [good] care of him.’ (12)
قَالَ إِنِّى لَيَحْزُنُنِىٓ أَن تَذْهَبُوا۟ بِهِۦ وَأَخَافُ أَن يَأْكُلَهُ ٱلذِّئْبُ وَأَنتُمْ عَنْهُ غَـٰفِلُونَ (١٣)
He said, ‘It really upsets me that you should take him away, and I fear the wolf may eat him while you are oblivious of him.’ (13)
قَالُوا۟ لَئِنْ أَكَلَهُ ٱلذِّئْبُ وَنَحْنُ عُصْبَةٌ إِنَّآ إِذًا لَّخَـٰسِرُونَ (١٤)
They said, ‘Should the wolf eat him while we are a hardy band, then we will indeed be losers!’ (14)
فَلَمَّا ذَهَبُوا۟ بِهِۦ وَأَجْمَعُوٓا۟ أَن يَجْعَلُوهُ فِى غَيَـٰبَتِ ٱلْجُبِّ ۚ وَأَوْحَيْنَآ إِلَيْهِ لَتُنَبِّئَنَّهُم بِأَمْرِهِمْ هَـٰذَا وَهُمْ لَا يَشْعُرُونَ (١٥)
So when they took him away and conspired to put him into the recess of a well, We revealed to him, ‘[A day will come when] you will surely inform them about this affair of theirs while they are not aware [of your identity].’ (15)
وَجَآءُوٓ أَبَاهُمْ عِشَآءً يَبْكُونَ (١٦)
In the evening, they came weeping to their father. (16)
قَالُوا۟ يَـٰٓأَبَانَآ إِنَّا ذَهَبْنَا نَسْتَبِقُ وَتَرَكْنَا يُوسُفَ عِندَ مَتَـٰعِنَا فَأَكَلَهُ ٱلذِّئْبُ ۖ وَمَآ أَنتَ بِمُؤْمِنٍ لَّنَا وَلَوْ كُنَّا صَـٰدِقِينَ (١٧)
They said, ‘Father! We had gone racing and left Joseph with our things, whereat the wolf ate him. But you will not believe us even if we spoke truly.’ (17)
وَجَآءُو عَلَىٰ قَمِيصِهِۦ بِدَمٍ كَذِبٍ ۚ قَالَ بَلْ سَوَّلَتْ لَكُمْ أَنفُسُكُمْ أَمْرًا ۖ فَصَبْرٌ جَمِيلٌ ۖ وَٱللَّهُ ٱلْمُسْتَعَانُ عَلَىٰ مَا تَصِفُونَ (١٨)
And they produced sham blood on his shirt. He said, ‘No, your souls have made a matter seem decorous to you. Yet patience is graceful, and Allah is my resort against what you allege.’ (18)
وَجَآءَتْ سَيَّارَةٌ فَأَرْسَلُوا۟ وَارِدَهُمْ فَأَدْلَىٰ دَلْوَهُۥ ۖ قَالَ يَـٰبُشْرَىٰ هَـٰذَا غُلَـٰمٌ ۚ وَأَسَرُّوهُ بِضَـٰعَةً ۚ وَٱللَّهُ عَلِيمٌۢ بِمَا يَعْمَلُونَ (١٩)
There came a caravan, and they sent their water-drawer, who let down his bucket. ‘Good news!’ he said. ‘This is a young boy!’ So they hid him as [a piece of] merchandise, and Allah knew best what they were doing. (19)
وَشَرَوْهُ بِثَمَنٍۭ بَخْسٍ دَرَٰهِمَ مَعْدُودَةٍ وَكَانُوا۟ فِيهِ مِنَ ٱلزَّٰهِدِينَ (٢٠)
And they sold him for a cheap price, a few dirhams, for they set small store by him. (20)
وَقَالَ ٱلَّذِى ٱشْتَرَىٰهُ مِن مِّصْرَ لِٱمْرَأَتِهِۦٓ أَكْرِمِى مَثْوَىٰهُ عَسَىٰٓ أَن يَنفَعَنَآ أَوْ نَتَّخِذَهُۥ وَلَدًا ۚ وَكَذَٰلِكَ مَكَّنَّا لِيُوسُفَ فِى ٱلْأَرْضِ وَلِنُعَلِّمَهُۥ مِن تَأْوِيلِ ٱلْأَحَادِيثِ ۚ وَٱللَّهُ غَالِبٌ عَلَىٰٓ أَمْرِهِۦ وَلَـٰكِنَّ أَكْثَرَ ٱلنَّاسِ لَا يَعْلَمُونَ (٢١)
The man from Egypt who had bought him said to his wife, ‘Give him an honourable place [in the household]. Maybe he will be useful to us, or we may adopt him as a son.’ Thus We established Joseph in the land and that We might teach him the interpretation of dreams. Allah has [full] command of His affairs, but most people do not know. (21)

(Commentary)

The narrative commences after the mention of the glad tidings, which served as a preamble alluding to the overall objective toward which the story concludes. These verses comprise the first chapter of the story’s episodes, detailing Yusuf’s separation from Ya‘qub (peace be upon him) and his departure from his father’s house until his settlement in the house of the ‘Aziz in Egypt. During these events, his brothers cast him into the well, a caravan extracted him from it, and his brothers sold him to the caravan, who then carried him to Egypt and sold him to the ‘Aziz, where he remained.

His saying, the Exalted: «لَقَدْ كانَ فِي يُوسُفَ وَإِخْوَتِهِ آياتٌ لِلسَّائِلِينَ» — In Yusuf and his brothers there are certainly signs for the seekers. (12:7). This is the commencement of the story, containing a reminder that the narrative encompasses divine signs (آيات) indicating the monotheism (التوحيد) — the absolute oneness of Allah in His essence, attributes, and acts — of Allah, the Glorified, and that He is the Guardian (الولي) — the protecting friend and master — who manages the affairs of His sincere servants until He raises them to the throne of might and establishes them upon the seat of perfection. For He, the Exalted, is the Master of His affair; He drives causes to wherever He wills, not to where others will, and He derives from them what He intends, not what appears manifest from them.

Thus, these brothers of Yusuf (peace be upon him) envied their brother, plotted against him, and cast him into the depths of a well, then sold him to the caravan as a slave, intending thereby to drive him toward destruction. Yet Allah gave him life through the very cause from which destruction appeared manifest. They intended to humiliate him, but Allah granted him might through the very cause of humiliation. They lowered him, but Allah raised him through the very cause of lowering and reduction. They intended to divert their father’s love to themselves so that their father’s attention would be exclusively theirs, but Allah reversed the matter. They took away their father’s sight when they announced Yusuf’s death to him with his shirt stained with blood, yet Allah restored his sight to him by means of the same shirt, brought by the bearer of good news, who cast it upon his face.

Yusuf (peace be upon him) continued such that whenever anyone intended evil toward him, Allah delivered him from it and made it an occasion for the manifestation of his nobility and the beauty of his soul. Whenever he was made to travel a path or embark on a way that seemed to lead to destruction or calamity, Allah guided him through that very path to a beautiful end and a manifest, noble merit. Yusuf (peace be upon him) alludes to this when he identifies himself to his brothers, saying: «أَنَا يُوسُفُ وَهذا أَخِي قَدْ مَنَّ اللهُ عَلَيْنا إِنَّهُ مَنْ يَتَّقِ وَيَصْبِرْ فَإِنَّ اللهَ لا يُضِيعُ أَجْرَ الْمُحْسِنِينَ» — ‘I am Yusuf, and this is my brother. Allah has certainly been gracious to us. Indeed whoever is God-conscious and patient—Allah does not let the reward of the virtuous go to waste.’ (12:90). And he says to his father in the presence of his brothers: «يا أَبَتِ هذا تَأْوِيلُ رُءْيايَ مِنْ قَبْلُ قَدْ جَعَلَها رَبِّي حَقًّا وَقَدْ أَحْسَنَ بِي إِذْ أَخْرَجَنِي مِنَ السِّجْنِ وَجاءَ بِكُمْ مِنَ الْبَدْوِ مِنْ بَعْدِ أَنْ نَزَغَ الشَّيْطانُ بَيْنِي وَبَيْنَ إِخْوَتِي» — ‘Father, this is the fulfillment of my dream of old. My Lord has made it come true, and He was certainly kind to me when He released me from the prison and brought you from the desert after Satan had incited ill feeling between me and my brothers.’ (12:100). Then, seized by divine attraction (الجذبية الإلهية), he turns with the entirety of his yearning soul toward his Lord, turning away from all else, saying: «رَبِّ قَدْ آتَيْتَنِي مِنَ الْمُلْكِ وَعَلَّمْتَنِي مِنْ تَأْوِيلِ الْأَحادِيثِ فاطِرَ السَّماواتِ وَالْأَرْضِ أَنْتَ وَلِيِّي فِي الدُّنْيا وَالْآخِرَةِ» — ‘My Lord! You have granted me a share in the kingdom, and taught me the interpretation of dreams. Originator of the heavens and the earth! You are my guardian in this world and the Hereafter.’ (12:101).

In His saying, the Exalted, «لِلسَّائِلِينَ» (for the seekers), there is an indication that there was a group who had asked the Prophet (صلى الله عليه وآله وسلم) about the story or about something related to it, so it was revealed in this Surah.

His saying, the Exalted: «إِذْ قالُوا لَيُوسُفُ وَأَخُوهُ أَحَبُّ إِلى أَبِينا مِنَّا وَنَحْنُ عُصْبَةٌ إِنَّ أَبانا لَفِي ضَلالٍ مُبِينٍ» — When they said, ‘Surely Yusuf and his brother are dearer to our father than we are, though we are a [strong] group. Our father is indeed in manifest error.’ (12:8). It is mentioned in al-Majma‘ that the "group" (العصبة) refers to a party whose members support one another (يتعصب بعضها لبعض). It applies to a group of ten to fifteen, and it is said between ten and forty. It has no singular form from its own root, like the terms qawm (قوم), raht (رهط), and nafar (نفر). End quote.

Regarding their saying, «لَيُوسُفُ وَأَخُوهُ أَحَبُّ إِلى أَبِينا مِنَّا» (Surely Yusuf and his brother are dearer to our father than we are), the speakers were the sons of Ya‘qub, excluding Yusuf and his brother whom they mentioned with him. Their number was ten, and they were strong men in whose hands lay the management of their father Ya‘qub’s household and the administration of his livestock and wealth, as indicated by their saying: «وَنَحْنُ عُصْبَةٌ» (though we are a [strong] group).

Their saying, «لَيُوسُفُ وَأَخُوهُ» (Surely Yusuf and his brother), attributing the brother to Yusuf despite all of them being sons of Ya‘qub and brothers among themselves, suggests that Yusuf and this brother were full brothers from one mother, while they were half-brothers to the speakers from the father’s side only. Narrations mention that the name of this brother of Yusuf was "Benjamin," and the context bears witness that they were both young, not yet engaged in the affairs of Ya‘qub’s household or the management of his livestock and wealth.

Their saying, «وَنَحْنُ عُصْبَةٌ» (though we are a [strong] group), meaning ten strong men whose individual weakness is bolstered by the strength of the others, is a circumstantial clause (حال) related to the preceding sentence. It indicates their envy and resentment toward the two of them, and their anger at their father Ya‘qub for loving them more than themselves. It serves as the complete justification for their subsequent statement: «إِنَّ أَبانا لَفِي ضَلالٍ مُبِينٍ» (Our father is indeed in manifest error).

Their saying, «إِنَّ أَبانا لَفِي ضَلالٍ مُبِينٍ» (Our father is indeed in manifest error), is a judgment they passed upon their father of being in "error" (الضلال). By "error," they meant a deviation in natural disposition (السليقة) and a flaw in conduct (السيرة), rather than error in religion (الدين).

This is so, firstly, because it is necessitated by what they discussed among themselves: that they were a group of strong, mutually supportive brothers who managed their father’s vital affairs, secured his livelihood, and repelled every harm facing him, while Yusuf and his brother were small children incapable of handling any life affairs, being nothing but a burden (كل) upon him and upon them. If this were the case, their father’s total immersion in his love for the two of them, his complete preoccupation with them over the others, and his turning toward them while turning away from the rest, would be a crooked and unsatisfactory way. For the wisdom of life requires that a person attend to each of his means and instruments in proportion to their impact. For a person to restrict his attention to those who are a burden and provide no benefit, while turning away from those who hold the keys to his life and the reins of his livelihood, is nothing but a deviation from the straight path (صراط الاستقامة) and a distortion in management. As for error in religion, it has other causes, such as disbelief in Allah and His signs, and opposing His commands and prohibitions.

Secondly, they were believers in Allah, acknowledging the prophethood of their father Ya‘qub, as evident from their saying: «وَتَكُونُوا مِنْ بَعْدِهِ قَوْماً صالِحِينَ» (and be righteous people after him) and their later saying: «يا أَبانَا اسْتَغْفِرْ لَنا ذُنُوبَنا» — ‘Father! Plead [with Allah] for the forgiveness of our sins.’ (12:97), and their final words to Yusuf: «تَاللهِ لَقَدْ آثَرَكَ اللهُ عَلَيْنا» — ‘By Allah, Allah has certainly preferred you over us.’ (12:91), among other instances. Had they intended by their saying, «إِنَّ أَبانا لَفِي ضَلالٍ مُبِينٍ» (Our father is indeed in manifest error), error in religion, they would have been disbelievers (كافرين) by that statement.

Furthermore, they loved their father, honored him, and revered him. They only did what they did to Yusuf so that their father’s love would be exclusively theirs, as they said: «اقْتُلُوا يُوسُفَ أَوِ اطْرَحُوهُ أَرْضاً يَخْلُ لَكُمْ وَجْهُ أَبِيكُمْ» — ‘Kill Yusuf or cast him into some land, so that your father’s attention may be exclusively yours.’ (12:9). Thus—as this context indicates—they loved him and desired that his love be purely for them. Had it been otherwise, they would naturally have been prompted to start with their father rather than their brother, and to kill Ya‘qub, isolate him, or weaken him until the atmosphere was clear for them and the matter settled; then dealing with Yusuf would have been easier for them.

They eventually confronted their father with a similar statement when he said to them: «إِنِّي لَأَجِدُ رِيحَ يُوسُفَ لَوْ لا أَنْ تُفَنِّدُونِ قالُوا تَاللهِ إِنَّكَ لَفِي ضَلالِكَ الْقَدِيمِ» — ‘I surely perceive the scent of Yusuf, unless you think I am doting.’ They said, ‘By Allah, you are still in your old error.’ (12:94-95). It is well known that the intended meaning was not error in religion, but rather an excess in his love for Yusuf and an exaggeration in his matter beyond what was appropriate.

It appears from the verse and the related verses that Ya‘qub (peace be upon him) lived in the desert (البدو) and had twelve sons from different mothers. Ten of them were adults, a strong and powerful group (عصبة) upon whom the wheel of his life turned, and through whose hands he managed his wealth and livestock. Two of them were young, full brothers from one mother, under their father’s care: Yusuf and his brother. Ya‘qub (peace be upon him) was devoted to them, loving them with an intense love because of the signs of perfection and godwariness (التقوى) — the state of being conscious of Allah and guarding oneself against His displeasure — that he discerned in their countenances, not out of some personal whim (هوى نفساني). How could it be otherwise? He was one of the sincere servants (المخلصين) of Allah, praised by His saying, the Exalted: «إِنَّا أَخْلَصْناهُمْ بِخالِصَةٍ ذِكْرَى الدَّارِ» — ‘Indeed We purified them with a quality of exclusive purity: the remembrance of the [ultimate] abode.’ (38:46), as previously indicated.

This love and preference stirred the envy of the other brothers toward them and ignited the fire of resentment against them. Ya‘qub (peace be upon him) discerned this and increased his love for them, especially for Yusuf. He feared for him from them, was not pleased with their being alone with him, and did not trust them with him. This only increased their envy and rage. He began to discern evil and plotting (المكر) in their faces, as inferred from his saying: «فَيَكِيدُوا لَكَ كَيْداً» (lest they should devise a plot against you), until Yusuf saw the dream and recounted it to his father. This increased his father’s apprehension for him, intensified his love, and deepened his affection. He instructed him to keep his dream secret and not inform his brothers, hoping thereby to secure him from their plot, but destiny (التقدير) overcame his management (تدبيره).

The elder sons of Ya‘qub gathered and discussed what they observed of their father’s conduct and his treatment of Yusuf and his brother—how he was preoccupied with them over the others and preferred them, though they were small children who provided him no benefit, while they were a strong group, the pillars of his life and his effective hands in repelling every common calamity, securing the benefits of livelihood, and managing wealth and livestock. They felt it was not part of good conduct or a straight path to prefer these two weaklings, despite their weakness, over that strong group, despite their strength. Thus, they censured their father’s conduct and judged that he was in manifest error regarding this method of his.

They did not intend by accusing their father of error an error in religion, which would have made them disbelievers, but rather error in his social conduct, as supported by numerous textual evidences already mentioned.

From this, the deviation in various commentaries regarding the interpretation of the verse becomes apparent:

Among them: what some have mentioned—that their judgment of their father’s deviation from the path of justice and equality was manifest ignorance and a great mistake, perhaps caused by an earlier accusation of his excessive love for their mother. Thus, the primary trigger would be the difference in mothers due to polygamy, especially involving bondwomen (1), which misled them regarding the parental instinct of increased affection for younger and weaker children, as they were the youngest of his children.

He said: Among the benefits of the story is the necessity of parents’ care in dealing with children, raising them on love and justice, and preventing the occurrence of mutual envy and hatred among them. This includes avoiding the favoring of some over others in a way that the unfavored child considers an insult to himself and a display of favoritism toward his brother based on whim. The Prophet (صلى الله عليه وآله وسلم) forbade this absolutely. It also includes following the path of wisdom in favoring those whom Allah the Exalted has favored with innate gifts (المواهب الفطرية) such as noble character, godwariness, knowledge, and intelligence.

Ya‘qub was not one to whom this was hidden, and he only forbade Yusuf from recounting his dream to them out of his knowledge of what was necessary. But what can a person do with his instinct, heart, and soul? Can he prevent their authority over his limbs? Not at all. End quote.

As for his saying that the origin of their envy and transgression was the difference in mothers, especially bondwomen, etc.—it must be said: while the fact that different mothers can lead to differences among children is something that cannot be reasonably denied, and its presence in this case is possible, the cause mentioned in the speech of Allah the Exalted for this is different. Had it been the sole cause, they would have done to Yusuf’s brother what they did to him and would not have been satisfied with Yusuf alone.

(1) An allusion to what is in the Torah: that Ya‘qub had twelve male children: Reuben, Simeon, Levi, Judah, Issachar, and Zebulun from Leah, the daughter of his maternal uncle; Yusuf and Benjamin from Rachel, his other maternal uncle’s daughter; Dan and Naphtali from Rachel’s handmaid; and Gad and Asher from Zilpah, Leah’s handmaid.

As for his saying: "which misled them regarding the parental instinct of increased affection for younger and weaker children," the implication of which is that Ya‘qub’s love for Yusuf was merely an instinctive tenderness and compassion due to their youth—as is observed in fathers toward their small children as long as they are young, and when they grow, it transfers to those younger than them.

It must be said: this type of love, mixed with tenderness and compassion, is something that adults concede to the young and cease to compete with or oppose them over. You see the elder children, when they witness the parents’ increased attention to their younger and weaker siblings, and they object that this is contrary to balance and equality, if they are answered that they are small and weak and must be treated with tenderness, compassion, and help until they are fit to stand on their own in life, they fall silent, cease their objection, and are convinced by that.

If the form of Ya‘qub’s love for Yusuf and his brother were the form of tenderness, kindness, and mercy for them due to their youth—which every member of the group recognizes in himself and remembers from his father toward him during his own childhood—they would not have found fault with it nor censured their father for it. Their saying «وَنَحْنُ عُصْبَةٌ» (though we are a [strong] group) would then have been evidence against them, indicating their own error in attributing error to their father, rather than evidence for them indicating their father’s error in his increased love for the two of them.

Furthermore, they said to their father when they spoke to him regarding Yusuf’s matter: «ما لَكَ لا تَأْمَنَّا عَلى يُوسُفَ وَإِنَّا لَهُ لَناصِحُونَ» (‘What is the matter with you that you do not trust us with Yusuf? We are indeed his well-wishers.’). It is well known that his honoring of Yusuf, keeping him close, monitoring him, and not trusting any of them with him, was a matter beyond love based on tenderness and mercy for him and for his youth and weakness.

As for his saying: "Ya‘qub was not one to whom this was hidden..." until the end of what he said—the meaning of which is that Ya‘qub’s whim for his son diverted him from what was obligatory in raising his children, despite his knowledge that this was contrary to justice and fairness, and that it would drive him into a trial regarding his children; then excusing him by saying that opposing the heart’s whim and the soul’s attachment is something a person cannot do.

It must be said: this is a corruption of the established rational and textual principles from which the realities of the stations of the prophets and the scholars of Allah—among the truthful (الصديقين), the witnesses (الشهداء), and the righteous (الصالحين)—are derived. It also contradicts what the study of noble character (كرائم الأخلاق) is built upon: that a person, according to his primordial nature (الفطرة) — the innate disposition toward truth that Allah placed in every human being — has the capacity to adopt such character and to annihilate the psychological vices whose origin and foundation is following the soul’s whim and preferring the pleasure of Allah, the Glorified, over every other pleasure and desire. This is something we hope for from anyone who has disciplined themselves with moral exercises among the people of godwariness and piety; so what then of the prophets, and specifically one like Ya‘qub (peace be upon him) among them?

I wonder, if it were not within a person’s capacity to oppose his soul’s whim in such matters, what would be the meaning of these numerous religious commands and prohibitions related to them? Would they be anything but sheer futility?

Moreover, what he mentioned is a disparagement of the stations of Allah’s prophets and His friends, and a lowering of their positions of servitude to the level of average people—captives of their souls’ whims, ignorant of their Lord’s station. Allah, the Glorified, has described the prophets with sayings such as: «وَاجْتَبَيْناهُمْ وَهَدَيْناهُمْ إِلى صِراطٍ مُسْتَقِيمٍ» — ‘and We chose them and guided them to a straight path.’ (6:87). He said regarding Ya‘qub and his fathers Ibrahim and Ishaq (peace be upon them): «وَكُلًّا جَعَلْنا صالِحِينَ وَجَعَلْناهُمْ أَئِمَّةً يَهْدُونَ بِأَمْرِنا وَأَوْحَيْنا إِلَيْهِمْ فِعْلَ الْخَيْراتِ وَإِقامَ الصَّلاةِ وَإِيتاءَ الزَّكاةِ وَكانُوا لَنا عابِدِينَ» — ‘and each of them We made righteous. We made them imams, guiding by Our command, and We revealed to them the performance of good deeds, the maintenance of prayer, and the giving of zakat, and they used to be Our worshippers.’ (21:72-73). He also said of them: «إِنَّا أَخْلَصْناهُمْ بِخالِصَةٍ ذِكْرَى الدَّارِ» — ‘Indeed We purified them with a quality of exclusive purity: the remembrance of the [ultimate] abode.’ (38:46).

Thus, He informed that He guided them to His straight path (صراط المستقيم) — the direct and unerring way to Allah — without qualifying that with any restriction, and that He chose them, gathered them, and purified them for Himself. They are thus the purified ones (المخلصين) — with the fathah on the lam — for Allah, the Glorified; no partner shares them with Him. They seek nothing but the truth He wills, and they do not prefer any pleasure over His pleasure, whether that other pleasure be their own or someone else’s. Allah, the Glorified, has repeatedly recounted in His speech the devil’s misleading of the children of Adam, yet He excepted the purified ones: «لَأُغْوِيَنَّهُمْ أَجْمَعِينَ إِلَّا عِبادَكَ مِنْهُمُ الْمُخْلَصِينَ» — ‘I will surely pervert them all, except Your devoted servants among them.’ (38:82-83).

The truth is that Ya‘qub only loved Yusuf and his brother for the sake of Allah, the Glorified, because of the godwariness and perfection he discerned in them, and specifically in Yusuf because of what his dream indicated—that Allah would choose him, teach him the interpretation of dreams, and complete His favor upon him and upon the House of Ya‘qub. His love was not a whim at all.

Among them: what some have mentioned—that their meaning in saying «إِنَّ أَبانا لَفِي ضَلالٍ مُبِينٍ» (Our father is indeed in manifest error) was error in religion. You have already seen that the context of the noble verses refutes this.

Opposing this view is the view of others: that Yusuf’s brothers were prophets, and they only attributed error to their father in his conduct and his deviation from justice and rectitude regarding them. If they are challenged regarding the sin and injustice they committed against their brother and father, they answer that it was a minor sin (معصية صغيرة) committed before their prophethood, or that it is acceptable based on the permissibility of minor sins being committed by prophets before prophethood. Sometimes it is answered that they might have been young adolescents at the time of the sin, and it is permissible for such things to occur from adolescent children. These are refuted delusions. The saying of the Exalted: «وَأَوْحَيْنا إِلى إِبْراهِيمَ وَإِسْماعِيلَ وَإِسْحاقَ وَيَعْقُوبَ وَالْأَسْباطِ» — ‘and We revealed to Ibrahim, Isma‘il, Ishaq, Ya‘qub, and the Tribes’ (4:163), which appears to indicate the prophethood of the Tribes (الأسباط), is not explicit regarding Yusuf’s brothers.

The truth is that Yusuf’s brothers were not prophets, but rather the sons of a prophet who envied Yusuf and sinned by oppressing Yusuf the Truthful (الصديق), then repented to their Lord and reformed. Ya‘qub and Yusuf (peace be upon them) sought forgiveness for them, as Allah recounted from their father: «سَوْفَ أَسْتَغْفِرُ لَكُمْ رَبِّي» — ‘I shall soon plead with my Lord to forgive you.’ (12:98), after their saying: «يا أَبانَا اسْتَغْفِرْ لَنا ذُنُوبَنا إِنَّا كُنَّا خاطِئِينَ» — ‘Father! Plead [with Allah] for the forgiveness of our sins. We have indeed been erring.’ (12:97). And from Yusuf: «يَغْفِرُ اللهُ لَكُمْ وَهُوَ أَرْحَمُ الرَّاحِمِينَ» — ‘May Allah forgive you, and He is the most merciful of the merciful.’ (12:92), after their admission to him: «وَإِنْ كُنَّا لَخاطِئِينَ» (‘and we have indeed been erring’).

Among them: the saying of some that Yusuf’s brothers only envied him after he recounted his dream to them, and Ya‘qub had forbidden him from recounting his dream to his brothers. The truth is that the dream only caused an increase in their envy, which had already attached to them before that, as previously explained.

His saying, the Exalted: «اقْتُلُوا يُوسُفَ أَوِ اطْرَحُوهُ أَرْضاً يَخْلُ لَكُمْ وَجْهُ أَبِيكُمْ وَتَكُونُوا مِنْ بَعْدِهِ قَوْماً صالِحِينَ» — ‘Kill Yusuf or cast him into some land, so that your father’s attention may be exclusively yours, and you may be righteous people after him.’ (12:9). This is the completion of the speech of Yusuf’s brothers. The verse contains the second chapter of their conspiracy in the meeting they held regarding Yusuf’s matter to draw up a plan that would relieve their souls of him, as mentioned by the Exalted: «وَما كُنْتَ لَدَيْهِمْ إِذْ أَجْمَعُوا أَمْرَهُمْ وَهُمْ يَمْكُرُونَ» — ‘You were not with them when they conspired together and were plotting.’ (12:102).

Allah, the Glorified, has mentioned the text of their conspiracy in these three verses: «قالُوا لَيُوسُفُ وَأَخُوهُ أَحَبُّ إِلى أَبِينا مِنَّا وَنَحْنُ عُصْبَةٌ» (When they said, ‘Surely Yusuf and his brother are dearer to our father than we are, though we are a [strong] group...’) until His saying: «إِنْ كُنْتُمْ فاعِلِينَ» (‘...if you are to do [anything].’).

First, they presented the mention of their affliction regarding Yusuf and his brother, as the two had diverted Ya‘qub’s attention from them to themselves and drawn his soul toward them away from the other children. He became attached to them and disregarded the others, regardless of what they did. This was a present trial for them, threatening them with a great danger in the future, involving the loss of their status, the failure of their efforts, and their humiliation after might and weakness after strength. This was a deviation by Ya‘qub in his conduct and method.

Second, they discussed the way to escape this calamity by each of them proposing the plan he had prepared and the opinion he held. Some suggested the necessity of killing Yusuf, while others suggested casting him into a distant land from which he could not return to his father or rejoin his family. Thus, his name would be forgotten and his trace erased, so that their father’s attention would be exclusively theirs, and his love and gifts would be spread among them.

Then they agreed on something close to the second opinion, which was to cast him into the depths of a well so that some caravan would pick him up and take him to some remote, distant land. Thus, news of him would be cut off and his trace obliterated.

So His saying, the Exalted, «اقْتُلُوا يُوسُفَ» (Kill Yusuf) is a narration of one of the two opinions among them regarding him. Their mention of Yusuf alone—whereas they had mentioned Yusuf and his brother together at the start of their conspiratorial talk: «لَيُوسُفُ وَأَخُوهُ أَحَبُّ إِلى أَبِينا مِنَّا» (Surely Yusuf and his brother are dearer to our father than we are)—is evidence that he was singled out for an excess of Ya‘qub’s love and the height of his care and attention, even if his brother was also favored with love and honor among them. And how could it be otherwise? Yusuf was the one who saw the dream and was given glad tidings of the most special divine favors and unseen honors. He was the elder of the two, and the danger perceived from him was closer than that from his brother. Perhaps the mention of the two brothers together alludes to Ya‘qub’s love for their mother, which naturally led to his love for them, thereby stirring the brothers’ envy, rage, and resentment toward them.

His saying, «أَوِ اطْرَحُوهُ أَرْضاً» (or cast him into some land) is a narration of their second opinion regarding him. The meaning is: make him stay or exile him in a land from which he cannot return to his father’s house, so he becomes like one killed whose trace is cut off and from whose danger one is relieved—such as casting him into a well or exiling him to a remote place and the like.

The evidence for this is the indefiniteness of "land" (أرضاً) and the word "casting" (الطرح), which is used for throwing away goods or furniture that one no longer needs and does not benefit from, out of disregard.

The attribution of the two opinions to them with the disjunctive "or" (أو) is evidence that the sum of the two opinions was acceptable to most of the brothers until one of them said: «لا تَقْتُلُوا يُوسُفَ» (‘Do not kill Yusuf...’).

His saying, «يَخْلُ لَكُمْ وَجْهُ أَبِيكُمْ» (so that your father’s attention may be exclusively yours) means: do one of the two things to him so that your father’s face becomes empty for you. This is a metaphor for his love being purely for them by the removal of the obstacle that draws love and affection to itself. It is as if they and Yusuf, when gathered with their father, Yusuf stood between him and them and diverted his face to himself; so when he is removed, their father’s face becomes clear for them, his love becomes exclusive to them, and his attention is restricted to them.

His saying, «وَتَكُونُوا مِنْ بَعْدِهِ قَوْماً صالِحِينَ» (and you may be righteous people after him) means: and you may be, after Yusuf—or after his killing or exile, the result being the same—righteous people by repenting from this sin.

In this is evidence that they saw it as a sin and an offense, and they respected and sanctified the matter of religion. However, envy overcame them and their souls enticed them to commit the sin and perpetrate the injustice. They secured themselves from the punishment of the sin by suggesting a way that would allow them to commit it without the necessity of divine punishment: that they commit the sin and then repent.

This is part of ignorance, for the repentance (التوبة) — the act of returning to Allah with remorse and a commitment to reform — that is of this nature is not accepted at all. For one who prepares himself beforehand for sin and then repentance from it does not truly intend by his repentance to return to Allah and submit to His station; rather, he only intends to plot against his Lord in repelling the punishment and penalty He promised for violating His command or prohibition. Thus, his repentance is a tail to what he prepared himself for initially: to sin and then repent. It is, in reality, a completion of what he first intended of the type of sin—namely, the sin followed by repentance—and is not a return to his Lord with remorse for what he did. The discussion on the meaning of repentance has already preceded in the commentary on His saying, the Exalted: «إِنَّمَا التَّوْبَةُ عَلَى اللهِ لِلَّذِينَ يَعْمَلُونَ السُّوءَ بِجَهالَةٍ» — ‘Acceptance of repentance by Allah is only for those who commit evil out of ignorance...’ (4:17) in the fourth volume of this book.

It is said that "righteousness" (الصلاح) in the verse refers to the rectitude of affairs in terms of the happiness of worldly life and the order of matters therein. The meaning would be: and you may be after him people whose affairs with your father are rectified.

His saying, the Exalted: «قالَ قائِلٌ مِنْهُمْ لا تَقْتُلُوا يُوسُفَ وَأَلْقُوهُ فِي غَيابَتِ الْجُبِّ يَلْتَقِطْهُ بَعْضُ السَّيَّارَةِ إِنْ كُنْتُمْ فاعِلِينَ» — One of them said, ‘Do not kill Yusuf, but cast him into the recess of the well; some caravan will pick him up, if you are to do [anything].’ (12:10). The jubb (الجب) is a well whose interior has not been lined with stones; if it is lined, it is called a tawiyy (طوي). The ghayabah (الغيابة), with a fathah on the ghayn, is a sunken part of the earth that hides what is in it from view; the ghayabat al-jubb is its bottom, which cannot be seen because of the darkness within it.

This speaker chose the second opinion mentioned in the previous verse, alluded to by the saying: «أَوِ اطْرَحُوهُ أَرْضاً» (or cast him into some land), except that he qualified it with that which would secure him from death or any other matter leading to his destruction—as if he were cast into a well and left there until he died of hunger or the like. What he proposed as an opinion involved the exile of Yusuf from the land without causing his destruction through killing, death, or a similar harm, which would be the destruction of a kinsman. This was to be done by casting him into one of the wells on the path of travelers so that they would come upon him when drawing water, take him, and travel with him to distant lands, obliterating his trace and cutting off news of him. The context bears witness that they accepted this opinion, as no rejection from them is mentioned regarding it, and their action followed it as mentioned in the subsequent verses.

Commentators have differed regarding the name of this speaker, after being certain that he was one of his brothers because of His saying, the Exalted: «قالَ قائِلٌ مِنْهُمْ» (One of them said). It is said he was Reuben, the son of Yusuf’s maternal aunt; it is said he was Judah, who was the eldest and most sensible of them; and it is said he was Levi. We are not concerned with investigating this after the Quran remained silent on identifying him by name, as no significant benefit follows from it.

Some have mentioned that the definition of "the well" (الجب) with the definite article indicates it was a well known among them. This is good if the article is not for the genus. They also differed as to where this well was; there are various opinions, none of which yield much benefit.

His saying, the Exalted: «قالُوا يا أَبانا ما لَكَ لا تَأْمَنَّا عَلى يُوسُفَ وَإِنَّا لَهُ لَناصِحُونَ» — They said, ‘Father! What is the matter with you that you do not trust us with Yusuf? We are indeed his well-wishers.’ (12:11). The origin of «لا تَأْمَنَّا» is la ta’mannuna, then it was elided through major assimilation (الإدغام الكبير).

The verse indicates that the brothers agreed on the speaker’s word: "Do not kill Yusuf, but cast him into the recess of the well," and they agreed to plot against their father to take Yusuf and do with him what they had resolved. Their father did not trust them with Yusuf and would not leave him with them. Therefore, it was necessary first to vindicate themselves before their father and clear his heart of the cloud of doubt and suspicion so they could take him and go with him. For this reason, they came to their father and addressed him saying: «يا أَبانا» (‘Father!’) — which stirs affection and mercy and prioritizes love — «ما لَكَ لا تَأْمَنَّا عَلى يُوسُفَ وَإِنَّا لَهُ لَناصِحُونَ» (‘What is the matter with you that you do not trust us with Yusuf?
