We are indeed his well-wishers.’), meaning: while the case is that we intend nothing but good for him and seek only what pleases and delights him.

Then they asked him for what they wanted, which was for him to send him with them to their pasture where they used to take their livestock and sheep, so he could graze and play there, while they would be his guardians. So they said: «أَرْسِلْهُ مَعَنا» (‘Send him with us...’).

His saying, the Exalted: «أَرْسِلْهُ مَعَنا غَداً يَرْتَعْ وَيَلْعَبْ وَإِنَّا لَهُ لَحافِظُونَ» — ‘Send him with us tomorrow that he may graze and play, and we will indeed take care of him.’ (12:12). Rat‘ (الرتع) is the expansion of an animal in grazing and a human in recreation and eating fruits and the like.

Their saying «أَرْسِلْهُ مَعَنا غَداً يَرْتَعْ وَيَلْعَبْ» (‘Send him with us tomorrow that he may graze and play’) is the proposal of their request, as previously indicated.

Their saying: «وَإِنَّا لَهُ لَحافِظُونَ» (‘and we will indeed take care of him’) was emphasized with various modes of emphasis: inna (إن), the lam (اللام), and the nominal sentence, on the pattern of their saying: «وَإِنَّا لَهُ لَناصِحُونَ» (‘We are indeed his well-wishers’). This indicates that each of the two sentences contains a type of reassurance for their father’s soul. It is as if they said: "What is the matter with you that you do not trust us with Yusuf? If you fear for him from us, the group of brothers, that we might intend evil toward him, then we are his well-wishers. And if you fear for him from others besides us—that some harm might befall him or someone might intend evil toward him while we are negligent in his protection and careless in his guardianship—then we are indeed his guardians."

Thus, the speech is conducted in its natural order: they first mentioned that he is in safety from their side always, then they asked that he send him with them tomorrow morning, then they mentioned that they would be his guardians as long as he was with them. From this, it appears that their saying: «وَإِنَّا لَهُ لَناصِحُونَ» (‘We are indeed his well-wishers’) is a permanent assurance for him from their own side, and their saying: «وَإِنَّا لَهُ لَحافِظُونَ» (‘and we will indeed take care of him’) is a temporary assurance for him from others.

His saying, the Exalted: «قالَ إِنِّي لَيَحْزُنُنِي أَنْ تَذْهَبُوا بِهِ وَأَخافُ أَنْ يَأْكُلَهُ الذِّئْبُ وَأَنْتُمْ عَنْهُ غافِلُونَ» — He said, ‘It really unduly grieves me that you should take him away, and I fear lest the wolf should eat him while you are oblivious of him.’ (12:13). This is what their father mentioned in response to what they asked. He did not explicitly deny that he did not trust them with him; rather, he mentioned the psychological state that would seize him if they took him away, saying with emphasis: «إِنِّي لَيَحْزُنُنِي أَنْ تَذْهَبُوا بِهِ» (‘It really unduly grieves me that you should take him away’). He revealed the obstacle to be his own self, which is grieved by their taking him away—not their taking him away itself being the cause of his grief—out of kindness in his response to them and so as not to stir their defiance and stubbornness. This is one of the subtle points of the discourse.

He excused himself to them by saying: «وَأَخافُ أَنْ يَأْكُلَهُ الذِّئْبُ وَأَنْتُمْ عَنْهُ غافِلُونَ» (‘and I fear lest the wolf should eat him while you are oblivious of him’). This is a justified excuse, for the deserts with pastures where livestock dwell and sheep graze are naturally not free of wolves or predatory beasts that frequent them and lie in wait for prey. It is possible that they might attend to some of their business and become oblivious of him, and the wolf would eat him.

His saying, the Exalted: «قالُوا لَئِنْ أَكَلَهُ الذِّئْبُ وَنَحْنُ عُصْبَةٌ إِنَّا إِذاً لَخاسِرُونَ» — They said, ‘Should the wolf eat him while we are a [strong] group, we will indeed be losers.’ (12:14). They feigned ignorance to their father, as if they did not understand except that he trusted them with him but feared the wolf might eat him during a moment of their oblivion. So they responded with the response of one who denies and finds it strange. They mentioned, to reassure him, that they were a group of strong, mutually supportive men of valor and strength. They swore by Allah that if the wolf were to eat him while they were such a group, it would dictate their loss, and they would never be losers. They only swore—as indicated by the lam of the oath—to reassure him and remove his grief so he would not prevent them from taking him. This is common in speech. In the speech is an implicit promise from them that they would not be oblivious. Yet they did not wait even a day before they belied themselves in what they swore to him and broke what they promised him, when they said: «يا أَبانا إِنَّا ذَهَبْنا نَسْتَبِقُ وَتَرَكْنا يُوسُفَ عِنْدَ مَتاعِنا فَأَكَلَهُ الذِّئْبُ» (‘Father! We went racing and left Yusuf with our belongings, whereat the wolf ate him.’).

His saying, the Exalted: «فَلَمَّا ذَهَبُوا بِهِ وَأَجْمَعُوا أَنْ يَجْعَلُوهُ فِي غَيابَتِ الْجُبِ» — So when they took him away and conspired to cast him into the recess of the well... (12:15). Al-Raghib said: "I conspired (ajma‘tu) upon such-and-such" is mostly said regarding that which is a collective decision reached through thought, like: «فَأَجْمِعُوا أَمْرَكُمْ وَشُرَكاءَكُمْ» (‘so conspire together, you and your partners’). He said: It is said "the Muslims conspired (ajma‘a) upon such-and-such" when their opinions agreed upon it. End quote.

In al-Majma‘: "They conspired" (ajma‘u) means they all resolved to cast him into the recess of the well, and their motives agreed upon it. For one who is called by a single motive to a thing is not said to have "conspired" upon it; so it is as if it is derived from the gathering of motives. End quote.

The verse suggests that they convinced their father with what they said to him and satisfied him so that he would not prevent them from taking Yusuf out with them to the desert. So they carried him with them to execute what they had resolved: casting him into the recess of the well.

The apodosis (جواب) for "when" (lamma) is omitted to indicate the tragedy and horror of the matter. This is a common literary device in speech: you see the speaker describing a horrific matter, like a tragic killing that burns the heart and which the ear cannot bear, so he begins by explaining its causes and the conditions leading to it. He proceeds in his description until he reaches the event itself, then he falls into a deep silence, then describes the events after the killing. By this, he indicates that the description of the killing reached such a level of tragedy that the speaker cannot articulate it and the listener cannot bear to hear it.

It is as if the One describing the story—exalted is His Name—when He said: «فَلَمَّا ذَهَبُوا بِهِ وَأَجْمَعُوا أَنْ يَجْعَلُوهُ فِي غَيابَتِ الْجُبِ» (So when they took him away and conspired to cast him into the recess of the well...), He fell silent for a while and withheld the mention of what they did to him out of sorrow and regret, because the ear cannot bear to perceive what they did to this innocent, oppressed child, the prophet and son of prophets, who had committed no crime to deserve anything of what they perpetrated against him—and they were his brothers, and they knew the extent of his father’s love for him, the noble prophet Ya‘qub. May Allah destroy envy! It destroys a brother like Yusuf the Truthful at the hands of his brothers, bereaves a noble father like Ya‘qub at the hands of his sons, and beautifies such a hideous transgression in the eyes of men raised in the lap of prophethood and grown in the house of prophets.

When the purpose was achieved by the silence regarding the apodosis of lamma, Allah, the Glorified, proceeded with the rest of the story, saying: «وَأَوْحَيْنا إِلَيْهِ» (and We revealed to him...).

His saying, the Exalted: «وَأَوْحَيْنا إِلَيْهِ لَتُنَبِّئَنَّهُمْ بِأَمْرِهِمْ هذا وَهُمْ لا يَشْعُرُونَ» — and We revealed to him, ‘You will surely inform them of this matter of theirs while they are not aware.’ (12:15). The pronoun refers to Yusuf. The manifest meaning of "revelation" (الوحي) is that it is the revelation of prophethood. The "matter of theirs" refers to their casting him into the recess of the well. Likewise, it is manifest that the phrase «وَهُمْ لا يَشْعُرُونَ» (while they are not aware) is a circumstantial clause (حال) related to the revelation indicated by His saying: «وَأَوْحَيْنا» (and We revealed). The object of "they are not aware" is the "matter"—meaning they are not aware of the reality of this matter of theirs—or the "revelation"—meaning they are not aware of what We revealed to him.

The meaning—and Allah knows best—is: We revealed to Yusuf, swearing: You shall surely inform them of the reality of this matter of theirs and the interpretation of what they did to you. For they see it as an exile of your person, an obliteration of your name, an extinguishing of your light, a humiliation of you, and a lowering of your status; whereas it is, in reality, a bringing of you closer to the seat of might and the throne of the kingdom, a revival of your remembrance, a completion of your light, and a raising of your status. They are not aware of this reality, and you will surely inform them of that. This is his saying to them when he reclined upon the seat of might while they stood before him seeking mercy, saying: «يا أَيُّهَا الْعَزِيزُ مَسَّنا وَأَهْلَنَا الضُّرُّ وَجِئْنا بِبِضاعَةٍ مُزْجاةٍ فَأَوْفِ لَنَا الْكَيْلَ وَتَصَدَّقْ عَلَيْنا إِنَّ اللهَ يَجْزِي الْمُتَصَدِّقِينَ» — ‘O ‘Aziz! Distress has befallen us and our family, and we have brought a paltry sum. So give us full measure and be charitable to us. Indeed Allah rewards the charitable.’ (12:88). At that point, he said: «هَلْ عَلِمْتُمْ ما فَعَلْتُمْ بِيُوسُفَ وَأَخِيهِ إِذْ أَنْتُمْ جاهِلُونَ» — ‘Have you realized what you did to Yusuf and his brother, when you were senseless?’ (12:89) until he said: «أَنَا يُوسُفُ وَهذا أَخِي قَدْ مَنَّ اللهُ عَلَيْنا» — ‘I am Yusuf, and this is my brother. Allah has certainly been gracious to us.’ (12:90).

Look at the place of his saying: «هَلْ عَلِمْتُمْ» (‘Have you realized...’), for it is an indication that this state you witness today is the reality of what you did to Yusuf. And his saying: «إِذْ أَنْتُمْ جاهِلُونَ» (‘when you were senseless’) corresponds in the verse we are currently discussing to His saying: «وَهُمْ لا يَشْعُرُونَ» (while they are not aware).

Other views have been mentioned regarding the meaning of the verse:

Among them: that you will inform your brothers of what they did to you at a time when they do not recognize you. This is what he informed them of in Egypt while they did not recognize him, then he made himself known to them.

Among them: that the intended meaning of his informing them is his recompensing them for the evil of what they did, like one who threatens someone who has mistreated him, saying: "I will surely inform you and make you know."

Among them: the saying of some, as narrated from Ibn ‘Abbas, that the intended meaning of his informing them of their matter is what occurred between him and his brothers in Egypt, where he saw them and recognized them while they were unaware of him. He took a cup and struck it, and it made a sound, so he said: "This cup informs me that you had a brother from your father whom you cast into the well and sold for a paltry price."

These views are not free of weakness. The correct view is what we have presented. This term has frequently appeared in His speech, the Exalted, in the sense of explaining the reality of an action, such as His saying, the Exalted: «إِلَى اللهِ مَرْجِعُكُمْ جَمِيعاً فَيُنَبِّئُكُمْ بِما كُنْتُمْ تَعْمَلُونَ» — ‘To Allah is the return of you all [together], then He will inform you of what you used to do.’ (5:105), and His saying: «وَسَوْفَ يُنَبِّئُهُمُ اللهُ بِما كانُوا يَصْنَعُونَ» — ‘and soon Allah will inform them of what they used to do.’ (5:14), and His saying: «يَوْمَ يَبْعَثُهُمُ اللهُ جَمِيعاً فَيُنَبِّئُهُمْ بِما عَمِلُوا» — ‘On the day when Allah will raise them all together, He will inform them of what they did.’ (58:6), among many other verses.

Among them: the saying of some that the meaning is: We revealed to him that you will inform them of what they did to you, while they are not aware of this revelation. This view is not far-fetched, but the issue lies in explaining the subtle point for qualifying the speech with this restriction, and there is no apparent need for it.

Among them: the saying of some that the meaning of the verse is: You will surely inform them of the elevation of your life, your might, and your kingdom by this matter of theirs, as Allah will make you prevail over them, humiliate them before you, and make your dream true, while they are not aware on that day of what Allah has given you.

The main difference between this view and the one we presented is that this view shifts the "informing" (الإنباء) from verbal informing to informing through the external state and the actual situation. There is no justification for this after He, the Glorified, recounted his saying: «هَلْ عَلِمْتُمْ ما فَعَلْتُمْ بِيُوسُفَ» (‘Have you realized what you did to Yusuf...’).

His saying, the Exalted: «وَجاؤُ أَباهُمْ عِشاءً يَبْكُونَ» — In the evening they came to their father, weeping. (12:16). ‘Isha’ (العشاء) is the end of the day; it is said to be from the time of the Maghrib prayer until the darkness of night. They were weeping only to disguise the matter to their father so that he would believe them in what they said and not belie them.

His saying, the Exalted: «قالُوا يا أَبانا إِنَّا ذَهَبْنا نَسْتَبِقُ وَتَرَكْنا يُوسُفَ عِنْدَ مَتاعِنا فَأَكَلَهُ الذِّئْبُ» — They said, ‘Father! We went racing and left Yusuf with our belongings, whereat the wolf ate him.’ (12:17) until the end of the verse. Al-Raghib said in al-Mufradat: The origin of sabq (السبق) is to precede in travel, as in: «فالسابقات سبقاً» (‘and those who take the lead, leading’). Istibaq (الاستباق) is to race one another (al-tasabuq). He said: «إِنَّا ذَهَبْنا نَسْتَبِقُ» (‘We went racing’) and «وَاسْتَبَقَا الْبابَ» (‘They raced for the door’). End quote. Al-Zamakhshari said in al-Kashshaf: Nastabiq means we race one another. The forms ifta‘ala and tafa‘ala share the same meaning, like intidal and tanadul (competing in archery), irtima’ and tarami (throwing at one another), and others. The meaning is: we race one another in running or in archery. End quote.

The author of al-Manar said in his commentary: «إِنَّا ذَهَبْنا نَسْتَبِقُ» (‘We went racing’) means we went from our gathering place to the race, each of us striving to precede the other. Thus, istibaq is the striving to precede, which is the purpose of musabaqah and tasabuq in the reciprocal forms intended for prevailing. It may be intended for its own sake or for another purpose in preceding. From this is: «فَاسْتَبِقُوا الْخَيْراتِ» (‘so vie with one another in good works’); this is intended for the sake of preceding itself, not for prevailing. And the upcoming saying in this Surah: «وَاسْتَبَقَا الْبابَ» (‘They raced for the door’) — Yusuf intended to exit the house fleeing, while the wife of the ‘Aziz intended to follow him to bring him back. The reciprocal form does not convey this meaning. Al-Zamakhshari, the master of language, and those who followed him, did not notice this subtle difference. End quote.

I say: The example he gave of His saying, the Exalted: «فَاسْتَبِقُوا الْخَيْراتِ» (‘so vie with one another in good works’) is among the instances of prevailing. For it is religiously recommended that a person not prefer another over himself in good deeds, rewards, and acts of proximity, and that he precede those below him in acquiring blessings. Thus, istibaq necessarily coincides with tasabuq. Likewise, His saying, the Exalted: «وَاسْتَبَقَا الْبابَ» (‘They raced for the door’), for the meaning is certainly that each of them wanted to precede the other to the door—he to open it and she to prevent him from opening it—which is the meaning of tasabuq. Thus, the truth is that the meanings of istibaq and tasabuq are identical in their application to the case. In al-Sihah: "I raced him (sabaqtuhu) and I preceded him (sabaqtuhu) in a race (sabqan), and we raced (istabaqna) in running, meaning we competed (tasabaqna)." End quote. In Lisan al-‘Arab: "I raced him and preceded him, and we raced (istabaqna) in running, meaning we competed (tasabaqna)." End quote.

Perhaps the reason for the coincidence of istabaqa and tasabaqa is that the concept of "preceding" (sabq) is itself a relative meaning. The weight ifta‘ala conveys the intensification of the meaning of the verb, the subject’s deep engagement in his action, and his taking it as a quality for himself, as observed in kasaba and iktasaba, hamala and ihtamala, sabara and istabara, qaruba and iqtaraba, khafiya and ikhtafa, jahada and ijtahada, and their likes. The occurrence of this characteristic upon the meaning of "preceding," given its relativity, conveys the subject’s effort to reserve the act of preceding for himself, which only occurs with a competition (tasabuq) in the matter.

His saying: «بِمُؤْمِنٍ لَنا» (‘a believer in us’) means a confirmer of our word. "Faith" (al-iman) is made transitive with the lam just as it is with the ba’. The Exalted said: «فَآمَنَ لَهُ لُوطٌ» — ‘So Lut believed in him.’ (29:26).

The meaning is that when they came to their father in the evening weeping, they said to their father: "Father! We, the group of brothers, went to the desert to race one another in running or archery—and perhaps it was in running, for that is more effective in distancing them from their camp and belongings—and Yusuf was with us, according to what they mentioned. We left Yusuf with our gear and belongings, and the wolf ate him. It is part of our disappointment and misery that you are not one to believe us in what we say and report, even if we were truthful in it."

Their saying: «وَما أَنْتَ بِمُؤْمِنٍ لَنا وَلَوْ كُنَّا صادِقِينَ» (‘But you will not believe us even if we were truthful.’) is the kind of speech used by one who offers an excuse when he is cut off from means and the paths of stratagem are closed to him. It indicates that his speech is not justified in the eyes of the one to whom he excuses himself, and his excuse is not heard, and he knows that; yet he is nevertheless compelled to report the truth and reveal the sincerity, even if he is not believed in it. Thus, it is a metaphor for truthfulness in speech.

His saying, the Exalted: «وَجاؤُ عَلى قَمِيصِهِ بِدَمٍ كَذِبٍ» — And they brought his shirt with false blood upon it. (12:18). Kadhib (الكذب), with a fathah then a kasrah, is a verbal noun used as an adjective for emphasis, meaning "with blood that is a lie," whose falsehood is manifest.

In the verse is an indication that the shirt, with blood upon it—and "blood" was made indefinite to indicate the weakness and insignificance of its evidence for what they described—was in a state that revealed their lie in their speech. For one who is preyed upon and eaten by predatory beasts would not have a shirt left whole and untattered. This is the nature of a lie: a false report or a false tale is never free of inconsistency among its parts and contradiction between its sides, or evidences from external conditions and states that surround it, calling out with the truth and revealing the veil from the ugliness of its inner reality and essence, even if its outward form appears good.

(A Discourse on the Fact that Falsehood Does Not Prosper)

It is a matter of experience that a lie does not endure in its credibility, and the liar does not wait long before he produces that which belies him or manifests that which reveals the veil from the vanity of what he reported or claimed. The reason for this is that the cosmos (الكون) proceeds according to a system in which its parts are linked to one another by relations and additions that are neither changing nor shifting. For every external event that occurs, there are corresponding necessary consequences (لوازم) and antecedents (ملزومات) that are inseparable from one another. They all have, among themselves, laws and effects that are connected to one another. If one of them were disrupted, all would be disrupted, and the soundness of one indicates the soundness of the chain. This is a universal law (قانون كلي) not subject to exception.

If, for example, a body moves from one place to another in a certain time, it is among its necessary consequences that it leaves the first place, distances itself from it, disappears from it and from everything that accompanies and connects to it, that the first place becomes empty of it, and the second becomes occupied by it, and that it traverses the interval between them, among other consequences. If one of these were disrupted—such as its being, in the supposed time, still occupying the first place—all the consequences surrounding it would be disrupted.

It is not within the power of a human being, nor any supposed cause, if they hide one of the cosmic realities with some kind of deception, to hide all the necessary consequences and antecedents linked to it, or to remove them from their actual places, or to divert them from their cosmic course. For if a veil is cast over one of them, another will appear, and if not, then a third, and so on.

From here, the final state (الدولة) belongs to the truth, even if falsehood has a temporary turn (جولة), and value belongs to sincerity, even if desire sometimes attaches to the lie. The Exalted said: «إِنَّ اللهَ لا يَهْدِي مَنْ هُوَ كاذِبٌ كَفَّارٌ» — ‘Indeed Allah does not guide someone who is a liar and an ingrate.’ (39:3). And He said: «إِنَّ اللهَ لا يَهْدِي مَنْ هُوَ مُسْرِفٌ كَذَّابٌ» — ‘Indeed Allah does not guide someone who is a prodigal and a liar.’ (40:28). And He said: «إِنَّ الَّذِينَ يَفْتَرُونَ عَلَى اللهِ الْكَذِبَ لا يُفْلِحُونَ» — ‘Indeed those who fabricate lies against Allah will not prosper.’ (16:116). And He said: «بَلْ كَذَّبُوا بِالْحَقِّ لَمَّا جاءَهُمْ فَهُمْ فِي أَمْرٍ مَرِيجٍ» — ‘No, they denied the truth when it came to them, so they are in a confused state.’ (50:5). That is because when they considered the truth a lie, they built upon vanity and relied upon it in their lives, so they fell into a disrupted system where some parts contradict others and one side repels the other.

His saying, the Exalted: «قالَ بَلْ سَوَّلَتْ لَكُمْ أَنْفُسُكُمْ أَمْراً فَصَبْرٌ جَمِيلٌ وَاللهُ الْمُسْتَعانُ عَلى ما تَصِفُونَ» — He said, ‘No, your souls have glosed over something for you. So [my recourse is] a worthy patience, and it is Allah whose help is to be sought against what you describe.’ (12:18). This is Ya‘qub’s response when he was suddenly confronted with the news of the death of his son and beloved Yusuf. They entered upon him without Yusuf, weeping, informing him that the wolf had eaten Yusuf, and this was his shirt stained with blood. He already knew the extent of their envy for him, and they had snatched him from his hand with insistence and persistence, and brought his shirt with false blood upon it that cried out their lie in what they said and reported.

He turned away from their saying: إِنَّا ذَهَبْنَا نَسْتَبِقُ "We went racing one another" etc., with his saying: بَلْ سَوَّلَتْ لَكُمْ أَنْفُسُكُمْ أَمْرًا "Rather your souls have made up a matter for you." Enticement (التسويل) — the act of whispering or seductive prompting — means that the matter is not as you report; rather, your souls have prompted you toward a certain matter. He left the matter vague (أبهم) and did not specify it. Then he informed them that he would be patient in this regard without holding them to account or seeking personal vengeance against them; rather, he would suppress (يكظم) what had assaulted his soul with total restraint.

His saying: بَلْ سَوَّلَتْ لَكُمْ أَنْفُسُكُمْ أَمْرًا "Rather your souls have made up a matter for you" is a rejection of what they reported regarding the affair of Joseph, and a clarification that he was aware that the loss of Joseph was not based on what they mentioned regarding the devouring by a wild beast, but rather was based on a plotted scheme and an enticement from their own souls. This speech serves as a preamble (توطئة) for what he mentioned afterward in his saying: فَصَبْرٌ جَمِيلٌ "So [my recourse is] a graceful patience" until the end of the verse.

His saying: فَصَبْرٌ جَمِيلٌ "[My recourse is] a graceful patience" is a praise of patience (الصبر). This is an instance of placing the cause in the position of the effect; the implied meaning is: "I shall be patient over what has befallen me, for patience is graceful." The use of the indefinite form (تنكير) for "patience" and the omission of its full description are intended to point toward the magnitude (فخامة) of its affair and the greatness of its status, or perhaps the bitterness of its taste and the difficulty of enduring it.

The phrase فَصَبْرٌ جَمِيلٌ "So [my recourse is] a graceful patience" was branched (فرع) from what preceded it to indicate that the circumstances surrounding him and the calamity poured upon him were such that nothing could be heard from him except that he follow the path of patience. This is because he (peace be upon him) had lost the most beloved of people to him, Joseph, and here they were mentioning to him that he had become food for a wolf, while this was his shirt stained with blood. He saw that they were lying in what they reported, and he saw that they had a hand in his disappearance and a plot in his affair. Yet, he had no way to investigate what had happened to Joseph, nor to search for what his affair had led to—where was he? and what was his state? For his only helpers in such calamities and his only supports to repel the misfortunes intended against him were his sons, and they were a strong and powerful band (عصبة). If they themselves were the causes for the descent of the calamity and the occurrence of the misfortune, then against whom could he strike? And with what could he repel them from himself? Thus, nothing was possible for him except patience.

However, patience (الصبر) — the steadfast endurance of the soul in the face of adversity — is not for a human to merely bear the tragedy placed upon him and to submit absolutely to whoever intends evil for him, like dead earth trodden by feet and played with by hands. Indeed, Allah, the Glorified, has fashioned the human being with a disposition to repel harm from himself and has equipped him with that which allows him to confront calamities and tragedies as much as he can. There is no virtue in nullifying this divine instinct (الغريزة). Rather, patience is the steadfastness (الاستقامة) of the heart and the preservation of the psychological order by which the affair of human life remains upright and free from disorder. It is the regulation of the internal assembly against dispersal, dissolution, the forgetting of management, the confusion of thought, and the corruption of judgment. Thus, the patient are those who stand firm in calamities upon their feet, whom the assaults of misfortunes do not displace; others are those who flee at the first assault and then pay no heed to anything.

From here it is known that patience is an excellent path for resisting a calamity and breaking its intensity, yet it is not the complete cause for the restoration of well-being and the return of safety. It is like a fortress (الحصن) in which a person takes refuge to repel a marauding enemy. As for the return of the blessing of security, peace, and the freedom of life, it may require another cause that brings about success and victory. This cause, in the creed of monotheism (ملة التوحيد), is Allah, mighty is His authority. Therefore, it is incumbent upon the monotheistic human (الموحد), when a calamity strikes him and a misfortune descends upon him, to first fortify himself with patience so that his internal order of servitude (النظام العبودي) is not disrupted and the camp of his faculties and perceptions does not dissolve. Then, he must place his trust (يتوكل) in his Lord, Who is above every cause, hoping that He will repel the evil from him and direct his affair toward the ultimate goodness of his state. Allah, the Glorified, is master over His affair. Some of this discussion has already preceded in the commentary on His saying, the Exalted: وَاسْتَعِينُوا بِالصَّبْرِ وَالصَّلَاةِ "And take recourse in patience and prayer" (Al-Baqarah: 45) in the first volume of this book.

For all of this, when Jacob (peace be upon him) said: فَصَبْرٌ جَمِيلٌ "So [my recourse is] a graceful patience," he followed it with his saying: وَاللَّهُ الْمُسْتَعَانُ عَلَىٰ مَا تَصِفُونَ "and it is Allah whose help is sought against what you describe." Thus, he completed the word of patience with the word of trust (التوكل) — the total reliance upon Allah while delegating one's affairs to Him — similar to what he expressed in his saying in the upcoming verses: فَصَبْرٌ جَمِيلٌ ۖ عَسَى اللَّهُ أَن يَأْتِيَنِي بِهِمْ جَمِيعًا ۚ إِنَّهُ هُوَ الْعَلِيمُ الْحَكِيمُ "So [my recourse is] a graceful patience. Maybe Allah will bring them all back to me. Indeed He is the All-knowing, the All-wise" (Verse 83 of the Surah).

His saying: وَاللَّهُ الْمُسْتَعَانُ عَلَىٰ مَا تَصِفُونَ "and it is Allah whose help is sought against what you describe"—which is among the most wondrous of speech—is an exposition of his trust in his Lord. He says: "I know that you have a plot in this matter and that a wolf did not eat Joseph, but I do not rely, in uncovering your lie and obtaining Joseph, upon the apparent causes (الأسباب الظاهرة) which avail nothing without permission from Allah, nor do I struggle vainly among them. Rather, I shall maintain the steadfastness of my soul through patience and entrust my Lord to manifest the truth against what you describe—that Joseph has met his end and become food for a wolf."

Thus, it becomes clear that his saying: وَاللَّهُ الْمُسْتَعَانُ عَلَىٰ مَا تَصِفُونَ "and it is Allah whose help is sought against what you describe" is a supplication (دعاء) from the station of trust (التوكل). Its meaning is: "O Allah, I have placed my trust in You in this affair of mine, so be a help for me against what these sons of mine describe." The phrase is built upon the monotheism of action (توحيد الفعل), for it is phrased in the manner of exclusivity (الحصر); its meaning is that Allah, the Glorified, is the only one whose help is sought—I have no helper other than Him. For he (peace be upon him) saw that there is no true judgment except the judgment of Allah, as he will say in his future speech: إِنِ الْحُكْمُ إِلَّا لِلَّهِ ۖ عَلَيْهِ تَوَكَّلْتُ "Judgment belongs only to Allah. In Him I have put my trust" (Verse 67). To complete this monotheism with that which is higher than it, he did not mention himself; he did not say, "I will be patient," nor did he say, "And Allah I seek help from against what you describe." Rather, he left himself out and mentioned the name of his Lord, indicating that the matter is tied to His true judgment. This is part of the perfection of his monotheism. He was immersed in his grief, regret, and sorrow for Joseph, yet he did not love Joseph, nor was he infatuated with him, nor did he find the pain of his loss except for Allah and in Allah.

His saying, the Exalted: وَجَاءَتْ سَيَّارَةٌ فَأَرْسَلُوا وَارِدَهُمْ فَأَدْلَىٰ دَلْوَهُ ۖ قَالَ يَا بُشْرَىٰ هَٰذَا غُلَامٌ ۚ وَأَسَرُّوهُ بِضَاعَةً ۚ وَاللَّهُ عَلِيمٌ بِمَا يَعْمَلُونَ And there came a caravan, and they sent their water-drawer. He let down his bucket. He said, ‘Good news! This is a boy!’ So they hid him as a merchandise, and Allah knew best what they were doing. (Verse 19). Al-Raghib said: "The term al-wurud (الورود) originally means heading toward water, then it is used for other things." He also said: "I dalawtu (دلوت) the bucket when I sent it down, and I adlaytuha (أدليتها) when I brought it out." Others said the opposite. He also said: "Concealment (الإسرار) is the opposite of publicizing (الإعلان)."

His saying: قَالَ يَا بُشْرَىٰ هَٰذَا غُلَامٌ "He said, ‘Good news! This is a boy!’" is mentioned without a conjunction (بالفصل) even though it is a consequence of letting down the bucket, to indicate that it was an unexpected event. For what is expected from letting down a bucket is the coming out of water, not the obtaining of a boy. Thus, it was a surprise to them, which is why he said: قَالَ يَا بُشْرَىٰ "He said, ‘Good news!’" The calling out to "good news" is like the calling out to "sorrow" or "woe" and their likes, to indicate its presence and the clarity of its appearance.

His saying: وَاللَّهُ عَلِيمٌ بِمَا يَعْمَلُونَ "and Allah knew best what they were doing" implies a censure of their action and a clarification that it was a sin recorded against them for which they would be held accountable. It is also possible that the meaning is that this occurred with the knowledge of Allah, Who intended thereby for Joseph to reach the station He had decreed for him. For had he not come out of the pit and been taken as merchandise, he would not have entered the house of the Aziz in Egypt, and thus would not have been given the sovereignty and honor he was given.

The meaning of the verse is: A group of travelers passed by there, and they sent someone to seek water for them. He sent his bucket into the pit, and when he pulled it out, he surprised them by saying: "O good news, this is a boy!"—Joseph having clung to the rope and come out. So they hid him as merchandise intended for sale and trade, while Allah, the Glorified, was All-Knowing of what they were doing, holding them accountable for it; or that this was by His knowledge, and He was directing Joseph on this path so that he might settle in the abode of honor, sovereignty, and prophethood.

His saying, the Exalted: وَشَرَوْهُ بِثَمَنٍ بَخْسٍ دَرَاهِمَ مَعْدُودَةٍ وَكَانُوا فِيهِ مِنَ الزَّاهِدِينَ And they sold him for a cheap price, a few dirhams, for they set small store by him. (Verse 20). A cheap price (الثمن البخس) is that which is less than the true value. A few dirhams (دراهم معدودة) means a small amount; the reason for this—as has been said—is that when dirhams or dinars were numerous, they would weigh them, and they would only count them when they were few. What is meant by dirhams is the silver currency circulating among them at that time. Selling (الشراء) here means the act of selling (البيع), and disinterest (الزهد) is the turning away from something, or it is a metaphor for being cautious (الاتقاء).

It is apparent from the context that the two plural pronouns in His saying: وَشَرَوْهُ "they sold him" and وَكَانُوا "they were" refer to the caravan (السيارة). The meaning is that the caravan members who brought him out of the pit and hid him as merchandise sold him for a cheap, deficient price, which was a few counted dirhams, and they were cautious (يتقون) lest the reality of the situation be revealed and he be snatched from their hands.

Most commentators are of the view that the two pronouns refer to Joseph's brothers. The meaning would be that they sold Joseph to the caravan after claiming he was a slave of theirs who had fallen into the well, and they only appeared there to bring him out of the pit; so they sold him to the caravan and were cautious of the situation being revealed.

Or, that the first of the two pronouns refers to the brothers and the second to the caravan. The meaning would be that the brothers sold him for a cheap price, a few dirhams, and the caravan members were among those who were disinterested in him, showing disinterest and lack of desire so that his price would not rise, or they were truly disinterested in buying him because they sensed that the matter was not free of a plot and that the boy did not have the features of slaves.

The context of the verses does not support either of these two views. The plural pronouns in the previous verse refer to the caravan, and no explicit mention of the brothers occurs after that such that the pronoun of وَشَرَوْهُ and وَكَانُوا, or one of them, should return to them. Furthermore, the apparent meaning of His saying in the following verse: وَقَالَ الَّذِي اشْتَرَاهُ مِن مِّصْرَ "The man from Egypt who bought him said" is that he bought him from those who possessed him through this sale.

As for what has been narrated in the reports—"that Joseph's brothers appeared there and took Joseph from them claiming he was their slave who fell into the well, then sold him to them for a cheap price"—it does not override the apparent context of the verses, nor do the verses override the reports.

It has also been said: "Selling" (الشراء) in the verse means "purchasing" (الاشتراء), which is a known usage. This is similar to the two previous possibilities and is likewise dismissed by the context.

His saying, the Exalted: وَقَالَ الَّذِي اشْتَرَاهُ مِن مِّصْرَ لِامْرَأَتِهِ أَكْرِمِي مَثْوَاهُ عَسَىٰ أَن يَنفَعَنَا أَوْ نَتَّخِذَهُ وَلَدًا The man from Egypt who bought him said to his wife, ‘Give him an honorable stay. Maybe he will be of use to us, or we may adopt him as a son.’ (Verse 21). The context indicates that the caravan carried Joseph with them to Egypt and offered him for sale there; so one of the people of Egypt bought him and brought him into his house.

The verses are wondrous in their mention and identification of this buyer. He is first mentioned with His saying, the Exalted: وَقَالَ الَّذِي اشْتَرَاهُ مِن مِّصْرَ "The man from Egypt who bought him said," informing us that he was a man from the people of Egypt. Secondly, with His saying: وَأَلْفَيَا سَيِّدَهَا لَدَى الْبَابِ "and they sighted her lord at the door," identifying him as a master (سيد) who was turned to for needs. Thirdly, with His saying: وَقَالَ نِسْوَةٌ فِي الْمَدِينَةِ امْرَأَتُ الْعَزِيزِ تُرَاوِدُ فَتَاهَا عَن نَّفْسِهِ "Some women in the city said, ‘The Aziz’s wife is soliciting her slave...’" clarifying that he was the Aziz (العزيز) — the powerful and noble minister — in Egypt, to whom the people of the city conceded honor and status. Then it pointed out that he possessed a prison, which is among the affairs of authority and leadership among people. From this, it is known that Joseph was purchased on the first day for the Aziz of Egypt and entered the house of honor.

In short, the man was not identified each time in the speech of the Exalted except by the amount required by the context of the story. For the first time, there was no need for more than describing him as a man from the people of Egypt whose house was there; thus, He restricted his identification to His saying: وَقَالَ الَّذِي اشْتَرَاهُ مِن مِّصْرَ.

In any case, the verse informs, in its brevity, that the caravan carried Joseph with them, entered Egypt, and sold him to one of its people, who brought him into his house and instructed his wife, saying: أَكْرِمِي مَثْوَاهُ عَسَىٰ أَن يَنفَعَنَا أَوْ نَتَّخِذَهُ وَلَدًا "Give him an honorable stay. Maybe he will be of use to us, or we may adopt him as a son."

Prevailing custom dictates that masters and lords do not concern themselves with the affairs of their slaves unless they discern in the slave's face the signs of nobility and maturity, and witness in his features goodness and fortune. This is especially true for kings, sultans, and leaders, into whose palaces dozens and hundreds of the finest boys and girls entered at all times; they would not become infatuated with everyone they acquired, nor captivated by everyone they grew familiar with. Thus, the command of the Aziz to give him an honorable stay and the hope of benefiting from him or adopting him as a son had a deep meaning, especially since he commanded his wife and the lady of his house with this. It is not customary for queens and noble ladies to manage minor and trivial matters, nor for ladies of high status to oversee the affairs of slaves and boys.

Indeed, Joseph (peace be upon him) possessed a wondrous beauty that dazzled minds and captivated hearts. Along with the beauty of his form, he was given excellence of character—patient, dignified, graceful in movement, pleasant in speech, wise in logic, noble in soul, and of distinguished origin. These are qualities that do not grow in a person unless their roots are emerging in him during the days of his childhood and their signs are apparent in his features from the very beginning.

These were the qualities that attracted the soul of the Aziz to Joseph—while he was a small child—until he hoped that Joseph would grow up with him in his private household, becoming one of the closest people to him, to be benefited from in his important affairs and high objectives, or to enter his lineage and be a son to him and his wife through adoption, thereby becoming the heir to his house.

From here, it can be inferred that the Aziz was sterile and had no child from his wife; therefore, he hoped that he and his wife would adopt Joseph.

So His saying: وَقَالَ الَّذِي اشْتَرَاهُ مِن مِّصْرَ "The man from Egypt who bought him said"—meaning the Aziz—لِامْرَأَتِهِ "to his wife"—meaning the Aziza—أَكْرِمِي مَثْوَاهُ "Give him an honorable stay"—meaning, attend to his affair yourself and grant him a noble station with you—عَسَىٰ أَن يَنفَعَنَا "Maybe he will be of use to us" in our high objectives and important affairs, أَوْ نَتَّخِذَهُ وَلَدًا "or we may adopt him as a son" through adoption.

His saying, the Exalted: وَكَذَٰلِكَ مَكَّنَّا لِيُوسُفَ فِي الْأَرْضِ وَلِنُعَلِّمَهُ مِن تَأْوِيلِ الْأَحَادِيثِ ۚ وَاللَّهُ غَالِبٌ عَلَىٰ أَمْرِهِ وَلَٰكِنَّ أَكْثَرَ النَّاسِ لَا يَعْلَمُونَ That is how We established Joseph in the land, and that We might teach him the interpretation of dreams. Allah has full command of His affairs, but most people do not know. (Verse 21). It is said in al-Mufradat: "Place (المكان) according to the linguists is the location containing the thing." He said: "It is said: makkantuhu (I established him) and makkantu lahu (I established for him), so he became established (tamakkana)." He, the Exalted, said: وَلَقَدْ مَكَّنَّاكُمْ فِي الْأَرْضِ "We have certainly established you on the earth," وَلَقَدْ مَكَّنَّاهُمْ فِيمَا إِن مَّكَّنَّاكُمْ فِيهِ "We had established them in respects We have not established you," أَوَلَمْ نُمَكِّن لَّهُمْ "Have We not established for them," and وَنُمَكِّنَ لَهُمْ فِي الْأَرْضِ "and establish them in the land." He said: "Al-Khalil said: al-makan (the place) is a noun of place (mif'al) from al-kawn (being), and due to its frequency in speech, it was treated like the pattern fa'al, so it was said tamakkana and tamaskana like tamanzala." End quote. Thus, al-makan is the dwelling of a thing on the earth, and al-imkan (possibility) and al-tamkin (establishment) are the settling and fixing in a location. Sometimes al-makan is used for al-makanah (status) regarding the dwelling of a thing in abstract matters, such as status in knowledge or among people. It is said: "I enabled him (amkantuhu) of the thing," so he became capable (tamakkana) of it, meaning I empowered him so he became able over it; this is of the nature of a metaphor.

Perhaps what is meant by establishing Joseph in the land is his settling in it such that he is able to enjoy the advantages of life and expand therein, after his brothers had denied him settlement on the face of the earth and cast him into the depths of the pit, then sold him for a cheap price to be carried by travelers from land to land, becoming a stranger to his own land and the dwelling of his father.

The Exalted mentioned His establishing Joseph in the land during the course of his story twice. The first is after mentioning his exit from the depths of the pit, the caravan carrying him to Egypt, and his sale to the Aziz, which is His saying in this verse: وَكَذَٰلِكَ مَكَّنَّا لِيُوسُفَ فِي الْأَرْضِ "That is how We established Joseph in the land." The second is after mentioning his exit from the prison of the Aziz and his appointment over the storehouses of the land of Egypt, where He, the Exalted, said: وَكَذَٰلِكَ مَكَّنَّا لِيُوسُفَ فِي الْأَرْضِ يَتَبَوَّأُ مِنْهَا حَيْثُ يَشَاءُ "That is how We established Joseph in the land: he settled in it wherever he wished" (Verse 56 of the Surah). The intent in both places is the same.

His saying: وَكَذَٰلِكَ مَكَّنَّا لِيُوسُفَ فِي الْأَرْضِ "That is how We established Joseph in the land"—the "that" (كذلك) refers to what He mentioned of his being brought out of the pit, his sale, and his settling in the house of the Aziz. If what is meant by his establishment in the land is this specific amount of establishment that he obtained by entering the house of the Aziz and settling therein in the most pleasant of lives by the recommendation of the Aziz, then the comparison is of the nature of comparing a thing to itself to indicate the abundance of the qualities mentioned for it. This is not the blameworthy type of comparing a thing to itself, like the saying:

It is as if we, with the water around us, Are a people sitting with water around them.

Rather, the meaning is that the establishment We performed for him in the land was similar to this which We have described and informed about; it contains as many abundant qualities as what We have narrated. This is a subtlety in exposition by making a thing like itself through a claimed comparison, in order to draw the listener's mind to the abundance and importance of its qualities and the soul's attachment to them, as is the function of comparison.

Of this category is His saying, the Exalted: لَيْسَ كَمِثْلِهِ شَيْءٌ "Nothing is like Him" (Ash-Shura: 11) and His saying, the Exalted: لِمِثْلِ هَٰذَا فَلْيَعْمَلِ الْعَامِلُونَ "For the like of this let the workers work" (As-Saffat: 61). The meaning is that everything that is described with the attributes with which Allah, the Glorified, is described, nothing resembles or equals it; and everything that contains the attributes contained by Paradise and resembles it in its qualities, for the sake of attaining it let the workers work.

If what is meant by establishment is his absolute establishment in the land, then its comparison to the mentioned description is of the nature of comparing a universal (الكلي) to some of its particulars (أفراده) to indicate that the state of the other particulars is like the state of this particular; or comparing the whole (الكل) to some of its parts (أجزائه) to indicate that the remaining parts are like that mentioned part. Thus, the meaning would be: Our establishment of Joseph in the land proceeded according to this pattern mentioned in the story of his exit from the pit, his entry into Egypt, and his settling in the house of the Aziz in the best of states. For his brothers envied him and denied him settlement on the face of the earth with his father, casting him into the depths of the pit, stripping him of the blessing of enjoyment in his homeland in the desert, and selling him to the caravan to estrange him from his family; yet Allah, the Glorified, made this very plot of theirs a means to reach establishment and settlement in the house of the Aziz in Egypt in the best of states. Then the wife of the Aziz became attached to him and solicited him, she and the women of Egypt, to lead him into youthful passion and indecency; but Allah turned their plot away from him and made that very thing a means for the manifestation of his sincerity and the truth of his faith. Then it seemed to them to put him in prison and strip him of the freedom of social interaction and mixing with people; but Allah, the Glorified, made that very thing a cause for his establishment in the land—an establishment where he settled in the land wherever he wished, with no preventer to stop him nor any repeller to push him away.

In short, the verse on this interpretation is like His saying, the Exalted: كَذَٰلِكَ يُضِلُّ اللَّهُ الْكَافِرِينَ "Thus does Allah lead the faithless astray" (Al-Mu'min: 74) and His saying: كَذَٰلِكَ يَضْرِبُ اللَّهُ الْأَمْثَالَ "Thus does Allah draw the parables" (Ar-Ra'd: 17). That is, His leading the faithless astray always proceeds in this manner, and His drawing of parables is always according to this type of parable drawn, which is a model to which others should be compared.

His saying: وَلِنُعَلِّمَهُ مِن تَأْوِيلِ الْأَحَادِيثِ "and that We might teach him the interpretation of dreams" is an exposition of the purpose (غاية) of the mentioned establishment, and the lam is for purpose. It is conjoined to an omitted phrase, the implied meaning being: "We established him in the land to do such and such for him and that We might teach him the interpretation of dreams." The first part of the conjunction was omitted to indicate that there are other purposes that the context of the discourse cannot encompass. Of this category is His saying, the Exalted: وَكَذَٰلِكَ نُرِي إِبْرَاهِيمَ مَلَكُوتَ السَّمَاوَاتِ وَالْأَرْضِ وَلِيَكُونَ مِنَ الْمُوقِنِينَ "Thus did We show Abraham the dominion of the heavens and the earth, that he might be of those who possess certitude" (Al-An'am: 75) and its likes.

His saying: وَاللَّهُ غَالِبٌ عَلَىٰ أَمْرِهِ وَلَٰكِنَّ أَكْثَرَ النَّاسِ لَا يَعْلَمُونَ "Allah has full command of His affairs, but most people do not know." It appears that what is meant by "affair" (الأمر) is the matter or state, which is what He does in creation from which the system of governance (نظام التدبير) is composed. He, the Exalted, said: يُدَبِّرُ الْأَمْرَ "He directs the command" (Yunus: 3). It is attributed to Him, the Exalted, because He is the owner of every affair, as He, the Exalted, said: أَلَا لَهُ الْخَلْقُ وَالْأَمْرُ ۗ تَبَارَكَ اللَّهُ رَبُّ الْعَالَمِينَ "Look! All creation and command belong to Him. Blessed is Allah, the Lord of all the worlds" (Al-A'raf: 54).

The meaning is that every state among the states of fashioning and origination is part of His affair, the Exalted, and He, the Exalted, is dominant over it. It is overcome by Him, subdued before Him, obeying Him in whatever He wills, and submitting to Him in whatever He intends. It has no power to wax proud or rebel so as to exit His authority, just as it has no power to precede Him, the Exalted, or escape Him. He, the Exalted, said: إِنَّ اللَّهَ بَالِغُ أَمْرِهِ "Indeed Allah carries through His command" (At-Talaq: 3).

In short, He, the Exalted, is dominant over these causes that are effective by His permission, imposing upon them what He wills; they have nothing but to hear and obey. However, most people do not know, because they assume that the apparent causes are independent in their influence and effective on their own. Thus, when events are driven in a certain direction, they think nothing can turn them from their course, and they are mistaken.

(Narrative Discussion)

In al-Ma'ani, with its chain of narration from Abu Hamzah al-Thumali, who said: "I prayed the dawn prayer with Ali ibn al-Husayn (peace be upon him) in Medina on a Friday. When he finished his prayer and glorification, he rose to go to his house and I was with him. He called a female servant of his named Sukaynah and said to her: 'Let no beggar pass by my door except that you feed him, for today is Friday.' I said: 'Not everyone who asks is deserving.' He said: 'O Thabit, I fear that some of those who ask us may be truly in need, and if we do not feed him and turn him away, there may descend upon us, the People of the House, what descended upon Jacob and his family. Feed them.'

'Indeed, Jacob used to slaughter a ram every day, giving it in charity and eating from it with his family. A believing beggar, who was a frequent faster and truly in need, having a high station with Allah, was passing by as a stranger and came to the door of Jacob on a Friday evening at the time of his breaking the fast. He called out at his door: "Feed the passing, hungry, stranger beggar from the surplus of your food." He called out with that at his door several times, but they were ignorant of his right and did not believe his word.'

'When he despaired of them feeding him and the night covered him, he recited the verse of return (استرجع), wept, and complained of his hunger to Allah. He spent the night with an empty stomach and woke up fasting, hungry, patient, and praising Allah. Meanwhile, Jacob and the family of Jacob spent the night full and satiated, and woke up having surplus food with them.'

He said: 'Then Allah, Mighty and Majestic, revealed to Jacob on the morning of that night: "O Jacob, you have humiliated My servant with a humiliation by which you have drawn My wrath, and necessitated My discipline and the descent of My punishment and trial upon you and your children. O Jacob, the most beloved of My prophets to Me and the most honored of them by Me is the one who has mercy on the poor among My servants, brings them close to him, feeds them, and is a shelter and refuge for them."'

'"O Jacob, you did not have mercy on Damyal, My servant who strives in His worship, who is content with the little of the outward world, yesterday evening when he came to your door at the time of his breaking the fast, calling out to you: 'Feed the stranger, passing, content beggar,' yet you did not feed him anything. So he recited the verse of return, wept, and complained of his state to Me. He spent the night hungry and with an empty stomach, praising Me, and woke up fasting for Me. While you, O Jacob, and your children were satiated, and you woke up with surplus food with you."'

'"Do you not know, O Jacob, that punishment and trial are faster toward My friends than toward My enemies? That is due to My good care for My friends and a gradual leading to ruin (استدراج) for My enemies. By My Might, I shall surely send down My trial upon you, and I shall surely make you and your children a target for My misfortunes, and I shall surely discipline you with My punishment. So prepare for My trial, be pleased with My decree, and be patient with the misfortunes."'

I said to Ali ibn al-Husayn (peace be upon him): 'May I be your ransom, when did Joseph see the vision?' He said: 'On that night in which Jacob and the family of Jacob spent the night satiated, and in which Damyal spent the night with an empty stomach and hungry. When Joseph saw the vision and woke up to relate it to his father Jacob, Jacob became grieved when he heard from Joseph and remained grieved. Then Allah revealed to him to prepare for the trial. Jacob said to Joseph: "Do not relate your vision to your brothers, for I fear they will plot a scheme against you." But Joseph did not keep his vision secret and related it to his brothers.'

Ali ibn al-Husayn (peace be upon him) said: 'The first trial that descended upon Jacob and the family of Jacob was the envy toward Joseph when they heard the vision from him.' He said: 'Jacob's tenderness for Joseph intensified, and he feared that what Allah, Mighty and Majestic, had revealed to him regarding preparing for the trial was specifically concerning Joseph. Thus, his tenderness for him intensified among all his children.'

'When the brothers of Joseph saw what Jacob was doing for Joseph, his honoring of him, and his preference of him over them, that became hard upon them and the trial began among them. They conspired among themselves and قَالُوا لَيُوسُفُ وَأَخُوهُ أَحَبُّ إِلَىٰ أَبِينَا مِنَّا وَنَحْنُ عُصْبَةٌ إِنَّ أَبَانَا لَفِي ضَلَالٍ مُّبِينٍ * اقْتُلُوا يُوسُفَ أَوِ اطْرَحُوهُ أَرْضًا يَخْلُ لَكُمْ وَجْهُ أَبِيكُمْ وَتَكُونُوا مِن بَعْدِهِ قَوْمًا صَالِحِينَ "They said, ‘Surely Joseph and his brother are dearer to our father than we are, though we are a hardy group. Our father is indeed in manifest error. Kill Joseph or cast him into some land, so that your father’s attention may be exclusively yours, and after that you may be a righteous people’"—meaning you will repent.'

'At that, قَالُوا يَا أَبَانَا مَا لَكَ لَا تَأْمَنَّا عَلَىٰ يُوسُفَ وَإِنَّا لَهُ لَنَاصِحُونَ "They said, ‘O father! Why is it that you do not trust us with Joseph? We are indeed his well-wishers.’" Jacob said: إِنِّي لَيَحْزُنُنِي أَن تَذْهَبُوا بِهِ وَأَخَافُ أَن يَأْكُلَهُ الذِّئْبُ وَأَنتُمْ عَنْهُ غَافِلُونَ "He said, ‘It indeed unduly grieves me that you should take him away, and I fear the wolf may eat him while you are oblivious of him.’" He took him away, being cautious and wary for him, fearing that the trial from Allah, Mighty and Majestic, upon Jacob would be specifically through Joseph, because of his place in his heart and his love for him.'

He said: 'The power of Allah, His decree, and His effective command prevailed over Jacob, Joseph, and his brothers. Jacob was not able to repel the trial from himself, nor from Joseph and his children. So he handed him over to them while he was loath to do so, expecting the trial from Allah regarding Joseph.'

'When they left their house, he followed them quickly, took him from their hands, pressed him to himself, embraced him, wept, and then handed him over to them. They set off with him quickly, fearing that he would take him from them and not return him. When they had gone far with him, they brought him to a thicket of trees and said: "We will slaughter him and throw him under this tree, so the wolf will eat him tonight." Their eldest said: لَا تَقْتُلُوا يُوسُفَ "Do not kill Joseph," but وَأَلْقُوهُ فِي غَيَابَتِ الْجُبِّ يَلْتَقِطْهُ بَعْضُ السَّيَّارَةِ إِن كُنتُمْ فَاعِلِينَ "and cast him into the depths of the pit; some caravan will pick him up, if you are to do [anything]."'

'So they went with him to the pit and cast him into it, thinking that he would drown in it. When he reached the bottom of the pit, he called out to them: "O children of Rumin, convey my greetings of peace to Jacob." When they heard his speech, they said to one another: "Do not leave from here until you know that he has died." They remained in his presence until they despaired, "and they came to their father at nightfall, weeping. They said: 'O father! We went racing one another and left Joseph with our goods, and the wolf ate him.'"

'When he heard their speech, he recited the verse of return, wept, and remembered what Allah, Mighty and Majestic, had revealed to him regarding preparing for the trial. So he was patient, submitted to the trial, and said to them: بَلْ سَوَّلَتْ لكُمْ أَنْفُسُكُمْ أَمْرًا "Rather your souls have made up a matter for you." Allah would not let the wolf eat the flesh of Joseph before I see the interpretation of his truthful vision.'

Abu Hamzah said: "Then the narration of Ali ibn al-Husayn (peace be upon him) broke off at this point."

Abu Hamzah said: "The next day, I went to him and said: 'May I be your ransom, you related to me yesterday a narration of Jacob and his children, then you broke it off regarding what happened in the story of Joseph's brothers and the story of Joseph after that.' He said: 'When they woke up, they said: "Let us go and see what is the state of Joseph: has he died or is he alive?"'

'When they reached the pit, they found a caravan present at the pit. They had sent their water-drawer, who let down his bucket. When he pulled his bucket, there was a boy clinging to his bucket. He said to his companions: "O good news, this is a boy!" When they brought him out, the brothers of Joseph approached them and said: "This is our slave who fell from us yesterday into this pit, and we have come today to bring him out." They snatched him from their hands and took him aside, saying to him: "Either you admit to us that you are our slave so we may sell you to some of the caravan, or we will kill you." Joseph said to them: "Do not kill me, and do what you wish."'

'So they approached the caravan and said: "Who among you will buy this slave from us?" One of them bought him for twenty dirhams, and his brothers set small store by him. The one who bought him from the desert traveled with him until he entered Egypt. Then the one who bought him from the desert sold him to the king of Egypt. That is the saying of Allah, Mighty and Majestic: وَقَالَ الَّذِي اشْتَرَاهُ مِن مِّصْرَ لِامْرَأَتِهِ أَكْرِمِي مَثْوَاهُ عَسَىٰ أَن يَنفَعَنَا أَوْ نَتَّخِذَهُ وَلَدًا "The man from Egypt who bought him said to his wife, ‘Give him an honorable stay. Maybe he will be of use to us, or we may adopt him as a son.’"'

Abu Hamzah said: "I said to Ali ibn al-Husayn (peace be upon him): 'How old was Joseph on the day they cast him into the pit?' He said: 'Nine years old.' I said: 'How far was it between the dwelling of Jacob on that day and Egypt?' He said: 'A journey of twelve days.'" (The narration continues).

I say: The narration has a sequel that we will provide in the following traditionary discussion, if Allah, the Exalted, wills. In it are points that may not seem to align with the apparent meaning of the previous exposition of the verses, but they are resolved with the slightest reflection.

In al-Durr al-Manthur, Ahmad and al-Bukhari narrated from Ibn Umar that the Messenger of Allah (peace be upon him and his progeny) said: "The noble son of the noble son of the noble son of the noble—Joseph son of Jacob son of Isaac son of Abraham."

In the Tafsir of al-Ayyashi, from Zurarah from Abu Ja'far (peace be upon him), who said: "The prophets are of five types: among them are those who hear a sound—like the sound of a chain—and know what is meant by it; among them are those who are informed in their sleep—like Joseph and Abraham (peace be upon them); among them are those who witness visually; and among them are those into whose hearts it is nudged and into whose ears it is whispered."

In the same source, from Abu Khadijah from a man from Abu Abdillah (peace be upon him), who said: "Jacob was only tried with Joseph because he slaughtered a fat ram while one of his companions, called Bayum (or Qawm), was in need and found nothing with which to break his fast. He was heedless of him and did not feed him, so he was tried with Joseph. After that, every morning his caller would call out: 'Whoever is not fasting, let him attend the lunch of Jacob,' and when it was evening, he would call out: 'Whoever is fasting, let him attend the dinner of Jacob.'"

In the Tafsir of al-Qummi, he said: "In the report of Abu al-Jarud from Abu Ja'far (peace be upon him) regarding His saying: لَتُنَبِّئَنَّهُم بِأَمْرِهِمْ هَٰذَا وَهُمْ لَا يَشْعُرُونَ 'You will surely inform them of this affair of theirs while they are not aware' (Verse 15), he said: 'They will not be aware that you are Joseph. Gabriel came to him and informed him of that.'"

In the same source, and in the report of Abu al-Jarud: "Regarding the saying of Allah: وَجَاءُوا عَلَىٰ قَمِيصِهِ بِدَمٍ كَذِبٍ 'And they brought his shirt with false blood on it' (Verse 18), he said: 'They slaughtered a kid over his shirt.'"

In the Amali of the Shaykh, with its chain of narration: "Regarding the saying of Allah, Mighty and Majestic: فَصَبْرٌ جَمِيلٌ 'So [my recourse is] a graceful patience,' he said: 'Without complaint.'"

I say: The report is likely from Imam al-Sadiq (peace be upon him) by virtue of it being preceded by a narration from him. This meaning was also narrated in al-Durr al-Manthur from Hayyan ibn Jabalah from the Prophet (peace be upon him and his progeny), and there are other reports with the same themes.

Ayahs

وَلَمَّا بَلَغَ أَشُدَّهُۥٓ ءَاتَيْنَـٰهُ حُكْمًا وَعِلْمًا ۚ وَكَذَٰلِكَ نَجْزِى ٱلْمُحْسِنِينَ (٢٢)
When he came of age, We gave him judgement and [sacred] knowledge, and thus do We reward the virtuous. (22)
وَرَٰوَدَتْهُ ٱلَّتِى هُوَ فِى بَيْتِهَا عَن نَّفْسِهِۦ وَغَلَّقَتِ ٱلْأَبْوَٰبَ وَقَالَتْ هَيْتَ لَكَ ۚ قَالَ مَعَاذَ ٱللَّهِ ۖ إِنَّهُۥ رَبِّىٓ أَحْسَنَ مَثْوَاىَ ۖ إِنَّهُۥ لَا يُفْلِحُ ٱلظَّـٰلِمُونَ (٢٣)
The woman in whose house he was, solicited him. She closed the doors and said, ‘Come!!’ He said, ‘God forbid! Indeed He is my Lord; He has given me a good abode. Indeed the wrongdoers are not felicitous.’ (23)
وَلَقَدْ هَمَّتْ بِهِۦ ۖ وَهَمَّ بِهَا لَوْلَآ أَن رَّءَا بُرْهَـٰنَ رَبِّهِۦ ۚ كَذَٰلِكَ لِنَصْرِفَ عَنْهُ ٱلسُّوٓءَ وَٱلْفَحْشَآءَ ۚ إِنَّهُۥ مِنْ عِبَادِنَا ٱلْمُخْلَصِينَ (٢٤)
She certainly made for him; and he would have made for her [too] had he not beheld the proof of his Lord. So it was, that We might turn away from him all evil and indecency. He was indeed one of Our dedicated servants. (24)
وَٱسْتَبَقَا ٱلْبَابَ وَقَدَّتْ قَمِيصَهُۥ مِن دُبُرٍ وَأَلْفَيَا سَيِّدَهَا لَدَا ٱلْبَابِ ۚ قَالَتْ مَا جَزَآءُ مَنْ أَرَادَ بِأَهْلِكَ سُوٓءًا إِلَّآ أَن يُسْجَنَ أَوْ عَذَابٌ أَلِيمٌ (٢٥)
They raced to the door, and she tore his shirt from behind, and they ran into her husband at the door. She said, ‘What is to be the requital of him who has evil intentions for your wife except imprisonment or a painful punishment?’ (25)
قَالَ هِىَ رَٰوَدَتْنِى عَن نَّفْسِى ۚ وَشَهِدَ شَاهِدٌ مِّنْ أَهْلِهَآ إِن كَانَ قَمِيصُهُۥ قُدَّ مِن قُبُلٍ فَصَدَقَتْ وَهُوَ مِنَ ٱلْكَـٰذِبِينَ (٢٦)
He said, ‘It was she who solicited me.’ A witness of her own household testified [thus]: ‘If his shirt is torn from the front, she tells the truth and he lies. (26)
وَإِن كَانَ قَمِيصُهُۥ قُدَّ مِن دُبُرٍ فَكَذَبَتْ وَهُوَ مِنَ ٱلصَّـٰدِقِينَ (٢٧)
But if his shirt is torn from behind, then she lies and he tells the truth.’ (27)
فَلَمَّا رَءَا قَمِيصَهُۥ قُدَّ مِن دُبُرٍ قَالَ إِنَّهُۥ مِن كَيْدِكُنَّ ۖ إِنَّ كَيْدَكُنَّ عَظِيمٌ (٢٨)
So when he saw that his shirt was torn from behind, he said, ‘This is [a case] of you women’s guile! Your guile is indeed great! (28)
يُوسُفُ أَعْرِضْ عَنْ هَـٰذَا ۚ وَٱسْتَغْفِرِى لِذَنۢبِكِ ۖ إِنَّكِ كُنتِ مِنَ ٱلْخَاطِـِٔينَ (٢٩)
Joseph, let this matter alone, and you, woman, plead for forgiveness for your sin, for you have indeed been erring.’ (29)
۞ وَقَالَ نِسْوَةٌ فِى ٱلْمَدِينَةِ ٱمْرَأَتُ ٱلْعَزِيزِ تُرَٰوِدُ فَتَىٰهَا عَن نَّفْسِهِۦ ۖ قَدْ شَغَفَهَا حُبًّا ۖ إِنَّا لَنَرَىٰهَا فِى ضَلَـٰلٍ مُّبِينٍ (٣٠)
Some of the townswomen said, ‘The chieftain’s wife has solicited her slave boy! He has captivated her love. Indeed we see her to be in manifest error.’ (30)
فَلَمَّا سَمِعَتْ بِمَكْرِهِنَّ أَرْسَلَتْ إِلَيْهِنَّ وَأَعْتَدَتْ لَهُنَّ مُتَّكَـًٔا وَءَاتَتْ كُلَّ وَٰحِدَةٍ مِّنْهُنَّ سِكِّينًا وَقَالَتِ ٱخْرُجْ عَلَيْهِنَّ ۖ فَلَمَّا رَأَيْنَهُۥٓ أَكْبَرْنَهُۥ وَقَطَّعْنَ أَيْدِيَهُنَّ وَقُلْنَ حَـٰشَ لِلَّهِ مَا هَـٰذَا بَشَرًا إِنْ هَـٰذَآ إِلَّا مَلَكٌ كَرِيمٌ (٣١)
When she heard of their machinations, she sent for them and arranged a repast, and gave each of them a knife, and said [to Joseph], ‘Come out before them.’ So when they saw him, they marvelled at him and cut their hands [absent-mindedly], and they said, ‘Good heavens! This is not a human being! This is but a noble angel!’ (31)
قَالَتْ فَذَٰلِكُنَّ ٱلَّذِى لُمْتُنَّنِى فِيهِ ۖ وَلَقَدْ رَٰوَدتُّهُۥ عَن نَّفْسِهِۦ فَٱسْتَعْصَمَ ۖ وَلَئِن لَّمْ يَفْعَلْ مَآ ءَامُرُهُۥ لَيُسْجَنَنَّ وَلَيَكُونًا مِّنَ ٱلصَّـٰغِرِينَ (٣٢)
She said, ‘He is the one on whose account you blamed me. Certainly, I did solicit him, but he was continent, and if he does not do what I bid him, surely he shall be imprisoned and humbled.’ (32)
قَالَ رَبِّ ٱلسِّجْنُ أَحَبُّ إِلَىَّ مِمَّا يَدْعُونَنِىٓ إِلَيْهِ ۖ وَإِلَّا تَصْرِفْ عَنِّى كَيْدَهُنَّ أَصْبُ إِلَيْهِنَّ وَأَكُن مِّنَ ٱلْجَـٰهِلِينَ (٣٣)
He said, ‘My Lord! The prison is dearer to me than to what they invite me. If You do not turn away their schemes from me, I will incline towards them and become one of the senseless.’ (33)
فَٱسْتَجَابَ لَهُۥ رَبُّهُۥ فَصَرَفَ عَنْهُ كَيْدَهُنَّ ۚ إِنَّهُۥ هُوَ ٱلسَّمِيعُ ٱلْعَلِيمُ (٣٤)
So his Lord answered him and turned away their stratagems from him. Indeed, He is the All-hearing, the All-knowing. (34)

(Commentary)

The verses encompass the story of Yusuf (peace be upon him) during the days of his stay in the house of the Aziz (the Mighty One of Egypt). In this period, he was tested by the love the wife of the Aziz harbored for him and her attempt to seduce him. He was further afflicted by the infatuation of the women of the city and their attempts to entice him. This was a profound trial (بلوى), through which an extraordinary degree of chastity (عفة) and purity of character (طهارة الذيل) became manifest, and even more wondrous was his total absorption (توله) in the love of his Lord.

His saying, the Exalted: «And when he reached his full maturity, We gave him judgment and knowledge, and thus do We reward the righteous» (وَلَمَّا بَلَغَ أَشُدَّهُ آتَيْناهُ حُكْماً وَعِلْماً وَكَذلِكَ نَجْزِي الْمُحْسِنِينَ). Reaching full maturity (بلوغ الأشد) refers to a person reaching an age where the physical powers of the body become firm and the limbs strengthen as the traces of childhood vanish. This typically begins from the age of eighteen and continues until middle age, at which point the intellect (العقل) reaches perfection and maturity (الرشد) is complete.

It appears that what is intended here is reaching the beginning of youth, rather than its middle or its end (such as the age of forty). The evidence for this is His saying, the Exalted, regarding Musa (peace be upon him): «And when he reached his full maturity and became firm, We gave him judgment and knowledge» (وَلَمَّا بَلَغَ أَشُدَّهُ وَاسْتَوى آتَيْناهُ حُكْماً وَعِلْماً) (Al-Qasas: 14), where He indicated the middle of that stage with the word "became firm" (اسْتَوى). Furthermore, His saying: «until, when he reaches his full maturity and reaches forty years, he says, ‘My Lord! Inspire me to give thanks for Your blessing...’» (حَتَّى إِذا بَلَغَ أَشُدَّهُ وَبَلَغَ أَرْبَعِينَ سَنَةً قالَ رَبِّ أَوْزِعْنِي أَنْ أَشْكُرَ نِعْمَتَكَ) (Al-Ahqaf: 15). If reaching full maturity were synonymous with reaching forty, there would have been no need to repeat the word "reaches" (بَلَغَ).

Thus, there is no merit to what some have mentioned—that reaching full maturity means reaching thirty or thirty-three—nor to what others have said—that it means reaching forty. Moreover, it would be absurd to suggest that the wife of the Aziz waited throughout Yusuf’s prime and the bloom of his youth, only to become infatuated and attempt to seduce him when he reached forty and approached old age.

Regarding His saying: «We gave him judgment» (آتَيْناهُ حُكْماً), judgment (الحكم) is the decisive word (القول الفصل) and the removal of doubt and uncertainty from matters subject to disagreement—as derived from the linguistic root. Its necessary consequence is the correctness of vision in general human knowledge pertaining to the Origin (المبدأ), the Return (المعاد), spiritual ethics (الأخلاق النفسانية), and the laws and etiquettes (الشرائع والآداب) related to human society.

In light of his (peace be upon him) saying to his two companions in prison: «Judgment belongs only to Allah» (إِنِ الْحُكْمُ إِلَّا لِلَّهِ) (Verse 40 of the Surah), and his subsequent saying: «The matter about which you both inquire has been decided» (قُضِيَ الْأَمْرُ الَّذِي فِيهِ تَسْتَفْتِيانِ) (Verse 41), it is known that this judgment (الحكم) granted to him was the judgment of Allah; thus, his judgment was Allah’s judgment. This is precisely what Ibrahim (peace be upon him) asked of his Lord when he said: «My Lord! Grant me judgment and join me with the righteous» (رَبِّ هَبْ لِي حُكْماً وَأَلْحِقْنِي بِالصَّالِحِينَ) (Ash-Shu'ara: 83).

Regarding His saying: «and knowledge» (وَعِلْماً), this mentioned knowledge, attributed to the granting of the Exalted, regardless of its nature or extent, is a knowledge (علم) unmixed with ignorance (جهل). Similarly, the judgment (الحكم) mentioned with it is a judgment unmixed with personal whim (هوى نفساني) or satanic suggestion (تسويل شيطاني). How could it be otherwise? For the One who granted them both is Allah, the Glorified, and He has said: «And Allah is dominant over His affairs» (وَاللهُ غالِبٌ عَلى أَمْرِهِ) (Verse 21), and: «Indeed Allah carries through His command» (إِنَّ اللهَ بالِغُ أَمْرِهِ) (At-Talaq: 3). Therefore, the judgment He grants is unmixed with the instability of doubt and uncertainty, and the knowledge He grants can never be ignorance.

Furthermore, it is understood that these divine gifts are not haphazard acts, nor are they futile or vain on the part of the Exalted. The souls granted this judgment and knowledge are not equal to the erring souls who are submerged in their ignorance. Allah the Exalted has said: «As for the good land, its vegetation comes out by the leave of its Lord, and as for that which is bad, it does not yield except scantily» (وَالْبَلَدُ الطَّيِّبُ يَخْرُجُ نَباتُهُ بِإِذْنِ رَبِّهِ وَالَّذِي خَبُثَ لا يَخْرُجُ إِلَّا نَكِداً) (Al-A'raf: 58). This is alluded to by His saying: «and thus do We reward the righteous» (وَكَذلِكَ نَجْزِي الْمُحْسِنِينَ), which indicates that this judgment and knowledge were not primary gifts without any prerequisite; rather, they were a form of reward (جزاء) with which Allah recompensed him for being among the righteous (المحسنين).

It is not far-fetched to infer from His saying: «and thus do We reward the righteous» that Allah the Exalted rewards every righteous person—according to their varying degrees of righteousness—with a portion of judgment and knowledge suitable to their station in righteousness. Allah the Exalted has said: «O you who have faith! Be wary of Allah and believe in His Apostle. He will grant you a double share of His mercy and give you a light to walk by» (يا أَيُّهَا الَّذِينَ آمَنُوا اتَّقُوا اللهَ وَآمِنُوا بِرَسُولِهِ يُؤْتِكُمْ كِفْلَيْنِ مِنْ رَحْمَتِهِ وَيَجْعَلْ لَكُمْ نُوراً تَمْشُونَ بِهِ) (Al-Hadid: 28), and: «Is he who was lifeless, then We gave him life and maintained for him a light by which he walks among the people...» (أَوَمَنْ كانَ مَيْتاً فَأحيَيناهُ وَجَعَلنا لَهُ نوراً يَمشي بِهِ فِي النّاسِ) (Al-An'am: 122).

This knowledge mentioned in the verse includes what Allah promised to teach Yusuf regarding the interpretation of dreams (تأويل الأحاديث). This is because it falls between His saying in the preceding verses: «and that We might teach him the interpretation of dreams» (وَلِنُعَلِّمَهُ مِنْ تَأْوِيلِ الْأَحادِيثِ) and His saying, narrating Yusuf’s words to his prison companions: «That is of what my Lord has taught me» (ذلِكُما مِمَّا عَلَّمَنِي رَبِّي). Understand this well.

His saying, the Exalted: «And she in whose house he was, solicited him, and she shut the doors and said, ‘Come!’ He said, ‘Allah forbid! Indeed he is my lord; he has given me a goodly lodging. Indeed the wrongdoers do not prosper’» (وَراوَدَتْهُ الَّتِي هُوَ فِي بَيْتِها عَنْ نَفْسِهِ وَغَلَّقَتِ الْأَبْوابَ وَقالَتْ هَيْتَ لَكَ قالَ مَعاذَ اللهِ إِنَّهُ رَبِّي أَحْسَنَ مَثْوايَ إِنَّهُ لا يُفْلِحُ الظَّالِمُونَ). Al-Raghib said in al-Mufradat: Al-Rawd (الرود) is to go to and fro in seeking something gently; from it comes the term ra'id (رائد) for one seeking pasture. He said: al-iradah (الإرادة - will/desire) is derived from rada yarudu (راد يرود) when one strives to seek something. He said: al-murawadah (المراودة - solicitation/enticement) is to contend with another regarding their will, desiring something other than what they desire, or seeking something other than what they seek. "I solicited so-and-so regarding such-and-such." The Exalted said: «It was she who solicited me» (هِيَ راوَدَتْنِي عَنْ نَفْسِي) and: «soliciting her slave-boy» (تُراوِدُ فَتاها عَنْ نَفْسِهِ), meaning she tried to turn him away from his own intent. Similarly: «I did solicit him» (وَلَقَدْ راوَدْتُهُ عَنْ نَفْسِهِ) and «We will solicit his father for him» (سَنُراوِدُ عَنْهُ أَباهُ). [End of quote].

In al-Majma': al-murawadah is to request a matter with gentleness and softness so that it may be acted upon. From it comes the term mirwad (مرود - a probe/applicator) because it is used gently. One does not say rawadahu when demanding a debt. Its origin is from rada yarudu when seeking pasture. In the proverb: "The scout (الرائد) does not lie to his people." Al-taghliq (التغليق) is to shut the door in a way that makes it difficult to open. The intensive form (شدد) is used to indicate the closing of many doors or to emphasize the security of the locking. [End of quote].

Hayta laka (هَيْتَ لَكَ) is a verbal noun meaning "Come!" or "I am yours." Ma'adh Allah (مَعاذَ اللهِ) means "I seek refuge in Allah (معاذا)"; it is an absolute object (مفعول مطلق) standing in place of its verb.

The noble verse «And she in whose house he was, solicited him, and she shut the doors and said, ‘Come!’ He said, ‘Allah forbid! Indeed he is my lord; he has given me a goodly lodging. Indeed the wrongdoers do not prosper’», despite its brevity, informs us of the general story of the solicitation. However, contemplation (التدبر) of the constraints employed, the context in which it occurs, and other aspects of his story mentioned in the Surah, clarifies the reality of the situation and removes the veil from the details of what was hidden.

Yusuf:

Here is a young child whom the hands of destiny have moved to the house of the Aziz, bearing the mark of slaves. Perhaps he was asked nothing but his name, and spoke nothing but "My name is Yusuf," or that was said of him. Nothing was apparent from his dialect except that he had been raised among the Hebrews. He was not asked about his home or lineage, for slaves have no homes, and it was not customary to preserve the lineages of bondsmen. He remained silent, his tongue sealed, speaking of nothing, though many a story lay within his breast. His lineage was not known until years later when he said to his prison companions: «I follow the creed of my fathers, Ibrahim, Ishaq, and Ya‘qub» (وَاتَّبَعْتُ مِلَّةَ آبائِي إِبْراهِيمَ وَإِسْحاقَ وَيَعْقُوبَ). Nor did he disclose the monotheism (التوحيد) of his servitude to Allah among those idolaters except what he mentioned to the wife of the Aziz when she solicited him, saying: «Allah forbid! Indeed He is my Lord...»

Today, he is an ally of silence, yet his heart is full of what he witnesses of Allah’s subtle handiwork with him. He remembers what his father, the Prophet Ya'qub, instilled in him regarding the reality of monotheism (التوحيد) — the absolute oneness of Allah in His essence, attributes, and acts — and the meaning of servitude (العبودية). He remembers the glad tidings of the vision—that Allah would choose him for Himself and join him with his fathers Ibrahim, Ishaq, and Ya'qub. He does not forget what his brothers did to him, nor what his Lord promised him in the depths of the well when he was cut off from all means: that he was under Divine Guardianship (الولاية الإلهية) and Lordly Upbringing (التربية الربوبية), cared for in his affairs, and that he would surely inform his brothers of this deed of theirs while they were unaware.

Thus, he (peace be upon him) was full of perception, his soul immersed in witnessing the hidden graces of his Lord. He saw himself under the guardianship (ولاية) of Allah, delighted by His beautiful handiwork, encountering nothing but good and facing nothing but beauty.

This is what eased the calamities that befell him and the successive trials and tribulations. He bore them despite their bitterness, neither complaining, nor despairing, nor losing his way. He mentioned this to his brothers when he identified himself to them, saying: «Indeed whoever is God-conscious and patient—Allah does not let the reward of the righteous go to waste» (إِنَّهُ مَنْ يَتَّقِ وَيَصْبِرْ فَإِنَّ اللهَ لا يُضِيعُ أَجْرَ الْمُحْسِنِينَ) (Verse 90).

Yusuf’s soul continued to be drawn to the beautiful handiwork of his Lord, his heart deeply contemplating the subtle signs pointing toward Him. Every day, his love increased through the evidence of guardianship (الولاية) he found, witnessing that his Lord is the One who maintains every soul according to what it has earned, and that He is a witness over all things.
