This is a characteristic of infatuation and terror; for when the human soul is attracted to something it excessively loves or fears and finds terrifying, it becomes agitated and bewildered. Death may surprise it, or it may be stripped of the consciousness necessary for managing the faculties and limbs and organizing affairs. Thus, one might rush toward the danger whose encounter stunned him, or might forget to flee and remain like a motionless stone, or might do something other than what he intends out of confusion. Examples of this in the realm of love are many, and the stories of infatuated and bewildered lovers are famous.

This was the difference between the wife of the Aziz and them: her immersion in the love of Yusuf happened gradually, whereas the women of the city were surprised by him suddenly. A covering of beauty overwhelmed their hearts, and love abandoned them, disgracing them, taking away their minds, and misleading their judgment. So they forgot the fruit and cut their hands, abandoning all endurance and patience, and revealed the infatuation of love in their souls, saying: «حاشَ لِلَّهِ ما هذا بَشَراً إِنْ هذا إِلَّا مَلَكٌ كَرِيمٌ» (‘Good Heavens! This is not a human being! This is nothing but a noble angel!’).

This happened while they were in the house of the Aziz, a house where every etiquette and dignity must be preserved. They should have been wary of her and respected her position, as they were noblewomen of beauty, with husbands, and of seclusion and veiling—all of which are factors preventing shamelessness and indecency. They had not forgotten what they were saying just yesterday, blaming and criticizing the wife of the Aziz for her love for Yusuf, while they had been in one house for years.

It was incumbent upon each of them to be wary of her companions and not be indecent, knowing what the affair of the wife of the Aziz had led to in terms of bad reputation and scandalous fame. All of this while Yusuf was standing before them, hearing their words and witnessing their deeds.

But what they witnessed suddenly of Yusuf's beauty abrogated what they had previously estimated in their souls and turned the gathering of etiquette and respect into a party of life whose participants hide no secret from themselves, and whose attendees do not care what was said or will be said about them. They did not hesitate to say: «حاشَ لِلَّهِ ما هذا بَشَراً إِنْ هذا إِلَّا مَلَكٌ كَرِيمٌ», having said not long before: «امْرَأَتُ الْعَزِيزِ تُراوِدُ فَتاها عَنْ نَفْسِهِ قَدْ شَغَفَها حُبًّا إِنَّا لَنَراها فِي ضَلالٍ مُبِينٍ».

Their saying this after their previous saying was an excuse from them. Its meaning is that what we were saying before was only true if this were a human being, but he is not. A person is only blamed and criticized if afflicted with the love of a human and soliciting him, when it was in his power to be satisfied with what is equivalent and sufficient. As for the beauty that no beauty equals, and which strips away all prudence and choice, there is no blame for its love, nor criticism for its infatuation.

Therefore, the gathering turned suddenly, and the bonds of respect were broken. They spoke openly and manifested their speech regarding Yusuf's beauty, each speaking what was in her heart regarding him. The wife of the Aziz said: «فَذلِكُنَّ الَّذِي لُمْتُنَّنِي فِيهِ وَلَقَدْ راوَدْتُهُ عَنْ نَفْسِهِ فَاسْتَعْصَمَ» (She said, ‘This is he for whom you blamed me. I did solicit him, but he was chaste’). She revealed a secret she had not admitted before, then threatened Yusuf with endurance to preserve her status before them and in hope of his compliance and submission: «وَلَئِنْ لَمْ يَفْعَلْ ما آمُرُهُ لَيُسْجَنَنَّ وَلَيَكُوناً مِنَ الصَّاغِرِينَ» (‘And if he does not do what I bid him, he shall surely be imprisoned, and he shall surely be among the humiliated’).

As for Yusuf, he was not taken by any of those beautiful faces with their enchanting glances, nor did he turn to any of their fine words, their pleasant solicitation, or her terrifying threat. For the direction of his soul was a beauty above all beauty, and a majesty before which every honor and majesty is humbled. He did not speak to them at all, nor did he turn to what the wife of the Aziz was saying. Rather, he turned back to his Lord and said: «رَبِّ السِّجْنُ أَحَبُّ إِلَيَّ مِمَّا يَدْعُونَنِي إِلَيْهِ وَإِلَّا تَصْرِفْ عَنِّي كَيْدَهُنَّ أَصْبُ إِلَيْهِنَّ وَأَكُنْ مِنَ الْجاهِلِينَ» (He said, ‘My Lord! The prison is dearer to me than what they invite me to. If You do not turn away their guile from me, I will succumb to them and become one of the ignorant’).

His speech here, when compared to what he said to the wife of the Aziz alone in the session of seduction: «مَعاذَ اللهِ إِنَّهُ رَبِّي أَحْسَنَ مَثْوايَ إِنَّهُ لا يُفْلِحُ الظَّالِمُونَ» (Allah forbid! He is my lord; he has given me a good abode. Indeed the wrongdoers do not prosper), indicates by its context that this situation was more difficult and bitter for Yusuf (peace be upon him). Yesterday he was resisting the inclination of the wife of the Aziz and dealing with her guile alone; today, the inclinations and guiles of all of them were directed at him. What happened yesterday was in a secret, veiled encounter; today, she and they were open in their love, manifest in their seduction, forcing him into solicitation. All causes and requirements today were judging for them against him more intensely than they were yesterday.

Therefore, he pleaded to his Lord, the Glorified, to repel their guile here, whereas he was satisfied with seeking refuge in Him, the Glorified, there. So his Lord answered him and turned away their guile from him; indeed, He is the All-hearing, the All-knowing.

Let us return to the study of the verses.

His saying, the Exalted: «وَقالَ نِسْوَةٌ فِي الْمَدِينَةِ امْرَأَتُ الْعَزِيزِ تُراوِدُ فَتاها» etc. 'Women' (النسوة) is a collective noun for woman. Restricting it with His saying: «فِي الْمَدِينَةِ» (in the city) implies that they were of such number or status that their words would affect the spread of the scandal.

The wife of the Aziz is the one in whose house Yusuf was, and she had solicited him. 'The Aziz' (العزيز) — the title of the high official — has a known meaning; it was the title of the master who bought Yusuf from the caravan, and it was the title of the leaders in Egypt, just as Yusuf was titled with it after he was placed over the storehouses of the land.

In His saying: «تُراوِدُ» (is soliciting) there is an indication of continuity, which is the most obscene form of solicitation. 'The slave' (الفتى) is the young man, and the woman is a fatah (فتاة). It became common to call a slave fata, and it is as if with this consideration he was added to her pronoun, so it was said: «فَتاها» (her slave).

In al-Mufradat: «شَغَفَها حُبًّا» means it reached the shaghaf (شغاف) of her heart, which is its interior. Narrated from al-Hasan. It was said: its center. Narrated from Abu ‘Ali. They are close in meaning. End quote. The shaghaf of the heart is the membrane surrounding it.

The meaning is: A number of the women of the city said—and their words were not without effect on her and her reputation: 'A woman who continues to solicit her slave; it is not fitting for her because she is a woman, and it is shameless for a woman to solicit a man; rather that—if it happens—is from the nature of men. And she is the wife of the Aziz, the governor of Egypt; so it is an inescapable duty that she observe the honor of her house, the dignity of her husband, and the status of herself. And the one she is attached to is her slave; it is hideous for someone like her, the wife of the Aziz, to be infatuated with a Hebrew slave from among his slaves. And she loved him and exceeded that to soliciting him, but he refused to respond to her, yet she did not stop until she persisted and continued in soliciting him—and that is more hideous, more scandalous, and deeper in error.'

Therefore, they followed their saying: «امْرَأَتُ الْعَزِيزِ تُراوِدُ» etc., with their saying: «إِنَّا لَنَراها فِي ضَلالٍ مُبِينٍ» (Indeed we see her to be in manifest error).

His saying, the Exalted: «فَلَمَّا سَمِعَتْ بِمَكْرِهِنَّ أَرْسَلَتْ إِلَيْهِنَّ وَأَعْتَدَتْ لَهُنَّ مُتَّكَأً وَآتَتْ كُلَّ واحِدَةٍ مِنْهُنَّ سِكِّيناً» (When she heard of their guile, she sent for them and prepared a repast for them, and gave each of them a knife). It is stated in al-Majma‘: Guile (المكر) is twisting with a ruse to achieve what is desired. End quote. Calling this speech of theirs 'guile' against the wife of the Aziz is because of the scandal and the tearing of her veil by her rivals out of jealousy and transgression. She only sent for them to show them Yusuf and afflict them with what she herself was afflicted with, so they would stop blaming her and excuse her for loving him.

Accordingly, their speech was called 'guile' and hearing was attributed to it because it was issued from them out of jealousy and transgression for the purpose of her scandal among the people.

It was said: Their speech was 'guile' because they made it a pretext to meet Yusuf when they heard of his wonderful beauty. They only said this so that the wife of the Aziz would hear it and send for them to attend her presence, so she would show him to them to excuse her for what they blamed her for, thus taking that as a path to solicit him themselves. However, the first view is closer to the context of the verses.

His saying: «أَرْسَلَتْ إِلَيْهِنَ» (she sent for them), its meaning is known, and it is an allusion to the invitation to attend her presence.

His saying: «وَأَعْتَدَتْ لَهُنَّ مُتَّكَأً وَآتَتْ كُلَّ واحِدَةٍ مِنْهُنَّ سِكِّيناً». 'Preparation' (الاعتاد) is preparing and readying. 'Repast' (المتكأ) — the place or arrangement for reclining — is the object of 'reclining' (الاتكاء); what is meant is that which is reclined upon, such as a cushion or a chair, as was the custom in the houses of the great. Al-mutta’ (المتكأ) was interpreted as the citron, which is a type of fruit, as read in the irregular readings «مُتَّكَأً» with a damma then a sukun, meaning the citron. It was also read «مُتَّكَأً» with a damma on the mim and a shadda on the ta without a hamza.

His saying: «وَآتَتْ كُلَّ واحِدَةٍ مِنْهُنَّ سِكِّيناً» (and gave each of them a knife), meaning to cut what they saw fit to eat of the fruit, like the citron or similar fruits eaten by cutting. And His saying: «وَقالَتِ اخْرُجْ عَلَيْهِنَ» (and she said [to Joseph], ‘Come out before them!’), meaning she commanded Yusuf to come out to them while they were unaware, their hearts empty, occupied with taking the fruit and cutting it. In the wording, there is an indication that he (peace be upon him) was absent from them, in a chamber there or another room inside the banquet hall where they were; for she said: «اخْرُجْ عَلَيْهِنَ» (Come out before them), and had he been outside the house, she would have said: 'Enter before them.'

In the context, there is an indication that this arrangement was a ruse from her against their ruse, so they would be disgraced by it and thus excuse her for what they blamed her for. She was correct in her judgment when she organized the program of the meeting: she prepared for them a repast, gave each of them a knife, hid Yusuf from their eyes, then surprised them by showing him suddenly so they would lose their minds and be stunned by that wonderful beauty, doing what no conscious person would ever do—cutting their hands instead of the fruits—not just one or two of them, but all of them.

His saying, the Exalted: «فَلَمَّا رَأَيْنَهُ أَكْبَرْنَهُ وَقَطَّعْنَ أَيْدِيَهُنَّ وَقُلْنَ حاشَ لِلَّهِ ما هذا بَشَراً إِنْ هذا إِلَّا مَلَكٌ كَرِيمٌ» (When they saw him, they were awestruck by him and cut their hands, and said, ‘Good Heavens! This is not a human being! This is nothing but a noble angel!’). 'Awestruck' (الإكبار) is to deem great, and it is an allusion to their bewilderment and the loss of their consciousness and will by the suddenness of witnessing that magnificent beauty, according to the general cosmic law: the submission of the small to the great and the subdual of the lowly by the magnificent. When the magnificent and great appears with its greatness and majesty to human consciousness, it subdues all other purposes and thoughts in the mind, making one forget them and act erratically.

Therefore, when they saw him, the sight of him subdued their consciousness, so they cut their hands repeatedly instead of the fruit they intended to cut. In the form of the verb (تقطيع), there is an indication of multiplicity; it is said: 'The people were slaughtered (تقتيلا)' and 'the drought killed them (تمويتا).'

His saying: «وَقُلْنَ حاشَ لِلَّهِ» (and said, ‘Good Heavens!’) is a sanctification of Allah, the Glorified, in the matter of Yusuf. This is like His saying, the Exalted: «ما يَكُونُ لَنا أَنْ نَتَكَلَّمَ بِهذا سُبْحانَكَ هذا بُهْتانٌ عَظِيمٌ» (It is not for us to say such a thing! Immaculate are You! This is a monstrous calumny) [al-Nur: 16]. This is from the etiquette of speech among religious people: when speech occurs regarding a matter involving a type of sanctification and exoneration for someone, one begins by sanctifying Allah, the Glorified, then proceeds to sanctify the one intended. When they wanted to exonerate him (peace be upon him) by saying «ما هذا بَشَراً» etc., they began by sanctifying Him, the Exalted, then began exonerating him.

His saying: «ما هذا بَشَراً إِنْ هذا إِلَّا مَلَكٌ كَرِيمٌ» is a negation of Yusuf (peace be upon him) being a human and an affirmation that he is a noble angel. This is based on what religious people—including polytheists—believe: that angels are noble beings who are the principles of every good and happiness in the world; from them emanates all life, knowledge, beauty, splendor, joy, and all other things hoped for and desired. In them is every formal and spiritual beauty. When they are represented, they are imagined in a beauty that cannot be measured, and the idol-worshippers represent them in beautiful, splendid human forms.

Perhaps this is the reason for their saying: «إِنْ هذا إِلَّا مَلَكٌ كَرِيمٌ», where they did not describe him with what indicates beauty of face and handsomeness of appearance—even though what did to them what it did was the beauty of his face and the perfection of his form—but rather called him a 'noble angel' so that in it would be an indication of the beauty of his form and character together, and the beauty of his creation and conduct, his exterior and interior all together. Allah knows best.

The precedence of their saying this over the saying of the wife of the Aziz: «فَذلِكُنَّ الَّذِي لُمْتُنَّنِي فِيهِ» indicates that they did not intend by this speech an excuse for the wife of the Aziz in her love for him and her infatuation and passion for him. Rather, it was a necessity for them to praise him and a forced manifestation of the attraction of their souls and the infatuation of their hearts toward him; for in it was their own disgrace. The wife of the Aziz did not say: «فَذلِكُنَّ الَّذِي لُمْتُنَّنِي فِيهِ» except after she had disgraced them in deed and word by the cutting of hands and the sanctification of beauty; thus, nothing remained for them but to believe her in what she said and excuse her in what she did.

His saying, the Exalted: «قالَتْ فَذلِكُنَّ الَّذِي لُمْتُنَّنِي فِيهِ وَلَقَدْ راوَدْتُهُ عَنْ نَفْسِهِ فَاسْتَعْصَمَ» through the end of the verse. The speech is in the position of answering a hidden question, as if someone said: 'So what did the wife of the Aziz say to them?' It was said: «قالَتْ فَذلِكُنَّ الَّذِي لُمْتُنَّنِي فِيهِ».

She branched her speech off what preceded it of their words and deeds, and pointed to the person for whom they blamed her, describing him as 'he for whom you blamed me' so that he himself would be the answer to what they accused her of—abandoning the honor of her house, the dignity of her husband, and the chastity of herself in his love—and an excuse against their blame of her for soliciting him. The strongest explanation is to refer the listener to direct vision. From this category is His saying, the Exalted: «أَهذَا الَّذِي يَذْكُرُ آلِهَتَكُمْ» (Is this the one who mentions your gods?) [al-Anbiya: 36], and His saying: «رَبَّنا هؤُلاءِ أَضَلُّونا» (Our Lord, these are the ones who led us astray) [al-A‘raf: 38].

Then she admitted to the solicitation and mentioned to them that she had solicited him, but he took to chastity and sought protection (استعصم). She only spoke freely and revealed what she had been hiding when she saw the agreement of their hearts on infatuation with him. So she shared her complaint with them and warned Yusuf that she would not leave him, so he should prepare himself to obey her in what she commanded. This is the meaning of her saying: «وَلَقَدْ راوَدْتُهُ عَنْ نَفْسِهِ فَاسْتَعْصَمَ».

Then she mentioned to them what she had resolved upon of forcing him to comply and punishing him if he disagreed, saying: «وَلَئِنْ لَمْ يَفْعَلْ ما آمُرُهُ لَيُسْجَنَنَّ وَلَيَكُوناً مِنَ الصَّاغِرِينَ». She emphasized the speech with types of emphasis like the oath, the nun, and the lam to indicate that she had resolved upon that with a firm resolve; she had what would force him to what she wanted, and if he refused, he should prepare himself for prison after comfort, and humiliation and lowliness after honor and respect. In the speech, there is vanity and a type of pride and resistance toward them, and a type of warning and threat toward Yusuf (peace be upon him).

This threat contained in her saying: «وَلَئِنْ لَمْ يَفْعَلْ ما آمُرُهُ لَيُسْجَنَنَّ وَلَيَكُوناً مِنَ الصَّاغِرِينَ» is more intense and terrifying than what she asked of her husband on the day of seduction when she said: «ما جَزاءُ مَنْ أَرادَ بِأَهْلِكَ سُوءاً إِلَّا أَنْ يُسْجَنَ أَوْ عَذابٌ أَلِيمٌ».

Firstly: Because she alternated the punishment there between imprisonment and painful punishment, while she combined the two punishments here: imprisonment and being among the humiliated.

Secondly: Because here she carried out the threat herself rather than asking her husband. Her speech is the speech of one who does not hesitate in what she has resolved upon, nor turns back from what she has decided. She confirmed that she possessed her husband's heart and was able to turn him from what he wanted to what she wanted, and was capable of managing his affair however she wished.

His saying, the Exalted: «قالَ رَبِّ السِّجْنُ أَحَبُّ إِلَيَّ مِمَّا يَدْعُونَنِي إِلَيْهِ وَإِلَّا تَصْرِفْ عَنِّي كَيْدَهُنَّ أَصْبُ إِلَيْهِنَّ وَأَكُنْ مِنَ الْجاهِلِينَ». Raghib said in al-Mufradat: Saba (صبا) so-and-so, yasbu (يصبو), sabwan (صبوا) and sabwatan (صبوة) when he yearns and longs and acts like children (صبيان). He, the Exalted, said: «أَصْبُ إِلَيْهِنَّ وَأَكُنْ مِنَ الْجاهِلِينَ». End quote. In al-Majma‘: al-sabwah (الصبوة) is the delicacy of passion. End quote.

The wife of the Aziz and the women deliberated, speaking and conversing at length, and they proceeded to disclose what was in their hearts. Meanwhile, Yusuf (peace be upon him) stood before them as they invited him and sought to seduce him. However, Yusuf (peace be upon him) did not pay them any heed, nor did he speak a single word to them; rather, he turned back to his Lord—Who possessed his heart—with a heart that had no room for anyone but Him and no preoccupation except with Him. He said: قَالَ رَبِّ السِّجْنُ أَحَبُّ إِلَيَّ مِمَّا يَدْعُونَنِي إِلَيْهِ — He said, ‘My Lord! The prison is dearer to me than what they invite me to’ [12:33], etc.

This saying of his was not a supplication against himself for imprisonment, nor a request that Allah avert their invitation by casting him into prison. Rather, it was an exposition of his state (بيان حال) to his Lord: that due to divine upbringing (تربية إلهية), he preferred the agony of prison in the proximity of Allah over the pleasure of sin and distance from Him. This speech of his is similar to what he said to the wife of the Aziz when she was alone with him and sought to seduce him: مَعَاذَ اللَّهِ إِنَّهُ رَبِّي أَحْسَنَ مَثْوَايَ إِنَّهُ لَا يُفْلِحُ الظَّالِمُونَ — He said, ‘God forbid! Indeed He is my Lord; He has given me a good abode. Indeed the wrongdoers are not delivered’ [12:23]. In both instances, there is a refusal and a seeking of refuge in Allah. The only difference is that in one, he was addressing the wife of the Aziz, and in the other, he was addressing his Lord, the All-Mighty, the All-Powerful. Neither of these two statements was a supplication (دعاء) at all.

His saying: رَبِّ السِّجْنُ أَحَبُّ إِلَيَّ — ‘My Lord! The prison is dearer to me’ [12:33], etc., serves as a kind of preamble to his saying: وَإِلَّا تَصْرِفْ عَنِّي كَيْدَهُنَّ أَصْبُ إِلَيْهِنَّ — ‘and if You do not turn away their stratagems from me, I shall lean toward them’ [12:33], etc., which is a supplication in the form of an exposition of state.

The meaning of the verse is: "O Lord, if I were given the choice between prison and what they invite me to, I would choose prison over anything else. I ask You to turn away their stratagems from me, for if You do not, I will be overcome and lean toward them, and thus become one of the ignorant (الجاهلين). For I only guard myself against their evil through the knowledge (العلم) You have taught me, by which You turn their stratagems away from me. If You withhold its outpouring (إفاضته) upon me, I would become ignorant and fall into the peril of youthful passion (الصبوة) and desire (الهوى)."

From the verse, with the aid of the context, several points become clear:

First: That his saying: رَبِّ السِّجْنُ أَحَبُّ إِلَيَّ — ‘My Lord! The prison is dearer to me’ [12:33], was not a supplication by Yusuf (peace be upon him) against himself for imprisonment, but rather an exposition of his state to his Lord, expressing his turning away from them and returning to Him. The meaning of "dearer to me" (أَحَبُّ إِلَيَّ) is that he would choose it over what they invited him to if given the choice. It does not indicate that what they invited him to was beloved to him in any way, except to the extent that the human nature (الطبع الإنساني) and the soul that incites to evil (النفس الأمارة) might be inclined.

Furthermore, the saying of the Exalted: فَاسْتَجَابَ لَهُ رَبُّهُ — ‘So his Lord answered him’ [12:34], refers to the answering of the supplication contained within his saying: وَإِلَّا تَصْرِفْ عَنِّي كَيْدَهُنَّ — ‘and if You do not turn away their stratagems from me’ [12:33]. This is supported by the fact that it is followed by: فَصَرَفَ عَنْهُ كَيْدَهُنَّ — ‘and He turned away their stratagems from him’ [12:34]. It was not an answer to a supplication for prison against himself, as some have imagined.

Evidence for this is found in what follows regarding his entry into prison: ثُمَّ بَدَا لَهُمْ مِنْ بَعْدِ مَا رَأَوُا الْآيَاتِ لَيَسْجُنُنَّهُ حَتَّى حِينٍ — ‘Then, after they had seen the signs, it occurred to them to imprison him for a while’ [12:35]. Had it been a supplication for prison and an answer from Allah, the Exalted, decreeing prison for him, the use of the word "then" (ثُمَّ) and the separation of this meaning from what preceded it would not have been appropriate. So understand this.

Second: The women invited him and sought to seduce him just as the wife of the Aziz had invited him and sought to seduce him. As for whether they invited him to themselves, or to the wife of the Aziz, or both—inviting him to her in her presence and then each secretly inviting him to herself—the verse is silent on this, except for what can be inferred from his saying: وَإِلَّا تَصْرِفْ عَنِّي كَيْدَهُنَّ أَصْبُ إِلَيْهِنَّ — ‘and if You do not turn away their stratagems from me, I shall lean toward them’ [12:33]. For if there were no invitation from them to themselves, there would be no apparent meaning for "leaning toward them" (الصبوة إليهن).

What is suggested by the words of the Exalted, recounting Yusuf's words in prison to the king's messenger: ارْجِعْ إِلَى رَبِّكَ فَاسْأَلْهُ مَا بَالُ النِّسْوَةِ اللَّاتِي قَطَّعْنَ أَيْدِيَهُنَّ — ‘Go back to your master and ask him, ‘‘What was the case of the women who cut their hands?’’ [12:50]—until he said— قُلْنَ حَاشَ لِلَّهِ مَا عَلِمْنَا عَلَيْهِ مِنْ سُوءٍ قَالَتِ امْرَأَةُ الْعَزِيزِ الْآنَ حَصْحَصَ الْحَقُّ أَنَا رَاوَدْتُهُ عَنْ نَفْسِهِ وَإِنَّهُ لَمِنَ الصَّادِقِينَ ذَلِكَ لِيَعْلَمَ أَنِّي لَمْ أَخُنْهُ بِالْغَيْبِ وَأَنَّ اللَّهَ لَا يَهْدِي كَيْدَ الْخَائِنِينَ — ‘They said, ‘‘God forbid! We do not know of any evil in him.’’ The governor’s wife said, ‘‘Now the truth has come to light! It was I who solicited him, and he is indeed truthful. [I admit this] so that he may know that I did not betray him in [his] absence, and that Allah does not guide the stratagems of the treacherous’’ [12:51-52].

This suggests that they invited him to the wife of the Aziz, and he included them in the story. Then she said: لَمْ أَخُنْهُ بِالْغَيْبِ — ‘I did not betray him in [his] absence’ [12:52], and she did not say "I did not betray [anyone] in absence," nor did she say "I did not betray him or anyone else." So reflect on this.

Nevertheless, it is habitually impossible that they would see from him that which caused them to lose their senses, stunned their minds, and led them to cut their hands, and then simply slip away without approaching him at all. Rather, habit dictates that they did not leave the gathering except that they were infatuated with him, distraught, spending their days and nights with him as their sole concern and desire, willing to sacrifice themselves for him, enticing him with whatever adornment they possessed, offering themselves to him, and employing every means to attain what they wanted from him.

This is evident from what Allah recounted of Yusuf in his saying: رَبِّ السِّجْنُ أَحَبُّ إِلَيَّ مِمَّا يَدْعُونَنِي إِلَيْهِ وَإِلَّا تَصْرِفْ عَنِّي كَيْدَهُنَّ أَصْبُ إِلَيْهِنَّ — ‘My Lord! The prison is dearer to me than what they invite me to; and if You do not turn away their stratagems from me, I shall lean toward them’ [12:33]. He did not turn away from speaking to them to converse with his Lord—Who is aware of his state and hears his speech—except due to the severity of the matter upon him and the encompassing nature of the trial and calamity coming from their side.

Third: That sacred power (القوة القدسية) by which Yusuf (peace be upon him) sought protection was like a gradual matter poured upon him moment after moment from the side of Allah, the Glorified. It was not a sudden, finished matter; otherwise, the need for Him, the Exalted, would have ceased. Therefore, he expressed it by saying: وَإِلَّا تَصْرِفْ عَنِّي — ‘and if You do not turn away from me’ [12:33], and he did not say "and if You have not turned away from me." Although the conditional sentence is stripped of time, there are indications in the form (الهيئة).

For this reason also, the Exalted said: فَاسْتَجَابَ لَهُ رَبُّهُ فَصَرَفَ عَنْهُ كَيْدَهُنَّ — ‘So his Lord answered him and He turned away their stratagems from him’ [12:34], attributing the repelling of evil from him to a new response and turning away.

Fourth: This sacred power is of the nature of sciences and gnosis (العلوم والمعارف). Therefore, he (peace be upon him) said: وَأَكُنْ مِنَ الْجَاهِلِينَ — ‘and I shall be one of the ignorant’ [12:33], and he did not say "and I shall be one of the wrongdoers," as he said to the wife of the Aziz: إِنَّهُ لَا يُفْلِحُ الظَّالِمُونَ — ‘Indeed the wrongdoers are not delivered’ [12:23], or "one of the treacherous," as he said to the king: وَأَنَّ اللَّهَ لَا يَهْدِي كَيْدَ الْخائِنِينَ — ‘and that Allah does not guide the stratagems of the treacherous’ [12:52]. He differentiated in the mode of address between them and his Lord. He addressed them with the outward aspect of the matter, observing their level of understanding, saying that it is wrongdoing (ظلم) and the wrongdoer does not prosper, and that it is treachery (خيانة) and Allah does not guide the stratagem of the treacherous. But he addressed his Lord with the reality of the matter (حقيقة الأمر), which is that leaning toward them (الصبوة إليهن) stems from ignorance (الجهل).

The reality of the situation regarding these two matters (the third and fourth) will be presented to you in the discussions appended to the commentary, if Allah, the Exalted, wills.

His saying, the Exalted: فَاسْتَجَابَ لَهُ رَبُّهُ فَصَرَفَ عَنْهُ كَيْدَهُنَّ إِنَّهُ هُوَ السَّمِيعُ الْعَلِيمُ — So his Lord answered him and He turned away their stratagems from him. Indeed He is the All-hearing, the All-knowing [12:34]. That is, Allah answered his request to turn away their stratagems from him when he said: وَإِلَّا تَصْرِفْ عَنِّي كَيْدَهُنَّ أَصْبُ إِلَيْهِنَّ — ‘and if You do not turn away their stratagems from me, I shall lean toward them’ [12:33]. Indeed, He is the All-Hearing (السميع) of the words of His servants, the All-Knowing (العليم) of their states.

(Discussions on Religious Godwariness and its Degrees)

In Several Sections

1 - Law, Noble Character, and Monotheism: Law (القانون) does not achieve success except through faith (إيمان) preserved by noble character (الأخلاق الكريمة), and noble character is not complete except through monotheism (التوحيد) — the absolute oneness of Allah in His essence, attributes, and acts. Monotheism is the root upon which the tree of human happiness grows and branches out into noble character. These branches are what bear their good fruits in society. The Exalted said: أَلَمْ تَرَ كَيْفَ ضَرَبَ اللَّهُ مَثَلًا كَلِمَةً طَيِّبَةً كَشَجَرَةٍ طَيِّبَةٍ أَصْلُهَا ثَابِتٌ وَفَرْعُهَا فِي السَّمَاءِ تُؤْتِي أُكُلَهَا كُلَّ حِينٍ بِإِذْنِ رَبِّهَا وَيَضْرِبُ اللَّهُ الْأَمْثَالَ لِلنَّاسِ لَعَلَّهُمْ يَتَذَكَّرُونَ وَمَثَلُ كَلِمَةٍ خَبِيثَةٍ كَشَجَرَةٍ خَبِيثَةٍ اجْتُثَّتْ مِنْ فَوْقِ الْأَرْضِ مَا لَهَا مِنْ قَرَارٍ — Have you not regarded how Allah draws a parable? A good word is like a good tree: its roots are steady and its branches are in the sky. It gives its fruit every season by the leave of its Lord. Allah draws these parables for mankind so that they may take admonition. And the parable of a bad word is that of a bad tree: uprooted from the ground, it has no stability [14:24-26]. Thus, He made faith in Allah like a tree with a root, which is undoubtedly monotheism, and fruit that it gives every season by the leave of its Lord, which is righteous deeds (العمل الصالح), and a branch, which is noble character such as godwariness (التقوى), chastity (العفة), gnosis (المعرفة), courage (الشجاعة), justice (العدالة), mercy (الرحمة), and their likes.

The Exalted also said: إِلَيْهِ يَصْعَدُ الْكَلِمُ الطَّيِّبُ وَالْعَمَلُ الصَّالِحُ يَرْفَعُهُ — To Him ascends the good word, and He elevates righteous conduct [35:10]. He made the happiness of ascending to Allah—which is proximity (القرب) to Him, the Exalted—belong to the good word, which is true belief (الاعتقاد الحق), and He made the action that is suitable and appropriate for it that which elevates and assists it in its ascent.

To explain this: It is well known that man does not attain his specific perfection (كماله النوعي), nor is he happy in his life—and he has no greater aim than achieving happiness—except through a gathering of individuals who cooperate in the works of life, despite their multiplicity and diversity. A single human being is not capable of performing all of them.

This is what necessitated social man to establish traditions and laws (سنن وقوانين) by which he preserves the rights of individuals from loss and corruption, so that each of them may do what is within his power to do, and then they exchange their works. Thus, each attains from the prepared results what equals his work and his social weight, without the strong and capable being unjust, or the weak and helpless being wronged.

It is accepted that these traditions and laws do not remain effective except through other penal traditions and laws that threaten those who deviate from the traditions and laws and transgress against the rights of those entitled to them, frightening them with evil for evil. Others encourage and motivate them toward doing good deeds, and a governing power (القوة الحاكمة) ensures the implementation of all this, ruling over them and controlling them with justice and truth.

This aspiration is only realized if the power implementing the laws is aware of the crime and strong enough to deal with the criminal. However, if it is ignorant and the crime occurs out of its ignorance or negligence—and how often does that happen—there is no barrier to prevent its occurrence, for laws have no hands with which to strike. Likewise, if the government weakens due to the loss of necessary forces or negligence in policy and action, and the criminal prevails over it, or the criminal is stronger, the laws are lost, and deviations and transgressions against people's rights spread. Man—as has been mentioned repeatedly in previous discussions of this book—is exploitative by nature (مستخدم بالطبع), drawing benefit to himself even if it harms others.

This affliction intensifies if this power is concentrated in the executive force or those who hold the reins of all affairs, such that they weaken the people and strip them of the ability to return them to justice and straighten them with truth. Thus, they become a power and might (شوكة) whose strength cannot be resisted and whose will cannot be opposed.

Recorded histories are full of stories of tyrants and despots (الجبابرة والطواغيت) and their unjust rulings over people, and this is visible before our eyes in most regions of the earth.

Thus, traditions and laws, even if they are just within the limits of their concepts, and penal rulings, even if they are extreme in their severity, do not proceed smoothly in society nor close the door of disagreement and the path of deviation except through noble human character (أخلاق فاضلة إنسانية) that cuts off the roots of injustice and corruption, such as the trait of following the truth and respecting humanity, justice, dignity, life, and spreading mercy, and their likes.

Do not be deceived by the power and might you observe in advanced nations and the apparent order and justice among them, even though their laws were not established on ethical foundations where there is no guarantor for their implementation. For they are nations that think with a social mindset; an individual among them sees only the benefit and good of the nation and repels only what harms his nation. His nation's only concern is the enslavement of other weak nations, draining them, colonizing their lands, and violating their lives, honors, and wealth. This progress and advancement have only bequeathed them the transfer of what the tyrants of the past used to impose on individuals to societies. Thus, the nation today has taken the place of the individual yesterday. Words have abandoned their meanings for their opposites: they proclaim freedom, nobility, justice, and virtue, while intending nothing but slavery, baseness, injustice, and vice.

In short, traditions and laws are not safe from deviation and loss unless they are founded upon noble human character and are supported by it.

Furthermore, ethics do not suffice to bring happiness to society, nor do they lead man to the righteousness of action, unless they rely on monotheism (التوحيد). This is the faith that the world—including man—has one eternal God from Whose knowledge nothing is hidden, and Whose power no one can overcome. He created things according to the most perfect order, not out of any need for them, and He will return them to Him and hold them to account. He will reward the doer of good for his goodness and punish the doer of evil for his evil, and then they shall abide forever, either in bliss or in punishment.

It is well known that when ethics rely on this belief, man's only concern remains observing His pleasure, the Exalted, in his actions. Godwariness (التقوى) becomes an internal deterrent for him against committing a crime. If ethics did not nurse from the breast of this belief—the belief in monotheism—man would have no goal in his vital actions except to enjoy the fleeting goods of this world and to take pleasure in the delights of material life. The most he could do to regulate his livelihood and thereby preserve social vital laws is to think to himself that it is necessary for him to adhere to the prevailing laws to save society from disintegration and the social order from corruption. He would think it necessary to deprive himself of some of his desires to preserve society, thereby attaining the remaining portion, and that people might praise and commend him as long as he lives, or his name might be written in the pages of history in golden letters.

As for the praise and appreciation of people for an action, it only occurs in significant matters they are aware of. As for details and what they do not know, such as secret actions, there is no protection to guard them. As for the ongoing mention and the lofty name, it mostly influences matters involving sacrifice and self-offering, such as being killed for the sake of the homeland or spending wealth and time in elevating the structures of the state and the like. This is not for one who seeks it and believes in it, and then does not believe in what lies beyond the life of this world except as a superstitious belief. For there is no human being—according to this view—after death and passing, such that any benefit from praise or good mention would return to him. What rational person would buy the enjoyment of another by depriving himself without any returning benefit, or offer life to another by choosing death for himself, when he has nothing after death but annihilation? A superstitious belief vanishes with the slightest awakening and attention.

Thus, it has become clear that none of these things are capable of taking the place of monotheism, nor of succeeding it in deterring man from sin and the violation of traditions and laws, especially if the action is of a nature that does not appear to people, and especially if its nature is such that if it appeared, it would appear contrary to what it is due to reasons necessitating that—such as the chastity that is alleged to have been greed and transgression, as previously mentioned in the account of the wife of the Aziz soliciting Yusuf (peace be upon him). His situation revolved between betraying the Aziz regarding his wife and the wife accusing him before the Aziz of intending evil toward her. Nothing prevented him (peace be upon him)—nor was it fitting that anything should prevent him—except the knowledge of the station of his Lord.

2 - Religious godwariness is attained through one of three matters, or if you wish, say: He, the Glorified, is worshipped through one of three paths: fear (الخوف), hope (الرجاء), and love (الحب). The Exalted said: فِي الْآخِرَةِ عَذَابٌ شَدِيدٌ وَمَغْفِرَةٌ مِنَ اللَّهِ وَرِضْوَانٌ وَمَا الْحَيَاةُ الدُّنْيَا إِلَّا مَتَاعُ الْغُرُورِ — In the hereafter there is a severe punishment and Allah’s forgiveness and pleasure, and the life of this world is nothing but the wares of delusion [57:20]. The believer must be mindful of the reality of the world: that it is the wares of delusion, like a mirage in a plain which the thirsty man supposes to be water until, when he comes to it, he finds it to be nothing. He must not make it the goal of his actions in life, and he must know that he has a home beyond it, which is the Abode of the Hereafter. Therein he shall attain the goal of his actions: it contains a severe punishment for evil deeds, which he must fear and fear Allah regarding it; forgiveness from Allah for his righteous deeds, which he must hope for and hope in Allah regarding it; and pleasure (رضوان) from Allah, which he must prioritize over the pleasure of his own soul.

Human natures differ in preferring and choosing these three paths. For some—and they are the majority—fear (الخوف) dominates their souls. Whenever they reflect on what Allah has threatened the wrongdoers and those who commit sins and offenses with—the various types of punishment prepared for them—fear increases in their souls and their limbs tremble, and they are led thereby to worship Him, the Exalted, out of fear of His punishment.

For others, hope (الرجاء) dominates their souls. Whenever they reflect on what Allah has promised those who believe and do righteous deeds—of blessing, honor, and a good end—hope increases, and they go to great lengths in godwariness and adherence to righteous deeds out of desire for forgiveness and Paradise.

A third group—and they are those who have gnosis of Allah (العلماء بالله)—do not worship Allah out of fear of His punishment nor out of desire for His reward. Rather, they worship Him because He is worthy of worship. This is because they have known Him through what befits Him of the Most Beautiful Names (الأسماء الحسنى) and the Sublime Attributes (الصفات العليا). They have known that He is their Lord Who possesses them, their will, their pleasure, and everything other than them, and He alone disposes of the affair. They are nothing but servants of Allah, and it is only for the servant to worship his Lord and to prioritize His pleasure and will over his own pleasure and will. They worship Allah and do not desire in any of their actions—whether doing or refraining—anything but His Face. They do not pay attention therein to a punishment that frightens them, nor to a reward that gives them hope, even if they fear His punishment and hope for His mercy. To this, his (peace be upon him) saying points: "I did not worship You out of fear of Your Fire, nor out of desire for Your Paradise—rather, I found You worthy of worship, so I worshipped You."

These people, when they restricted their various desires to seeking the pleasure of their Lord and purified their actions in seeking a goal that is their Lord, divine love (المحبة الإلهية) appeared in their hearts. This is because they know their Lord through what He has made Himself known to them. He has named Himself with the most beautiful names and described His essence with every beautiful attribute. It is a characteristic of the human soul to be attracted to the beautiful, so how much more so to the Absolute Beautiful? The Exalted said: ذَلِكُمُ اللَّهُ رَبُّكُمْ لَا إِلَهَ إِلَّا هُوَ خَالِقُ كُلِّ شَيْءٍ فَاعْبُدُوهُ — That is Allah, your Lord, there is no god except Him, the creator of all things; so worship Him [6:102]. Then He said: الَّذِي أَحْسَنَ كُلَّ شَيْءٍ خَلَقَهُ — who perfected everything that He created [32:7]. Thus, He informed that creation revolves around beauty (الحسن) and that they are inseparable and synonymous. Then He, the Glorified, mentioned in many verses that whatever He created is a sign (آية) that points to Him, and that in the heavens and the earth are signs for those possessors of intellect. There is nothing in existence that does not point to Him, the Exalted, nor recount something of His beauty and majesty.

Things, from the perspective of their types of creation and their beauty, point to His beauty which has no end, and they praise and commend His beauty which does not perish. From the perspective of the types of deficiency and need within them, they point to His absolute self-sufficiency (غناه المطلق) and they glorify and hallow the courtyard of sanctity and greatness, as the Exalted said: وَإِنْ مِنْ شَيْءٍ إِلَّا يُسَبِّحُ بِحَمْدِهِ — There is not a thing but celebrates His praise [17:44].

These people proceed in the knowledge of things through the path their Lord has guided them to and made them known to them: that they are signs for Him and marks of His attributes of beauty and majesty. They have no selfhood, primacy, or independence except that they are like mirrors reflecting through their beauty what lies beyond them of infinite beauty, and through their poverty and need what encompasses them of absolute self-sufficiency, and through their lowliness and submissiveness what is above them of might and greatness. The observer of the universe with this vision does not wait long before his soul is attracted to the courtyard of might and majesty, and his heart is covered with divine love that makes him forget himself and everything else. It erases the marks of desires and psychological inclinations from his inner being and replaces his heart with a sound heart (قلب سليم) in which there is nothing but Allah, exalted is His Name. The Exalted said: وَالَّذِينَ آمَنُوا أَشَدُّ حُبًّا لِلَّهِ — but those who believe are more ardent in their love of Allah [2:165].

Therefore, the people of this path see that the other two paths—the path of worship out of fear and the path of worship out of desire—are not free from polytheism (شرك). For he who worships Him, the Exalted, out of fear of His punishment, uses Him, the Exalted, as a means to repel punishment from himself. Similarly, he who worships Him out of desire for His reward uses Him, the Exalted, as a means to attain blessing and honor. Had it been possible for him to reach what he seeks without worshipping Him, he would not have worshipped Him, nor would he have hovered around His gnosis. The narration from al-Sadiq (peace be upon him) has already been mentioned: "Is religion anything but love?" and his (peace be upon him) saying in a hadith: "And I worship Him out of love for Him, and this is a hidden station—none touch it except the purified (المطهرون)." The people of love were only purified because of their being cleansed of psychological desires and material impurities. Thus, sincerity (الإخلاص) in worship is not completed except through the path of love.

3 - How does love bequeath sincerity? Worshipping Him, the Exalted, out of fear of punishment, drives man toward refrainment (التروك), which is asceticism (الزهد) in the world for salvation in the Hereafter. The ascetic's role is to avoid prohibitions or what is in the sense of the prohibited, meaning the abandonment of obligations. Worshipping Him, the Exalted, out of desire for reward, drives toward actions, which is worship in the world through righteous deeds to attain the blessings of the Hereafter and Paradise. The worshipper's role is to adhere to obligations or what is in the sense of the obligatory, which is the abandonment of the prohibited. Both paths only call for sincerity to the religion, not to the Lord of the religion.

As for the love of Allah, the Glorified, it purifies the heart from attachment to other than Him, the Exalted—from the ornaments and beauty of the world, such as children, spouses, wealth, or status, even the self and its portions and hopes. It restricts the heart to attachment to Him, the Exalted, and to what is attributed to Him of religion, prophet, or wali, and all that returns to Him, the Exalted, in any way. For the love of a thing is love for its effects.

This person loves of actions what Allah loves and hates of them what Allah hates. He is pleased with the pleasure of Allah and for His pleasure, and he is angry with the anger of Allah and for His anger. This is the light that illuminates for him the path of action. The Exalted said: أَوَمَنْ كَانَ مَيْتًا فَأَحْيَيْنَاهُ وَجَعَلْنَا لَهُ نُورًا يَمْشِي بِهِ فِي النَّاسِ — Is he who was lifeless, then We gave him life and provided him with a light by which he walks among the people... [6:122]. And the spirit (الروح) that points him toward good things and righteous deeds. The Exalted said: وَأَيَّدَهُمْ بِرُوحٍ مِنْهُ — and He has strengthened them with a spirit from Him [58:22]. This is the secret as to why nothing issues from him except the beautiful and the good, and he avoids every disliked thing and evil.

As for the cosmic existents and the occurring events, his gaze does not fall upon any of them—significant or insignificant, much or little—except that he loves it and deems it beautiful. For he sees nothing in them except that they are pure signs (آيات محضة) manifesting to him what lies beyond them of absolute beauty and infinite goodness, free from every blemish and disliked thing.

Therefore, this person is delighted with the blessing of his Lord with a joy accompanied by no grief, and a pleasure and exhilaration accompanied by no pain or sadness, and a security accompanied by no fear. For these evil accidents only occur from a perception of evil and an expectation of harm and the disliked. He who sees nothing but the good and the beautiful, and finds nothing except what proceeds according to his will and pleasure, has no way for grief, sadness, fear, or anything that harms or hurts man to reach him. Rather, he attains such joy, exhilaration, and security that none can estimate or encompass except Allah, the Glorified. This is a matter that ordinary souls cannot conceptualize or grasp except through a kind of deficient conceptualization.

To this, the likes of the saying of the Exalted point: أَلَا إِنَّ أَوْلِيَاءَ اللَّهِ لَا خَوْفٌ عَلَيْهِمْ وَلَا هُمْ يَحْزَنُونَ الَّذِينَ آمَنُوا وَكَانُوا يَتَّقُونَ — Look! The friends of Allah will indeed have no fear nor will they grieve—those who have faith and are God-conscious [10:62-63]. And His saying: الَّذِينَ آمَنُوا وَلَمْ يَلْبِسُوا إِيمَانَهُمْ بِظُلْمٍ أُولَئِكَ لَهُمُ الْأَمْنُ وَهُمْ مُهْتَدُونَ — Those who have faith and do not taint their faith with wrongdoing—for such there shall be safety, and they are the [rightly] guided [6:82].

These are the near ones (المقربون) who have succeeded in His proximity, the Exalted, as nothing that falls upon the senses, or to which the imagination attaches, or which the soul desires, or which Satan cloaks, comes between them and their Lord. For everything that appears to them is nothing but a sign revealing the Transcendent Truth, not a covering veil. Thus, their Lord pours upon them the knowledge of certainty (علم اليقين), and reveals to them the realities with Him that are hidden from these blind material eyes, after He lifts the veil between Him and them, as indicated by the saying of the Exalted: كَلَّا إِنَّ كِتَابَ الْأَبْرَارِ لَفِي عِلِّيِّينَ وَمَا أَدْرَاكَ مَا عِلِّيُّونَ كِتَابٌ مَرْقُومٌ يَشْهَدُهُ الْمُقَرَّبُونَ — No indeed! The record of the pious is in ‘Illiyyun. And what will show you what is ‘Illiyyun? It is a written record, witnessed by those brought near [to Allah] [83:18-21]. And the saying of the Exalted: كَلَّا لَوْ تَعْلَمُونَ عِلْمَ الْيَقِينِ لَتَرَوُنَّ الْجَحِيمَ — No indeed! Were you to know with the knowledge of certainty, you would surely see hell [102:5-6]. A discussion on this meaning has already preceded in the commentary on the saying of the Exalted: يَا أَيُّهَا الَّذِينَ آمَنُوا عَلَيْكُمْ أَنْفُسَكُمْ — O you who have faith! Take care of your own souls [5:105] in the sixth volume of the book.

In short, these are in reality those who trust in Allah (المتوكلون على الله), who delegate to Him (المفوضون إليه), who are pleased with His decree (الراضون بقضائه), and who submit to His command (المسلمون لأمره). Since they see nothing but good and witness nothing but beauty, there settle in their souls noble traits and noble character that harmonize with this monotheism. Thus, they are sincere to Allah in their character just as they were sincere to Him in their actions. This is the meaning of the servant making his religion sincere to Allah. The Exalted said: هُوَ الْحَيُّ لَا إِلَهَ إِلَّا هُوَ فَادْعُوهُ مُخْلِصِينَ لَهُ الدِّينَ — He is the Living One, there is no god except Him. So supplicate Him, making your religion sincere to Him [40:65].

4 - As for His making His servant sincere to Him (إخلاصه تعالى عبده له), it is what the servant finds in himself of sincerity to Him, attributed to Him, the Exalted. For the servant possesses nothing of himself except through Allah, and Allah, the Glorified, is the owner of what He has made him own. Thus, his making his religion sincere—or if you wish, say: his making himself sincere to Allah—is His, the Exalted, making him sincere for Himself.

Yes, there is something here: Allah, the Glorified, created some of these servants of His upon the uprightness of the primordial nature (استقامة الفطرة) and the balance of creation. They grew up from the very beginning with brilliant minds, correct perceptions, pure souls, and sound hearts. Thus, they attained, by the mere purity of the primordial nature and the soundness of the soul, the blessing of sincerity that others attained through exertion and acquisition—rather, even higher and more sublime, due to the purity of their inner being from contamination by the impurities of obstacles and conflicts. It appears that these are the "made sincere" (المُخْلَصون) — those whom Allah has purified and chosen for Himself — in the terminology of the Quran.

These are the Prophets and the Imams. The Quran has explicitly stated that Allah chose them (اجتباهم), meaning He gathered them for Himself and made them sincere for His presence. The Exalted said: وَاجْتَبَيْنَاهُمْ وَهَدَيْنَاهُمْ إِلَى صِرَاطٍ مُسْتَقِيمٍ — and We chose them and guided them to a straight path [6:87]. And He said: هُوَ اجْتَبَاكُمْ وَمَا جَعَلَ عَلَيْكُمْ فِي الدِّينِ مِنْ حَرَجٍ — He has chosen you and has not placed for you any obstacle in religion [22:78].

Allah, the Glorified, gave them a knowledge that is a trait (ملكة) protecting them from committing sins and offenses. With it, the issuance of any of them—small or large—becomes impossible. By this, inerrancy (العصمة) — the divine protection from sin and error — is distinguished from justice (العدالة). Both prevent the issuance of sin, but with inerrancy, the issuance is impossible, unlike justice.

It was mentioned just now that a characteristic of these people is that they know of their Lord what others do not know. Allah, the Glorified, confirms this by His saying: سُبْحانَ اللَّهِ عَمَّا يَصِفُونَ إِلَّا عِبَادَ اللَّهِ الْمُخْلَصِينَ — Clear is Allah of what they describe, [all] except Allah’s chosen servants [37:160]. Divine love drives them to desire nothing but what Allah desires and to turn away from sins. Allah, the Glorified, affirms this through what He recounted from Iblis in more than one place in His speech, such as His saying: قَالَ فَبِعِزَّتِكَ لَأُغْوِيَنَّهُمْ أَجْمَعِينَ إِلَّا عِبَادَكَ مِنْهُمُ الْمُخْلَصِينَ — He said, ‘By Your might, I will surely pervert them all, except Your chosen servants among them’ [38:82-83].

Evidence that inerrancy is of the nature of knowledge is the saying of the Exalted, addressing His Prophet (صلى الله عليه وآله وسلم): وَلَوْلَا فَضْلُ اللَّهِ عَلَيْكَ وَرَحْمَتُهُ لَهَمَّتْ طَائِفَةٌ مِنْهُمْ أَنْ يُضِلُّوكَ وَمَا يُضِلُّونَ إِلَّا أَنْفُسَهُمْ وَمَا يَضُرُّونَكَ مِنْ شَيْءٍ وَأَنْزَلَ اللَّهُ عَلَيْكَ الْكِتَابَ وَالْحِكْمَةَ وَعَلَّمَكَ مَا لَمْ تَكُنْ تَعْلَمُ وَكَانَ فَضْلُ اللَّهِ عَلَيْكَ عَظِيمًا — Were it not for Allah’s grace and His mercy on you, a group of them were bent on leading you astray; but they do not mislead anyone except themselves, and they cannot do you any harm. Allah has sent down to you the Book and wisdom, and He has taught you what you did not know, and Allah’s grace on you has been great [4:113]. We have detailed the meaning of the verse in the commentary on Surah al-Nisa.

And the saying of the Exalted, recounting from Yusuf (peace be upon him): قَالَ رَبِّ السِّجْنُ أَحَبُّ إِلَيَّ مِمَّا يَدْعُونَنِي إِلَيْهِ وَإِلَّا تَصْرِفْ عَنِّي كَيْدَهُنَّ أَصْبُ إِلَيْهِنَّ وَأَكُنْ مِنَ الْجَاهِلِينَ — He said, ‘My Lord! The prison is dearer to me than what they invite me to; and if You do not turn away their stratagems from me, I shall lean toward them and become one of the ignorant’ [12:33]. We have clarified how the verse points to this.

From this, several points appear: First: This knowledge differs from other sciences in that its practical effect—which is turning man away from what is inappropriate toward what is appropriate—is definitive and never fails. This is unlike other sciences, where the turning away is frequent but not permanent. The Exalted said: وَجَحَدُوا بِهَا وَاسْتَيْقَنَتْهَا أَنْفُسُهُمْ — They impugned them—though they were convinced in their hearts [27:14]. And He said: أَفَرَأَيْتَ مَنِ اتَّخَذَ إِلَهَهُ هَوَاهُ وَأَضَلَّهُ اللَّهُ عَلَى عِلْمٍ — Have you seen him who has taken his desire to be his god and whom Allah has led astray knowingly [45:23]. And He said: فَمَا اخْتَلَفُوا إِلَّا مِنْ بَعْدِ مَا جَاءَهُمُ الْعِلْمُ بَغْيًا بَيْنَهُمْ — and they did not differ except after knowledge had come to them, out of envy among themselves [45:17].

This is also indicated by the saying of the Exalted: سُبْحَانَ اللَّهِ عَمَّا يَصِفُونَ إِلَّا عِبَادَ اللَّهِ الْمُخْلَصِينَ — Clear is Allah of what they describe, [all] except Allah’s chosen servants [37:160]. This is because these made sincere ones among the Prophets and Imams (peace be upon them) have explained to us the generalities of gnosis related to His names and attributes through hearing, and we have attained knowledge of it through demonstration (البرهان) as well. Yet the verse, despite this, declares Him, the Glorified, free from what we describe Him with, but not from what those made sincere ones describe Him with. Thus, it is only that the knowledge is different from the knowledge, even if the object of both knowledges is the same from one perspective.

Second: This knowledge—meaning the trait of inerrancy—does not change the human nature that is free in its volitional actions, nor does it move it into the realm of compulsion (الإجبار) and necessity (الاضطرار). How could it, when knowledge is one of the principles of choice? The mere strength of knowledge only necessitates the strength of will, like a seeker of safety who is certain that a certain liquid is a lethal poison at that moment; he will definitively refrain from drinking it by his own choice. The agent is only necessitated and compelled if someone who compels him moves one of the two sides of action or omission from possibility to impossibility.

Witness to this is His saying: وَاجْتَبَيْنَاهُمْ وَهَدَيْنَاهُمْ إِلَى صِرَاطٍ مُسْتَقِيمٍ ذَلِكَ هُدَى اللَّهِ يَهْدِي بِهِ مَنْ يَشَاءُ مِنْ عِبَادِهِ وَلَوْ أَشْرَكُوا لَحَبِطَ عَنْهُمْ مَا كَانُوا يَعْمَلُونَ — and We chose them and guided them to a straight path. That is Allah’s guidance: with it He guides whomever He wishes of His servants. But were they to ascribe any partners [to Allah], what they used to do would surely go for nothing [6:87-88]. The verse informs that it is within their possibility to ascribe partners to Allah, even if the divine choice and guidance prevent that. And His saying: يَا أَيُّهَا الرَّسُولُ بَلِّغْ مَا أُنْزِلَ إِلَيْكَ مِنْ رَبِّكَ وَإِنْ لَمْ تَفْعَلْ فَمَا بَلَّغْتَ رِسَالَتَهُ — O Apostle! Communicate that which has been sent down to you from your Lord, and if you do not, you will not have communicated His message [5:67], among other verses.

Thus, the inerrant human only turns away from sin by himself, by his choice, and by his will. The attribution of the turning away to His inerrancy, the Exalted, is like the attribution of the non-inerrant's turning away from sin to His success (توفيق), the Exalted.

This also does not contradict what His speech, the Exalted, points to and what the reports explicitly state: that this from the Prophets and Imams is through the strengthening of the Holy Spirit (روح القدس). For the attribution to the Holy Spirit is like the attribution of the believer's strengthening to the spirit of faith (روح الإيمان), and the attribution of misguidance and perversion to Satan and his embellishment. None of this removes the action from being an action issuing from its agent, based on his choice and will. So understand that.

Yes, there are people who claimed that Allah, the Glorified, only turns man away from sin not through his choice and will, but through the conflict and overcoming of causes—by creating a will or sending an angel who opposes the human will, preventing it from having an effect or changing its course and diverting it to something other than what man would naturally intend, just as a strong man prevents a weak one from the action he intends according to his nature.

Some of these, although they are from the determinists (المجبرة), the common principle upon which their view and its likes are built is that they see the need of things for the True Creator, the Glorified, is only in their coming into being (حدوث). As for their persistence (بقاء) after they have existed, they have no need for Him. Thus, He, the Glorified, is a cause alongside other causes, except that since He was more powerful and stronger than everything, He had the right to dispose of things during their persistence in whatever way He willed—of prevention or release, giving life or causing death, healing or sickening, expansion or restriction, and so on—through coercion.

So if Allah, the Glorified, willed to turn a servant away from evil, for example, He sent to him an angel who contested with him regarding the requirement of his nature and changed the course of his will, for example, from evil to good. Or if He willed to lead a servant astray because of his deserving it, He gave Iblis power over him, who turned him from good to evil, even if that was not to an extent that necessitated compulsion and necessity.

This is refuted by what we observe from ourselves in the works of good and evil with an eyewitness observation: that there is no other cause there that differs from us and contests with us, overcoming us, other than our own selves which perform their actions based on awareness of them and a will resulting from it, being sustained by them. What hearing and intellect establish beyond our souls of causes, such as the angel and Satan, is a longitudinal cause (سبب طولي), not a horizontal one (عرضي), and this is evident.

In addition, the Quranic gnosis of monotheism and what pertains to it refutes this saying from its root, and a large portion of that has already preceded in the folds of the previous discussions.

(Discussion of Narrations)

In al-Ma'ani, with its chain of transmission from Abu Hamzah al-Thumali, from Imam al-Sajjad (peace be upon him), in a hadith whose beginning preceded in the previous discussion of narrations:

He (peace be upon him) said: "Yusuf was among the most beautiful people of his time. When Yusuf reached puberty, the king's wife solicited him. He said: 'God forbid! We are a household who do not commit adultery.' She locked the doors upon herself and him and said: 'Do not fear,' and she threw herself upon him. He escaped from her, fleeing to the door, and opened it. She caught up with him and pulled his shirt from behind, pulling him out of it. Yusuf escaped from her in his clothes. They found her master at the door. She said: 'What is the recompense for him who intended evil toward your wife, except that he be imprisoned or a painful punishment?'"

He said: "The king intended to punish Yusuf. Yusuf said to him: 'I did not intend evil toward your wife; rather, she solicited me. Ask this infant which of us solicited the other.' He said: There was an infant with her from her family visiting her. Allah made the infant speak to deliver the decisive judgment. He said: 'O king, look at Yusuf's shirt. If it is torn from the front, then he is the one who solicited her. If it is torn from the back, then she is the one who solicited him.'"

"When the king heard the infant's speech and what he recounted, it terrified him greatly. The shirt was brought, and he looked at it. When he saw it torn from the back, he said to her: 'Indeed, it is of your stratagems; indeed, your stratagem is great.' And he said to Yusuf: 'Turn away from this, let no one hear it from you, and keep it secret.'"

He said: "Yusuf did not keep it secret and spread it in the city, until some women said: 'The wife of the Aziz solicits her servant.' This reached her, so she sent for them and prepared food and a gathering for them. Then she brought them citrons and gave each of them a knife. Then she said to Yusuf: 'Come out before them.' When they saw him, they extolled him and cut their hands, and they said what they said—meaning the women. She said to them: 'This is the one for whom you blamed me'—meaning in her love for him. The women left her presence, and each one of them secretly sent to Yusuf, apart from her companion, asking him for a visit. He refused them and said: 'If You do not turn away their stratagems from me, I shall lean toward them and become one of the ignorant.' And Allah turned away their stratagems from him."

"When the matter of Yusuf, the wife of the Aziz, and the women became widespread in Egypt, it occurred to the king, after hearing the infant's words, to imprison Yusuf. So he imprisoned him in the prison. Two youths entered the prison with Yusuf, and their story and Yusuf's story were as Allah recounted in the Book." Abu Hamzah said: "Then the hadith of Ali ibn al-Husayn (peace be upon him) ended."

I say: Al-Ayyashi narrated its meaning in his Tafsir from Abu Hamzah from him (peace be upon him) with a slight difference. His (peace be upon him) saying: "He said: 'God forbid! We are a household who do not commit adultery,'" is an interpretation by way of parallelism to his saying in the verse: إِنَّهُ رَبِّي أَحْسَنَ مَثْوَايَ — ‘Indeed He is my Lord; He has given me a good abode’ [12:23], etc. This supports what we previously presented in the explanation of the verse: that the pronoun refers to Allah, the Glorified, not to the Aziz of Egypt, as most commentators have held. So understand that.

His saying: "He refused them and said: إِلَّا تَصْرِفْ عَنِّي — ‘if You do not turn away’ [12:33]," etc., is evident in that he (peace be upon him) did not take his saying: رَبِّ السِّجْنُ أَحَبُّ إِلَيَّ مِمَّا يَدْعُونَنِي إِلَيْهِ — ‘My Lord! The prison is dearer to me than what they invite me to’ [12:33], as part of the supplication. This agrees with what we presented in the explanation of the verse: that it is not a supplication.

In al-Uyun, with its chain of transmission from Hamdan from Ali ibn Muhammad ibn al-Jahm, who said: "I attended the gathering of al-Ma'mun, and with him was al-Rida, Ali ibn Musa. Al-Ma'mun said to him: 'O son of the Messenger of Allah, is it not your view that the Prophets are inerrant?' He said: 'Yes.' And he mentioned the hadith until he said therein: 'Then tell me about the saying of Allah, the Exalted: وَلَقَدْ هَمَّتْ بِهِ وَهَمَّ بِهَا لَوْلَا أَنْ رَأَى بُرْهَانَ رَبِّهِ — She certainly made for him; and he would have made for her [too] had he not beheld the proof of his Lord [12:24].' Imam al-Rida (peace be upon him) said: 'She certainly made for him, and had he not beheld the proof of his Lord, he would have made for her. But he was inerrant, and the inerrant does not intend a sin nor does he commit it.'"

"My father narrated to me from his father al-Sadiq (peace be upon him) that he said: 'She intended to do, and he intended not to do.'" Al-Ma'mun said: "May Allah reward you, O Abu al-Hasan."

I say: It was previously mentioned that this Ibn al-Jahm is not free from criticism. However, the beginning of the hadith—meaning the answer of al-Rida (peace be upon him)—agrees with what we presented in the explanation of the verse. As for what he narrated from his grandfather al-Sadiq (peace be upon him), "She intended to do, and he intended not to do," perhaps what is meant by it is what al-Rida (peace be upon him) mentioned in the answer, as it is capable of being applied to it. Or perhaps what is meant by it is his intention to kill her, as supported by the following hadith, thus applying to some of the possibilities previously mentioned in the explanation of the verse.

In the same source, with its chain of transmission from Abu al-Salt al-Harawi, who said: "When al-Ma'mun gathered for Ali ibn Musa al-Rida (peace be upon him) the people of various views from the people of Islam, and from the religions of the Jews, Christians, Zoroastrians, Sabaeans, and all other people of views, no one stood up except that he established the proof against him, as if he had silenced him with a stone."

"Ali ibn Muhammad ibn al-Jahm stood up to him and said: 'O son of the Messenger of Allah, do you say the Prophets are inerrant?' He said: 'Yes.' He said to him: 'Then what do you say regarding the saying of Allah, the Mighty and Majestic, concerning Yusuf: وَلَقَدْ هَمَّتْ بِهِ وَهَمَّ بِهَا — She certainly made for him; and he would have made for her [too] [12:24]?' He said to him: 'As for the saying of the Exalted concerning Yusuf: وَلَقَدْ هَمَّتْ بِهِ وَهَمَّ بِهَا, she intended the sin, and Yusuf intended to kill her if she forced him, because of the greatness of what had come upon him. So Allah turned away from him her killing and the indecency. This is the saying of the Mighty and Majestic: كَذَلِكَ لِنَصْرِفَ عَنْهُ السُّوءَ وَالْفَحْشَاءَ — Thus it was, that We might turn away from him evil and indecency [12:24]. Evil (السوء) is killing, and indecency (الفحشاء) is adultery.'"

In al-Durr al-Manthur, Abu Nu'aym recorded in al-Hilyah from Ali ibn Abi Talib: regarding His saying: وَلَقَدْ هَمَّتْ بِهِ وَهَمَّ بِهَا, he said: "She desired him and he desired her. Part of the desire was that he intended to loosen the waistband. She went to an idol crowned with pearls and rubies in a corner of the house and covered it with a white cloth between her and it. He said: 'What are you doing?' She said: 'I am ashamed before my god that he should see me in this state.' Yusuf (peace be upon him) said: 'You are ashamed before an idol that does not eat or drink, and I should not be ashamed before my God Who is a watcher over every soul as to what it earns?' Then he said: 'You shall never attain it from me.' That was the proof he saw."

I say: This narration is among the fabrications. How could it be otherwise, when his speech and the speech of the other Imams of the Ahl al-Bayt (peace be upon them) are filled with the mention of the inerrancy of the Prophets, and their school of thought in that is well known?

Furthermore, her covering the idol and his transition from that to the proof he mentioned to her is not considered "beholding the proof." This meaning has been reported in several narrations from the paths of the Ahl al-Bayt (peace be upon them), but they are isolated reports (آحاد) that cannot be relied upon. Yes, it is not far-fetched that the woman might go to cover an idol that was there, and Yusuf's soul would then yearn to witness the sign of monotheism at that moment, and the veil between him and the courtyard of greatness would be lifted, so he would see what turned him away from every evil and indecency, as he had that before. The Exalted said regarding him: إِنَّهُ مِنْ عِبَادِنَا الْمُخْلَصِينَ — Indeed he was one of Our chosen servants [12:24]. If anything of these narrations is correct, let this be its meaning.

In the same source: Abu al-Shaykh recorded from Ibn Abbas, who said: "Yusuf (peace be upon him) stumbled three times: when he intended her, so he was imprisoned; when he said: اذْكُرْنِي عِنْدَ رَبِّكَ — ‘Mention me to your master’ [12:42], so he remained in prison for several years—for Satan caused him to forget the mention of his Lord; and when he said: إِنَّكُمْ لَسَارِقُونَ — ‘You are indeed thieves’ [12:70], they said: 'If he steals, a brother of his has stolen before.'"

I say: This narration contradicts the explicit speech of the Exalted, where He mentions that Allah chose him and made him sincere for Himself, and that Satan has no way to those whom Allah has made sincere for Himself. How can it be consistent for one who intended the most indecent sin, and Satan caused him to forget the mention of his Lord, and then he lied in his speech, so Allah punished him with prison and then with remaining in it for several years, and he was confronted with theft—that Allah should count him as a truthful one (صديق) among His made sincere and virtuous servants? He mentions that He gave him judgment and knowledge, chose him, and completed His blessing upon him. Along this path are many narrations recorded in al-Durr al-Manthur, some of which were previously cited in the explanation of the verses, and none of them can be relied upon.

In the same source: Ahmad, Ibn Jarir, and al-Bayhaqi in al-Dala'il recorded from Ibn Abbas from the Prophet (صلى الله عليه وآله وسلم), who said: "Four spoke while they were infants: the son of Pharaoh's daughter's hairdresser, the witness of Yusuf, the companion of Jurayj, and Jesus son of Mary."

In Tafsir al-Qummi, he said: In the narration of Abu al-Jarud from Abu Ja'far (peace be upon him): regarding His saying: قَدْ شَغَفَهَا حُبًّا — ‘he has smitten her to the heart with love’ [12:30], he says: "His love has veiled her from the people, so she understands nothing else. The veil is the shaghaf (الشغاف), and the shaghaf is the veil of the heart."

In the same source, in the hadith of her gathering the women and their cutting their hands, he said: "Yusuf (peace be upon him) did not reach the evening of that day until every woman who saw him sent to him, inviting him to herself. Yusuf became distressed on that day and said: رَبِّ السِّجْنُ أَحَبُّ إِلَيَّ مِمَّا يَدْعُونَنِي إِلَيْهِ وَإِلَّا تَصْرِفْ عَنِّي كَيْدَهُنَّ أَصْبُ إِلَيْهِنَّ وَأَكُنْ مِنَ الْجَاهِلِينَ فَاسْتَجَابَ لَهُ رَبُّهُ فَصَرَفَ عَنْهُ كَيْدَهُنَّ — ‘My Lord! The prison is dearer to me than what they invite me to; and if You do not turn away their stratagems from me, I shall lean toward them and become one of the ignorant.’ So his Lord answered him and He turned away their stratagems from him [12:33-34]." The hadith.

Ayahs

ثُمَّ بَدَا لَهُم مِّنۢ بَعْدِ مَا رَأَوُا۟ ٱلْـَٔايَـٰتِ لَيَسْجُنُنَّهُۥ حَتَّىٰ حِينٍ (٣٥)
Then it appeared to them, after they had seen all the signs [of his innocence], that they should confine him for some time. (35)
وَدَخَلَ مَعَهُ ٱلسِّجْنَ فَتَيَانِ ۖ قَالَ أَحَدُهُمَآ إِنِّىٓ أَرَىٰنِىٓ أَعْصِرُ خَمْرًا ۖ وَقَالَ ٱلْـَٔاخَرُ إِنِّىٓ أَرَىٰنِىٓ أَحْمِلُ فَوْقَ رَأْسِى خُبْزًا تَأْكُلُ ٱلطَّيْرُ مِنْهُ ۖ نَبِّئْنَا بِتَأْوِيلِهِۦٓ ۖ إِنَّا نَرَىٰكَ مِنَ ٱلْمُحْسِنِينَ (٣٦)
There entered the prison two youths along with him. One of them said, ‘I dreamt that I am pressing grapes.’ The other said, ‘I dreamt that I am carrying bread on my head from which the birds are eating.’ ‘Inform us of its interpretation,’ [they said], ‘for indeed we see you to be a virtuous man.’ (36)
قَالَ لَا يَأْتِيكُمَا طَعَامٌ تُرْزَقَانِهِۦٓ إِلَّا نَبَّأْتُكُمَا بِتَأْوِيلِهِۦ قَبْلَ أَن يَأْتِيَكُمَا ۚ ذَٰلِكُمَا مِمَّا عَلَّمَنِى رَبِّىٓ ۚ إِنِّى تَرَكْتُ مِلَّةَ قَوْمٍ لَّا يُؤْمِنُونَ بِٱللَّهِ وَهُم بِٱلْـَٔاخِرَةِ هُمْ كَـٰفِرُونَ (٣٧)
He said, ‘Before the meals you are served come to you I will inform you of its interpretation. That is among things my Lord has taught me. Indeed, I renounce the creed of the people who have no faith in Allah and who [also] disbelieve in the Hereafter. (37)
وَٱتَّبَعْتُ مِلَّةَ ءَابَآءِىٓ إِبْرَٰهِيمَ وَإِسْحَـٰقَ وَيَعْقُوبَ ۚ مَا كَانَ لَنَآ أَن نُّشْرِكَ بِٱللَّهِ مِن شَىْءٍ ۚ ذَٰلِكَ مِن فَضْلِ ٱللَّهِ عَلَيْنَا وَعَلَى ٱلنَّاسِ وَلَـٰكِنَّ أَكْثَرَ ٱلنَّاسِ لَا يَشْكُرُونَ (٣٨)
I follow the creed of my fathers, Abraham, Isaac and Jacob. It is not for us to ascribe any partner to Allah. That is by virtue of Allah’s grace upon us and upon all mankind, but most people do not give thanks. (38)
يَـٰصَـٰحِبَىِ ٱلسِّجْنِ ءَأَرْبَابٌ مُّتَفَرِّقُونَ خَيْرٌ أَمِ ٱللَّهُ ٱلْوَٰحِدُ ٱلْقَهَّارُ (٣٩)
O my prison mates! Are different masters better, or Allah, the One, the All-paramount? (39)
مَا تَعْبُدُونَ مِن دُونِهِۦٓ إِلَّآ أَسْمَآءً سَمَّيْتُمُوهَآ أَنتُمْ وَءَابَآؤُكُم مَّآ أَنزَلَ ٱللَّهُ بِهَا مِن سُلْطَـٰنٍ ۚ إِنِ ٱلْحُكْمُ إِلَّا لِلَّهِ ۚ أَمَرَ أَلَّا تَعْبُدُوٓا۟ إِلَّآ إِيَّاهُ ۚ ذَٰلِكَ ٱلدِّينُ ٱلْقَيِّمُ وَلَـٰكِنَّ أَكْثَرَ ٱلنَّاسِ لَا يَعْلَمُونَ (٤٠)
You do not worship besides Him but [mere] names that you and your fathers have coined, for which Allah has not sent down any authority. Sovereignty belongs only to Allah. He has commanded you to worship none except Him. That is the upright religion, but most people do not know. (40)
يَـٰصَـٰحِبَىِ ٱلسِّجْنِ أَمَّآ أَحَدُكُمَا فَيَسْقِى رَبَّهُۥ خَمْرًا ۖ وَأَمَّا ٱلْـَٔاخَرُ فَيُصْلَبُ فَتَأْكُلُ ٱلطَّيْرُ مِن رَّأْسِهِۦ ۚ قُضِىَ ٱلْأَمْرُ ٱلَّذِى فِيهِ تَسْتَفْتِيَانِ (٤١)
O my prison mates! As for one of you, he will serve wine to his master, and as for the other, he will be crucified and vultures will eat from his head. The matter about which you inquire has been decided.’ (41)
وَقَالَ لِلَّذِى ظَنَّ أَنَّهُۥ نَاجٍ مِّنْهُمَا ٱذْكُرْنِى عِندَ رَبِّكَ فَأَنسَىٰهُ ٱلشَّيْطَـٰنُ ذِكْرَ رَبِّهِۦ فَلَبِثَ فِى ٱلسِّجْنِ بِضْعَ سِنِينَ (٤٢)
Then he said to the one whom he knew would be delivered from among the two: ‘Mention me to your master.’ But Satan caused him to forget mentioning [it] to his master. So he remained in the prison for several years. (42)

(Commentary)

The verses encompass a portion of his story (peace be upon him), namely his entry into prison and his stay therein for several years. This served as a prelude to his complete proximity to the King and his attainment of the high status of Egypt. Within this period is his call in prison to the religion of monotheism (التوحيد) — the absolute oneness of Allah in His essence, attributes, and acts — which he presented with a wondrous exposition, revealing for the first time that he belonged to the family of Ibrahim, Ishaq, and Ya‘qub.

His saying, the Exalted: ثُمَّ بَدا لَهُمْ مِنْ بَعْدِ ما رَأَوُا الْآياتِ لَيَسْجُنُنَّهُ حَتَّى حِينٍ — Then it occurred to them, after they had seen the signs, to imprison him for a while. (12:35). The term "occurrence" (البداء) — the emergence of an opinion after it was not present — is used when one says, "It occurred to me regarding such a matter," meaning a new opinion appeared to me concerning it. The pronoun in His saying "to them" (لَهُمْ) refers to the Governor (العزيز), his wife, and those who followed them among the elite, the King's aides, and the nobility.

What is meant by "the signs" (الآيات) are the evidences and proofs indicating the innocence of Yusuf (peace be upon him) and the purity of his character from what they accused him of, such as the testimony of the infant, the tearing of the shirt from behind, and their racing to the door together. Perhaps among these signs was also the women cutting their hands upon seeing him, his steadfastness (استعصامه) — his seeking divine protection and remaining chaste — against their solicitation of him, and the confession of the Governor's wife to them that she had indeed solicited him but he remained chaste.

His saying "to imprison him" (لَيَسْجُنُنَّهُ) contains the letter lam for an oath, meaning they swore and resolved that they would certainly imprison him. This is an explanation of the opinion that occurred to them. Connected to it is His saying "for a while" (حَتَّى حِينٍ). This phrase carries a sense of waiting, considering that "while" (حين) is cut off from its genitive construction (الإضافة). The meaning based on this is: they would imprison him until the widespread talk of the solicitation in the city ceased and people forgot it.

The meaning of the verse is: Then a new opinion appeared to the Governor and those following him—his wife and the rest of his advisors—regarding Yusuf, after they had seen these signs indicating his innocence and impeccability (العصمة). This opinion was to imprison him for a period of time until the talk of the solicitation, which brought them shame and disgrace (الشين), was forgotten; and they swore an oath upon that.
